although students demonstrated engagement and acquired foundational concepts during the lectures the minute timeframe constrained the depth of technical instruction consequently a dedicated tutoring period was implemented to provide individualized support facilitating project progress and reinforcing key lessons to further mitigate time constraints scaffolding was provided for various project components while students were tasked with designing their projects and coding the initial segments of each assignment during class sessions the one hour duration proved insufficient for both mastering new topics and completing assignments with student consent preparatory work included the completion of remaining assignment components and the integration of base code to prepare for subsequent lessons this approach ensured students were prepared to advance to new material this strategy of providing individualized assistance to maintain curriculum momentum was adopted following initial sessions and consultation with the students regular instructor the instructor emphasized that given the limited time preventing students from becoming stalled by tedious tasks was critical for maintaining engagement and encouragement while it is acknowledged that repetitive tasks can be pedagogically valuable this approach was deemed the optimal balance between engagement and learning efficacy a conclusion supported by post survey student feedback the successful implementation of this course necessitated several specific logistical considerations which are outlined below for future program developers significant lead time was required prior to commencement due to the sensitive nature of the population specifically volunteers underwent background clearance and training on facility regulations to access the juvenile hall additionally a mechanism was established to award high school credits accommodating the diverse academic requirements of the student body while some students could apply the course toward general elective credits others required it to fulfill specific math or art electives this variability in academic needs underscores the value of integrating multiple subject areas within a computer science curriculum

li et al b investigate word alignment within neural machine translation nmt however their methodology differs from the two step masking strategy proposed herein specifically they substitute the target token solely with a zero embedding or a randomly selected vocabulary item discussion and conclusion a primary concern raised by reviewers pertains to the modest performance of the proposed probes the induced syntactic trees exhibit only marginal improvements over simple baselines e g right branching structures and in the context of discourse parsing perform inferiorly nevertheless these results do not imply that supervised probing is invalid nor that bert captures less syntactic information than previously hypothesized indeed there is no theoretical guarantee that an unsupervised probe will yield a strong correlation with human defined syntax as the latter is not introduced as a supervisory signal rather the proposed method reveals the natural syntax inherent in bert which is acquired through self supervised learning on raw text consequently this work complements existing supervised probing research in two distinct ways first it establishes a lower bound for the unsupervised syntactic parsing capabilities of bert enhancing this lower bound may facilitate the discovery of more precise information to corroborate findings from supervised probes second as demonstrated in section the syntax learned by bert proves empirically beneficial when integrated into downstream applications despite not being identical to human designed schemas in summary we introduce a parameter free probing technique designed to augment the current body of work on interpreting bert through a carefully designed two stage perturbation process we derive impact matrices from bert these matrices mirror the function of attention mechanisms in capturing inter word correlations however unlike attention they emerge from the model s final output rather than intermediate representations we further develop algorithms to extract syntactic trees from these matrices our findings reinforce the conclusions of hewitt and manning liu et al jawahar et al and tenney et al a b who demonstrated that bert encodes rich syntactic properties additionally we extend our methodology to probe document structure thereby illuminating bert s efficacy in modeling long sequences finally we observe that incorporating empirically induced dependency structures into a downstream system zhang et al yields further accuracy improvements these gains are comparable to and in some cases superior to those achieved using human designed dependency schemas offering valuable insights into the success of bert in downstream tasks

we look at the state graphs and sequences of three players seen in in the glyph visualization we can easily see that player a had a clear linear progression from start to finish by taking a closer look at their sequences we can see that though they started with two syntax errors they only ever made small changes to their code and that they progressed from two errors to one error to errorfree code we can hypothesize that this player had a highly efficient debugging strategy that focused on small localized changes targeting a single error at a time as discussed previously such a strategy was common among higher performing debuggers another observation about player a is that they never submitted code with no changes a behavior that was performed by most players in the dataset this may be the result of the player being at a high level of understanding in terms of how their code affected the environment interacted with the puzzle the simplicity of the puzzle in the given level or a combination of the two a very different story is told by the graph of player b unlike player a player b visits a larger number of error states their graph contains frequent looping between and within error states and there is less of a sequential linear path from the beginning to the solution this indicates that player b struggled to debug their code frequently resubmitting code with the same error or introducing new errors while trying to correct older ones looking at player b ’s sequence we can see that they frequently made medium sized code changes and had very few small changes indicating that their code edits were less targeted perhaps because they were less certain of where the error was than player a however they never make large code changes and do progress into a brief series of smaller code changes towards the end of their sequence additionally while they do resubmit code without changes they do not do so an exhaustive number of times these resubmits likely represent a strategy of observation in which the code is run multiple times to confirm the output is consistent

the hierarchical attention network han leverages the intrinsic hierarchical structure of textual data—spanning documents sentences and words—by integrating an attention mechanism designed to identify salient words and sentences within their broader context this architecture employs a dual level attention strategy word level attention isolates significant terms within individual sentences while sentence level attention determines the relative importance of sentences within a document the comprehensive architecture of the han model is illustrated in figure in this study the han model is trained on a contextualized corpus utilizing generated pseudolabels the resulting predictions subsequently inform the processes of seed expansion and disambiguation seed expansion and disambiguation seed expansion given a set of contextualized documents and their associated predicted class labels we propose a methodology to rank contextualized terms and incorporate the highest ranking candidates into the seed word sets the efficacy of this process relies fundamentally on a specific ranking function an ideal seed word s for a given label l is characterized as a distinctive term that appears with high frequency exclusively within documents belonging to class l consequently for a specific class c j and a candidate word w the ranking score is evaluated based on three primary criteria label indicative since pseudolabel generation is predicated on the presence of seed words an optimal seed word should strongly indicate class membership ideally the posterior probability of a document belonging to class c j given the presence of word w denoted as p c j w should approach unity therefore we define the label indicative measure as li c j w p c j w frac f c j w f c j where f c j represents the total count of documents predicted as class c j and f c j w denotes the subset of those documents containing word w all frequency counts are derived from the prediction results applied to the input unlabeled documents frequency an ideal seed word s for label l must exhibit significant frequency within the documents assigned to that label to quantify this we first calculate the average frequency of the seed word s across all documents belonging to label l

the integration of domestic medical robotics and wearable technologies is poised to revolutionize clinical research by automating data collection thereby significantly reducing costs and accelerating the drug discovery process traditional clinical trials often incur expenses in the hundreds of millions of dollars due to complex legal and organizational frameworks by leveraging home based robotic systems to record patient activity the clinical trial model can be decentralized reducing reliance on large pharmaceutical entities lowering operational expenditures and enhancing data objectivity furthermore autonomous drones equipped with pharmaceuticals and defibrillators will be deployed to provide immediate emergency response based on alerts from wearable monitoring systems in the domestic sphere these robotic systems will serve multifaceted roles including continuous family health monitoring treatment assistance medication adherence tracking physical therapy instruction and predictive disease analysis additionally by providing essential companionship to the elderly these technologies are expected to contribute to increased life expectancy parallel advancements are anticipated in the integration of monitoring systems directly into human physiology through nanomedicine the human immune system inherently manages critical data regarding inflammation sites and viral neutralization capabilities however this information remains largely inaccessible to conscious human control while current vaccination methods allow for limited training of the immune response the ability to directly read and upload information to the immune system would enable the treatment of a broad spectrum of conditions including autoimmune disorders infections organ failure neoplasms and tissue senescence projections suggest that such bidirectional communication capabilities may emerge within the s functioning analogously to modern computerized automotive diagnostics this proposed communication channel between an organism s immune system and external computing devices is termed a humoral interface sharing conceptual similarities with neurointerfaces realization of this technology may rely on advanced nanotechnology or biotechnology such as computer programmed cells the subsequent evolution of this field involves the development of artificial human immune system management potentially comprising biological organisms genetically enhanced endogenous cells or micro robots circulating within the bloodstream

abstract while existing encoder decoder architectures gadetsky et al ishiwatari et al washio et al produce satisfactory generation results they rely heavily on the decoder to infer comprehensive semantic components this dependency often results in definitions that lack specificity due to the omission of critical semantic elements as illustrated in table generating a precise definition for the term captain requires the integration of distinct semantic components the entity type person the associated domain ship and the functional relationship manages in the absence of explicit modeling for these components standard models frequently fail to capture the functional aspect manages linguistic theory posits that word definitions are constructed by decomposing meaning into constituent semantic components and expressing them in natural language wierzbicka drawing inspiration from this approach yang et al incorporated sememes—the minimal units of semantic meaning bloomfield dong and dong —into chinese definition generation however the manual annotation of such semantic components is as resource intensive if not more so than the manual composition of definitions to address these limitations this paper proposes a framework that explicitly decomposes word meanings into semantic components for definition generation we introduce a set of discrete latent variables to model these underlying semantic structures building upon established training techniques for discrete latent variables in representation learning roy et al and machine translation van den oord et al kaiser et al shu et al we further propose two auxiliary loss functions to ensure that the latent variables effectively capture word semantics experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves significant improvements over prior approaches on two definition generation datasets furthermore analysis confirms that the model learns meaningful latent representations resulting in the generation of more precise and specific definitions background this section outlines the foundational context of the definition modeling task and reviews two prominent works that have advanced the field definition modeling the task of definition generation was initially introduced by noraset et al

this task aims to produce captions in german and french utilizing a limited training corpus containing images and captions in english german and french derived from the flickrk dataset in this context we utilize the results reported by caglayan et al to quantitatively benchmark our proposed approach a pertinent line of inquiry is presented by jaffe which examines various lstm based encoder decoder architectures for multilingual caption generation the model most analogous to our work is the dual attention model which first generates an english caption and subsequently employs an lstm with attention mechanisms over both the image and the generated english text to produce a german caption however quantitative evaluations in that study did not demonstrate significant advantages for this cascaded approach while our work shares conceptual similarities it diverges in key technical aspects specifically the plugs approach employs an end to end model based on a transformer decoder vaswani et al this architecture leverages the generated english prefix via the self attention mechanism to predict the non english target caption conditioning the output on english tokens across multiple layers of the decoder stack furthermore this study addresses the challenge of web scale multilingual image captioning by utilizing the large scale conceptual captions dataset for training we also investigate the implications of employing captions across multiple languages and the efficacy of multilingual single model configurations model architecture we model the output caption using a sequence generation approach grounded in transformer networks vaswani et al where the output consists of a sequence of subtokens comprising the target caption as illustrated in figure the input sequence is constructed by concatenating specific feature representations global image embedding we utilize a global image representation derived from the graphrise model juan et al this model is based on a resnet architecture he et al trained for image classification at ultra fine granularity levels it produces a compact image embedding denoted as i with a dimensionality of d i

execution dynamics the system facilitates the instruction of various coding errors the primary objective is to enhance student comprehension regarding coding practices that result in the overwriting of memory with unintended values the specific locations where such overwrites occur and the consequent effects on program execution specifically the visualization system elucidates the placement of variables within the process address space the influence of calling conventions and compiler operations on stack variable ordering and the security implications of variable placement regarding potential buffer overflow exploits furthermore it demonstrates defensive mechanisms such as stack canaries and identifies coding patterns that permit unauthorized memory modification the system is engineered to accommodate learners across a spectrum of proficiency levels address space data is presented through four user selectable detail modes novice intermediate advanced and expert the applicable the command line invocation used to launch the program is also provided access to the program address space is facilitated via the dedicated program address space tab additional visualizations are available through the call graph file operations and sensitive data tabs each corresponding to its respective function the source code tab is addressed subsequently as illustrated in our study on the impact of training set size we find in experiments across a range of tasks that the performance of the noncontextual embeddings glove random improves rapidly as we increase the amount of training data often attaining within to accuracy of bert embeddings when the full training set is used this suggests that for many tasks these embeddings could likely match bert given sufficient data which is precisely what we observe in our experiments with industryscale data given the computational overhead of contextual embeddings this exposes important tradeoffs between the computational resources required by the embeddings the expense of labeling training data and the accuracy of the downstream model to better understand when contextual embeddings give large boosts in performance we identify three linguistic properties of nlp tasks which help explain when these embeddings will provide gains • complexity of sentence structure how interdependent are different words in a sentence this aligns with recent observations from experiments with classic word embeddings at apple these tasks are proprietary so we share these results anecdotally as motivation for our study • ambiguity in word usage are words likely to appear with multiple labels during training • prevalence of unseen words how likely is encountering a word never seen during training intuitively these properties distinguish between nlp tasks involving simple and formulaic text e g assistant commands vs more unstructured and lexically diverse text e g literary novels we show on both sentiment analysis and ner tasks that contextual embeddings perform significantly better on more complex ambiguous and unseen language according to proxies for these properties thus contextual embeddings are likely to give large gains in performance on tasks with a high prevalence of this type of language background we discuss the different types of word embeddings we compare in our study contextual pretrained embeddings noncontextual pretrained embeddings and random embeddings we also discuss the relative efficiency of these embedding methods both in terms of computation time and memory sec pretrained contextual embeddings recent contextual word embeddings such as bert and xlnet consist of multiple layers of transformers which use selfattention

introduction to computer science is designed with a forward looking perspective to equip students with the foundational knowledge necessary for advanced study and professional advancement in fields such as machine learning cybersecurity and cloud computing developed through extensive consultation with educators and industry professionals across diverse institutions this text addresses the varied needs of both computer science majors and non majors the curriculum is organized into five comprehensive sections each dedicated to a critical domain of the discipline foundations of computer science this section establishes the core principles of computational thinking problem solving methodologies and algorithm design it covers data structures the formal properties of algorithms and algorithmic paradigms through practical examples and exercises students acquire the skills required to construct analyze and apply algorithms across various domains hardware and software realization here students examine the implementation of algorithms within hardware and software environments the progression moves from low level programming languages to hardware design and computer systems organization key topics include models of computation machine level representation processor architectures and memory hierarchy fostering a deep understanding of the relationship between abstract algorithms and their physical execution software development and data management this section addresses the principles of software engineering high level programming languages and data management students gain hands on experience with both relational and non relational database systems while learning to design robust software solutions the emphasis is on managing complex data structures to prepare students for future roles in software development and engineering web and cloud native applications in this module students apply their accumulated knowledge to design and build modern web and cloud native applications the content includes contemporary web architectures responsive design techniques and cloud based solutions utilizing platform as a service paas and function as a service faas technologies furthermore students explore the development of hybrid multi cloud digital solutions gaining experience in addressing complex business and social challenges through modern computing technologies ethics and societal implications the final section investigates the ethical and societal dimensions of computing topics encompass cybersecurity the governance of cyber resources and responsible computing practices students are guided to navigate the complexities of security and governance while critically evaluating the broader impact of technology on society each core concept is structured to build sequentially upon the previous one ensuring a coherent learning trajectory that provides students with a comprehensive and clear understanding of the field

subsequently the coherence score is derived by calculating the mean cosine similarity between vectors representing adjacent utterances within this framework utterance vectors are constructed exclusively from content words with all stop words removed the aseq model gandhe and traum relies solely on transitions between dialogue acts das and remains agnostic to semantic relationships such as entity transitions between utterances its coherence features consist of n gram probabilities across the sequence of das associated with the dialogue utterances these features are subsequently input into a support vector machine svm to rank dialogues the eagrid model cervone et al identified as the highest performing approach in the cited study leverages both entity and da transitions it represents semantic relationships across utterances via a grid structure where rows correspond to utterances and columns represent entities with a single column dedicated to das entities are defined as mentions extracted by a coreference resolution system the intersection of an entity column and an utterance row indicates the grammatical role of that entity within the utterance while the intersection of the da column and an utterance row denotes the utterance s da label cervone et al utilize transitions in both entity grammatical roles and da labels across utterances as indicative patterns of coherence the frequencies of these patterns serve as coherence features which are fed into svms to discriminate dialogues based on their coherence levels sdicoh refers to the proposed dicoh model trained exclusively using the supervision signal for coherence ranking with a total loss function defined as l l phi coh see equation notably this model does not incorporate da information to enrich utterance vectors mdicoh represents the full proposed model trained via the suggested multi task learning mtl framework using supervision signals for both coherence ranking and dialogue act prediction dap the primary advantage of this architecture is its ability to learn to focus on salient utterance information for coherence assessment conditioned on the provided das in alignment with prior coherence research barzilay and lapata guinaudeau and strube mesgar and strube cervone et al accuracy is employed as the evaluation metric

problem solving activities to ensure consistency and clarify expectations an exemplar journal was provided illustrating how to address all target behaviors with the requisite level of detail recognizing the personal nature of programming related emotions struggles and successes participants were assured of the anonymity of their entries consequently no rigid structural requirements or mandatory content specifications were enforced this approach facilitated authentic reflection allowing students to prioritize content they deemed valuable and useful to further scaffold the reflective challenges students encountered when reflecting on their programming processes rq a survey was administered concurrently with the submission of each assignment and corresponding journal this total of distinct statements reflecting on the students programming experiences

however morphotactic constraints restrict the ways in which affixes can be attached to stems and other affixes e g the suffix less can be combined with ness atom less ness but not with ity atom less ity the semantic and formal complexity of derivation makes predicting the mwf of derivatives more challenging than the mwf of inflectional forms here we model the mwf of derivatives as the likelihood of their existence in the mental lexicon derivatives in the mental lexicon how likely a derivative is to exist is influenced by various factors in this study we concentrate on the role of the structure of the mental lexicon the mental lexicon can be thought of as a set of associations between meaning m and form f i e words organized in a network where links correspond to shared semantic and phonological properties see pierrehumbert for a review since we base our study on textual data we will treat the form of words orthographically rather than phonologically we will refer to the type of information conveyed by the cognitive structure of the mental lexicon as associative information sets of words with similar semantic and formal properties form clusters in the mental lexicon the semantic and formal properties reinforced by such clusters create abstractions that can be extended to new words if the abstraction hinges upon a shared derivational pattern the effect of such an extension is a new derivative the extent to which a word conforms to the properties of the cluster influences how likely the abstraction in our case a derivational pattern is to be extended to that word this is what is captured by the notion of mwf derivational graphs the main goal of this paper is to predict the mwf of morphological derivatives i e how likely is a word to be formed as an extension of a lexical cluster by directly leveraging associative information

to tackle the above challenges in this paper we propose a novel method to model both crossmodality contrast and semantic association by constructing the decomposition and relation network i e d r net for multimodal sarcasm detection task the decomposition network implicitly models crossmodality contrast information via representing the commonality and discrepancy between image and text in tweets the relation network explicitly captures the semantic association between image and text via a crossmodality attention mechanism the main contributions of our work are as follows • we identify the essential research issue in multimodal sarcasm detection and propose a method to model crossmodality contrast in the associated context of multimodal sarcastic tweets • we construct the decomposition and relation network d r net to implicitly represent the contextual contrast and explicitly capture the semantic association between image and text which provides the reasoning ability and wordlevel interpretability for multimodal sarcasm detection • we compare our model with the existing stateoftheart methods and experimental results on a publicly available dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our model in multimodal sarcasm detection related work textual sarcasm detection traditional sarcasm detection takes textbased approaches including feature engineering context based and neural network models earlier feature engineering approaches are based on the insight that sarcasm usually occurs with specific signals such as syntactic patterns e g using highfrequency words and content words lexical indicators e g interjections and intensifiers or special symbols e g ’ ’ ’ ’ hashtags and emojis as sarcasm is often associated with an implicit contrast or disparity between conveyed sentiment and user’s situation in context some studies rely on this basic character of sarcasm to detect contextual contrast at different linguistic levels including immediate communicative context between speaker and audience historical context between current and past tweets or wordlevel context by computing semantic similarity recently researchers utilize the powerful techniques of neural networks to get more precise semantic representations of sarcastic text and model the sequential information of sarcastic context

multimodal interactions despite the challenges noted both the speech s and multimodal mm approaches garnered substantial positive feedback regarding insertion and replacement tasks participants observed that the utility of these modalities increased proportionally with the volume of text to be added the primary advantage of the mm approach as highlighted by participants was the reduction in verbal input required however this benefit was accompanied by the cognitive load of performing dual tasks simultaneously mouse and keyboard the mouse and keyboard modality achieved the highest performance scores for insertion and replacement tasks demonstrating superior speed in these operations furthermore it received favorable ratings for deletion and reordering tasks where it exhibited high efficiency though it was not the fastest option for reordering nevertheless some participants offered critical commentary noting that the modality s effectiveness is largely contingent upon extensive prior expertise general observations notably the study s findings diverge from the intuitions of translators reported in a previous elicitation study herbig et al a specifically touch based interaction performed significantly better than anticipated whereas handwriting of entire subphrases proved less effective than expected it is also significant that several newly introduced modalities were able to compete with the traditional mouse and keyboard despite participants inherent bias stemming from years of training with the latter overall participants provided highly positive feedback regarding this initial prototype which integrates pen touch speech and multimodal combinations for post editing machine translation pe mt this reception encourages further development additionally several promising suggestions for enhancing and expanding the prototype were proposed the primary objective of this study was to conduct a detailed exploration of the implemented interactions evaluating each modality for every operation type irrespective of frequency the selected methodology ensured the collection of comparable feedback from professional translators by requiring them to correct identical errors using different modalities future research should prioritize a follow up study utilizing a more realistic natural workflow such a study would determine whether participants dynamically switch modalities within sentences based on error types or prefer to maintain a single modality to minimize the cognitive cost of frequent switching conclusion as an increasing number of professional translators adopt post editing pe to enhance productivity and reduce errors current computer assisted translation cat interfaces remain heavily reliant on traditional mouse and keyboard input this persistence occurs despite literature suggesting that alternative modalities could effectively support pe operations consequently this paper presents mmpe a cat prototype that integrates pen touch speech and multimodal interactions alongside conventional mouse and keyboard inputs the study explores the efficacy of these diverse modalities when utilized by professional translators

in maxmargin incremental ccg parsing miloˇs stanojevi´c school of informatics university of edinburgh mark steedman school of informatics university of edinburgh incremental syntactic parsing has been an active research area both for cognitive scientists trying to model human sentence processing and for nlp researchers attempting to combine incremental parsing with language modelling for asr and mt most effort has been directed at designing the right transition mechanism but less has been done to answer the question of what a probabilistic model for those transition parsers should look like a very incremental transition mechanism of a recently proposed ccg parser when trained in straightforward locally normalised discriminative fashion produces very bad results on english ccgbank we identify three biases as the causes of this problem label bias exposure bias and imbalanced probabilities bias while known techniques for tackling these biases improve results they still do not make the parser state of the art instead we tackle all of these three biases at the same time using an improved version of beam search optimisation that minimises all beam search violations instead of minimising only the biggest violation the new incremental parser gives better results than all previously published incremental ccg parsers and outperforms even some widely used nonincremental ccg parsers introduction it has been known for a long time that human sentence processing is highly incremental with early formation of semantic representations a parser that is able to form representation early must have some notion of partial structure such as “s missing an object np” also such parser needs to be able to combine partial structures into bigger partial structures these two properties are at the core of combinatory categorial grammar ccg ccg represents partial constituents using complex categories for example s np is the category of a transitive sentential prefix such as i like or i think i like requiring an object np on its right such prefix categories are constructed using combinatory rules such as function composition in this way we can form mostly leftbranching derivation trees that can be parsed incrementally even with simple transition mechanisms such as shiftreduce parsers still left branching structures are not sufficient to solve all the problems of incremental sentence processing right adjuncts are particularly problematic

engineers with an ecological focus observed that the traditional model of industrial activityin which individual manufacturing processes take in raw materials and generate products to be sold plus waste to be disposed ofshould be transformed to a more integrated modelan industrial ecosystem in such a system the consumption of energy and materials is optimized waste generation is minimized and effluents of one processwhether they are spent catalysts from petroleum refining fly and bottom ash from electric power generation or discarded plastic engineering traditionally concerns itself with safety and efficiency ecological design asks that we do more we have already made dramatic progress in many areas by substituting design intelligence for the extravagant use of energy and materials computing power that years ago would fill a house full of vacuum tubes and wires can now be held in the palm of your hand the old steel mills whose blast furnaces slag heaps and towering smokestacks dominated the industrial landscape have been replaced with efficient scaleddown facilities and processes many products and processes have been miniaturized dramatically reducing the flow of energy and materials required to fabricate and operate them for instance there is manufacturing using molecular nanotechnology if we think about products at their most fundamental level as being made from atoms then the properties of these products depend on how the atoms are arranged if atoms of coal are rearranged diamonds may be created similarly by rearranging the atoms of sand with a few trace elements added computer chips are made today s manufacturing methods are very crnde when seen from a molecular viewcasting grinding milling and even lithography move atoms in huge statistical groups molecular nanotechnology or manufacturing allows positioning of every atom in its correct place yielding reduced costs in terms of materials and energy of course society plays a pivotal role in creating the climate for change making it economically feasible to use intelligence of ecological design for instance in germany manufacturers are now required by law to either take back and recycle old packaging or pay a steep tax this has transformed the german packaging industry questions now include how can durability and reuse be designed into the packaging how can easy disassembly of packaging components facilitate recycling these questions have triggered extraordinary innovations in reusable or recyclable packaging with corresponding environmental benefits including decreased waste and use of virgin materials

the proposed methodology constructs a feature vector comprising n dimensions where n represents the total number of concepts in the vocabulary specifically in this study each dimension corresponds to the similarity score between a target concept and every other concept within the set this approach is grounded in the geometric intuition that if two data points exhibit similar distances to a common set of reference points in euclidean space they are likely to be proximal to one another to operationalize these similarity based features for classification thirteen distinct measures were employed ten derived from lexical resources and three based on co occurrence statistics the lexical measures include one senticnet based metric and nine wordnet based metrics the co occurrence measures consist of text distance pointwise mutual information and emotional affinity specifically the senticnet similarity is defined as the inverse of the distance where distance is calculated as the absolute difference between the polarity scores of two concepts as all concepts utilized in this study are sourced from senticnet valid polarity scores are available for the entire set for wordnet based calculations the open source wordnet similarity package utilizing english wordnet was employed this package provides nine distinct similarity measures derived from the analysis of the wordnet hierarchy glosses and associated metadata all nine were utilized as independent information sources a challenge arose due to the incomplete overlap between senticnet and wordnet of the concepts identified in the isear database were directly mapped to wordnet the remaining concepts underwent manual review and rephrasing to facilitate mapping for instance multi word expressions were reduced to their primary lexical head e g make mistake was reduced to mistake through this process an additional concepts were successfully mapped resulting in a total of concepts with valid wordnet similarity scores for the remaining concepts that could not be mapped to wordnet similarity scores were assigned random values within the interval this stochastic assignment was chosen over a fixed value such as zero to prevent unmapped concepts from being artificially clustered together or uniformly distanced from the rest of the concept space thereby preserving the structural integrity of the feature representation

one employs traditional machine learning and the other deep learning zhang et al for example proposed a unified framework for csc consisting of a pipeline of error detection candidate generation and final candidate selection using traditional machine learning wang et al proposed a seqseq model with copy mechanism which transforms an input sentence into a new sentence with spelling errors corrected more recently bert the language representation model is successfully applied to many language understanding tasks including csc in the stateoftheart method using bert a characterlevel bert is first pretrained using a large unlabelled dataset and then finetuned using a labeled dataset the labeled data can be obtained via data augmentation in which examples of spelling errors are generated using a large confusion table finally the model is utilized to predict the most likely character from a list of candidates at each position of the given sentence the method is powerful because bert has certain ability to acquire knowledge for language understanding our experimental results show that the accuracy of the method can be further improved however one observation is that the error detection capability of the model is not sufficiently high and once an error is detected the model has a better chance to make a right correction we hypothesize that this might be due to the way of pretraining bert with mask language modeling in which only about of the characters in the text are masked and thus it only learns the distribution of masked tokens and tends to choose not to make any correction this phenomenon is prevalent and represents a fundamental challenge for using bert in certain tasks like spelling error correction to address the above issue we propose a novel neural architecture in this work referred to as softmasked bert softmasked bert contains two networks a detection network and a correction network based on bert the correction network is similar to that in the method of solely using bert the detection network is a bigru network that predicts the probability that the character is an error at each position the probability is then utilized to conduct softmasking of embedding of character at the position

to do this we now consider the different biases we wish to mitigate and construct a specific unlikelihood loss for each in turn repetition and copying generative dialogue models are known to both i rely too much on copying existing context knowledge or dialogue history and ii repeat themselves within individual utterances to address this with unlikelihood we define two types of negative candidate tokens which either appear in a repeating ngram from the context or from the generated label itself c contextcopy t y t y t ∈ repeat context ngram ∅ otherwise c labelrepeat t y t y t ∈ repeating label ngram ∅ otherwise where y t is a token in a repeating context ngram when y t is part of an ngram that already appeared in the context tokens x and is in a repeating label ngram when y t is part of an ngram that already appeared in y t given a groundtruth context x i we apply these two forms of unlikelihood to a modelgenerated sequence ˆ y i in summary we either apply the perexample loss l i ul p θ c contextcopy y x i ˆ y i for controlling context copies or l i ul p θ c labelrepeat y x i ˆ y i for controlling label repeats we also consider mixing the two losses to mitigate both issues vocabulary usage neural sequence models trained with maximum likelihood generate sequences with token distributions that differ from those of human text in particular these models tend to produce high frequency tokens too often and low frequency tokens too rarely where frequency is defined by the human token distribution we address this with unlikelihood by penalizing tokens according to the mismatch between the model and groundtruth unigram distributions

consequently a primary objective of computer science cs education is to ensure that graduates possess clarity regarding their professional trajectory are adequately prepared for their chosen path and are equipped to secure their desired positions as noted by faculty the overarching goal is to prepare students for success in any subsequent pursuit whether that entails entering the industry founding a startup or pursuing graduate studies a critical component of this preparation involves the understanding of the role of computing in society faculty members emphasize the necessity for students to contextualize their technical work within a broader societal framework specifically students must comprehend the potential societal impacts of their contributions and the associated ethical implications the following analysis categorizes faculty perspectives on the role of undergraduate cs education in preparing students for industry based on responses to inquiries specifically regarding industry bound students foundational competencies success in the industry is predicated upon a core set of fundamental skills faculty identified two distinct categories within this domain vocational skills these include fluency in software engineering principles proficiency with industry standard tools and the ability to collaborate and communicate effectively core cs skills a paramount example cited is the capacity to rapidly assimilate new technologies or algorithms this adaptability is viewed as a direct result of a robust foundational understanding of computer science concepts programmatic responsibilities cs programs bear specific obligations in facilitating student readiness for the workforce faculty assert that curricula must raise awareness educate students regarding diverse industry career pathways and the requisite knowledge for each provide practical experience offer opportunities for experiential learning such as internships cooperative education programs or research collaborations with faculty integrate substantial projects ensure the inclusion of at least one significant project that exposes students to large scale codebases and collaborative development environments maintain relevance continuously update the curriculum to align with current industry standards incorporating feedback from returning interns and alumni facilitate employment readiness acknowledge the realities of the job market by ensuring students are prepared to successfully navigate technical interviews and secure employment

appendixes at the end when there are only a few figures or tables they may be included within the report text this is comparatively easy to accomplish with word processing software to cut and paste graphs tables and drawings if this is the situation the figure should be located as near to its mention in text as possible also refer to the figure or table in the text and label as such you will notice that in the design of this text the text figures have numbers and labels and are located near to where they are mentioned in the the actual computer program and analysis sample calculations are placed in an appendix this section demonstrates your technical prowess allowing the reader to assess the validity of your assumptions the results and discussion of them are usually combined except in large reports there will be results from your analysis more important than the results is your interpretationare they reasonable in light of your analysis error analysis is included here as there are errors associated with any measurement with many computer programs and with statistical sampling of data no assessment no value judgment has been made regarding the results the next section deals with this critical issue in the conclusion section you apply your engineering knowhow to explain why the results are as they are combining your theoretical knowledge with the actual facts to reach meaningful judgments in this section you also summarize the key points and key facts that have been discussed leaving the reader with the perspective you desire it is also possible to generalize in both flat text classification and hierarchical text classification htc have historically been addressed using traditional machine learning classifiers liu et al kim et al or deep neural networks peng et al conneau et al however contemporary approaches predominantly rely on parameter heavy models that necessitate extensive training durations while certain studies in the domain of flat classification have mitigated efficiency concerns through external optimization strategies rather than architectural modifications joulin et al howard and ruder such methodologies remain underdeveloped for htc consequently the most recent and effective htc methods continue to incur significant computational costs yang et al banerjee et al this context motivates the central research question of this study how can htc performance be enhanced while reducing computational overhead accordingly the primary focus and contributions of this work are as follows development of a lightweight htc model a robust architecture adhering to the sequence to sequence paradigm characterized by a minimal parameter count and reduced training time integration of an auxiliary task the practical application of a computationally efficient auxiliary task designed to augment predictive capacity within a bottom up framework leveraging external class definitions an investigation into strategies that utilize textual definitions of classes these definitions are encoded within the word vector space and integrated into the model via incorporation at each prediction step and an adapted beam search mechanism efficient strategies for hierarchical text classification sequence to sequence approach hierarchical classification can be conceptualized as a multilabel classification task wherein labels exhibit hierarchical dependencies specifically labels at lower levels are conditioned upon those at higher levels to address this the proposed methodology diverges from prior work by framing the task as a sequence to sequence problem in this formulation an encoder processes the input textual description while a decoder generates class labels sequentially progressing from the highest to the lowest level of the hierarchy the baseline model employed is a sequence to sequence neural network sutskever et al comprising the following components embedding layer this layer transforms each input word into a vector representation w i where i in dots n and n denotes the total number of tokens in the input document

numerous natural language processing nlp applications including information extraction question answering summarization and dialogue systems have traditionally confined their scope to objective informational content this approach prioritizes relatively uncontroversial facts that can be inferred from an utterance stored within a database and subjected to logical reasoning however equating such objective data with the full semantic meaning of an utterance is a simplification a substantial body of literature in linguistics and psycholinguistics posits that an utterance conveys significantly more than a mere set of facts it carries a complex array of implications derived from the speaker s specific linguistic choices including considerations of perspective emphasis and framing consequently linguistic choices do not merely transmit objective facts they subtly modulate meaning reflecting how speakers conceptualize events and influencing listener interpretation in predictable ways this phenomenon is illustrated by metaphorical descriptions such as the following portrayal of a newborn nora s arrival brought a regime change life under her adorable tyranny was filled with squawking swaddling and ceaseless sleep input output cycles we were relieved when she relaxed her tiny iron grip while this passage reports factual elements regarding new parenthood and daily caregiving routines its primary emphasis lies in the parents subjective experience of being suppressed and controlled the infant is framed variously as a tyrant regime a bird squawking and a relentless machine sleep input output cycles iron grip thereby shaping the reader s understanding of the situation through specific metaphorical lenses the capacity of linguistic choices to shape comprehension is further evident in more conventional examples a chuck bought a car from jerry jerry sold a car to chuck jerry paid chuck for the car b i work at microsoft i work for microsoft c the statue stands in the plaza the statue is standing in the plaza in each set the sentences describe the same underlying scenario yet differ significantly in their pragmatic and semantic implications in example a the choice between buy sell and pay directs attention to different participants and sub events within a commercial transaction example b demonstrates a subtler distinction in emphasis the preposition at highlights the physical location of employment whereas for evokes the beneficiary relationship with the employer finally example c illustrates how grammatical marking can alter event connotations specifically through the contrast between a stative description and a temporary progressive state

building on the nrc model of a stem learning ecosystem the brightcs theoretical framework outlines a cs learning ecosystem specifically for middle school girls of color as shown in developing an ecosystem focused on the intersectionality of race and gender in adolescence adolescence is a time of many dynamic changes not only do students experience physical social and cognitive changes but their schooling experience changes as coursework becomes more rigorous teachers’ expectations change class schedules become more intense and expected parent roles change from more to less involved rather than nurturing interest in a specific subject or topic area research has shown the importance of nurturing “possible selves” and growth mindset to persistence and academic success within this context of adolescent development the social emotional development and needs of black girls and other girls of color are also important to address for girls of color in addition to their identity formation at large their development includes making sense of their racial and gender experiences such as stereotype threat imposter syndrome and explicit and implicit biases —especially when there are few of them in advanced and gifted classes at school brightcs starts by forming a cohort of girls of color from the same middle school grades the girls come together for a weekly month afterschool program at their school a twoweek summer experience at a local university or nonprofit organization and ongoing mentoring from women and persons of color who are local community leaders computer scientists and entrepreneurs as an ecosystem the program provides girls and their families with deep learning and engagement through partnerships between schools local universities and community organizations the program curriculum which focuses on solving problems with technology also empowers girls with valuable leadership skills the goal of the program is not only to build computer science interest knowledge and skills but to also promote and support social emotional development in so doing this program seeks to reinforce positive prosocial behaviors including academic performance college career readiness and taking rigorous coursework in high school including cs courses see outcomes in however it is not enough simply to create a cs program for black girls and other girls of color which can have very limited sustained impacts with distinct fadeout effects

among existing pooling methods average pooling is a representative one which takes the average of the l norm of input features however average pooling equally regards the input representation vector at each position and ignores their different informativeness for learning text representation which may not be optimal thus other pooling methods such as max pooling and attentive pooling are widely used in neural nlp models for example kim proposed to apply max pooling to the contextual word representations learned by cnn networks to build the representations of the entire sentence yang et al proposed to use attentive pooling at both word and sentence levels to learn informative sentence and document representations by selecting important words and sentences however these pooling methods use fixed average norms i e l norm for average and attentive pooling and l ∞ norm for max pooling to build text representations which may not be optimal when handling different tasks our work is motivated by the following observations first different contexts usually have different informativeness for learning text representations for example in fig the word “but” is very important for inferring the sentiment polarity of this sentence while “the” is uninformative thus modeling the different informativeness of contexts and attending to them differently may help learn more informative text representations second different tasks and even different datasets have different characteristics for example in fig sentiment and negation words may be the key clues for inferring the sentiment polarity of the first sentence while the global contexts may be useful for understanding the topic of the second sentence thus using a fixed pooling norm for universal text representation learning is probably not optimal third in popular pooling methods such as max pooling and attentive pooling some contexts may be overemphasized and other useful contextual information is not fullyrespected

rich analytics and the required technical support and professional development can enhance teaching and learning practices providing support for ethical and lowcost educational research can play a challenges associated with computer science i cs curricula is that of student satisfaction which directly translates to success and retention this challenge is exacerbated at small institutions in assist the students this approach has found success at both large and small institutions where student demographics and populations are widely varied in most implementations the peer mentor is a cs course may count as an elective course necessary for graduation or to fulfill another majors requirements in this situation nonmajor students are often cited as feeling excluded which then becomes departmentallevel tutoring in order to better assist all student backgrounds

this actually creates a discrepancy between training and test at training time each training instance contributes equally to the objective function whereas at test time f gives equal weight to positive and negative examples the overwhelming effect of easynegative examples as pointed out by meng et al a significantly large number of negative examples also means that the number of easynegative example is large the huge number of easy examples tends to overwhelm the training making the model not sufficiently learn to distinguish between positive examples and hardnegative examples the crossentropy objective ce for short or maximum likelihood mle objective which is widely adopted as the training objective for dataimbalanced nlp tasks handles neither of the issues to handle the first issue we propose to replace ce or mle with losses based on the sørensen–dice coefficient or tversky index the sørensen–dice coefficient dice loss for short is the harmonic mean of precision and recall it attaches equal importance to false positives fps and false negatives fns and is thus more immune to dataimbalanced datasets tversky index extends dice loss by using a weight that trades precision and recall which can be thought as the approximation of the f β score and thus comes with more flexibility therefore we use dice loss or tversky index to replace ce loss to address the first issue only using dice loss or tversky index is not enough since they are unable to address the dominating influence of easynegative examples this is intrinsically because dice loss is actually a soft version of the f score taking the binary classification task as an example at test time an example will be classified as negative as long as its probability is smaller than but training will push the value to as much as possible this gap isn’t a big issue for balanced datasets but is extremely detrimental if a big proportion of training examples are easynegative ones easynegative examples can easily dominate training since their probabilities can be pushed to fairly easily

hereafter referred to as o init and o freeze respectively vary contingent upon the presence of the gold synset within the training corpus when weights are initialized and subsequently updated the columns in o corresponding to unattested synsets receive exclusively negative signals from the cross entropy loss function conversely synsets present in the training data can be further refined ngrams emoticons and partofspeech features some of these features include multiword expressions which suffer from the data sparsity problem due to this such features are not effective and contain a large amount of noise syntactic ngrams have performed better than traditional linear ngrams because they are more informative and less arbitrary these features are also more accurate in comparison with information gain chisquare test and ngrams the approach proposed by singh et al obtained good accuracy though it requires extensive calculation of many pmi values which is computationally sentiment analysis approaches have different advantages and disadvantages we gave an overview of stateoftheart multilingual sentiment analysis methods we described data preprocessing typical features and the main resources used for multilingual sentiment analysis then we discussed different approaches applied by their authors to english and other languages we have classified these approaches into corpusbased lexiconbased and hybrid ones the real value of a technique for the research community corresponds to the results that can be reproduced with it not in the results its original authors reportedly obtained with it to evaluate this real value we have implemented eleven approaches as closely as we could basing on their descriptions in the original papers and tested them on the same two corpora in the majority of the cases we obtained lower results than those reported by their corresponding authors we attribute this mainly to the incompleteness of their descriptions in the original papers in some cases though the methods were developed for a specific domain so in such cases comparison on our test corpora may not be fair a lesson learnt was that for a method to be useful for the research community authors should provide sufficient detail to allow its correct implementation by the reader according to our results the approach proposed by singh et al outperforms other approaches

methodology overview the proposed approach initiates by enumerating the complete set of candidate spans denoted as sp s s dots s k derived from the input sentence each span represents a contiguous subsequence of the input text constrained by a maximum length l s based on this set of candidates the model addresses two primary objectives the classification of term types t for all spans in sp to facilitate aspect term at and opinion term ot recognition and the determination of pairwise relations r for all span pairs in sp times sp to identify valid at ot associations these subtasks are formally defined as follows term recognition this task involves assigning a unique label t in a o text null to each candidate span s c here a indicates that s c constitutes an aspect term s c in at o indicates that s c constitutes an opinion term s c in ot and text null signifies that the span belongs to neither category pairwise relation identification this task entails assigning a binary label r in text true text false to each ordered span pair s c s c the relation is defined as a directed association originating from an aspect term and terminating at an opinion term consequently within this formulation s c functions as the candidate at and s c as the candidate ot a label of text true denotes a valid association between the two spans framework architecture the overall architecture of the proposed span based multitask framework referred to as spanmlt is illustrated in figure the process begins with a base encoder that processes the input sentence to generate contextualized word representations subsequently a span generator enumerates all feasible spans deriving their representations from the hidden states of the base encoder within the multitask learning paradigm these span representations are shared across two distinct output modules term scorer assigns the most probable term label to each individual span relation scorer evaluates the correspondence between every pair of spans and assigns a binary label to each pair span generation given an input sentence represented as a sequence of tokens w w dots w n a span s i is defined as a single word or phrase comprising the sequence w text start i dots w text end i this span is characterized by a specific starting index text start i and an ending index text end i

the word embedding association test weat quantifies bias within word embeddings by evaluating the relative similarity between two sets of target words and two sets of attribute words for instance to assess social bias regarding gender in the context of professions one might designate programmer engineer and scientist as one target set and nurse teacher and librarian as the other while using man male and woman female as the respective attribute sets in an unbiased representation the relative similarity between the target sets and the attribute sets should be negligible weat quantifies this relationship as an effect size where an absolute value approaching zero indicates a lower degree of bias to extend this evaluation to sentence level representations we employ the methodology outlined by may et al which adapts weat into the sentence encoder association test seat in this framework individual words are converted into sentences via a template based approach allowing the weat metric to be applied to fixed length pre trained sentence embeddings for assessments involving multiple classes we utilize the mean average cosine mac similarity metric which generalizes seat to a multiclass setting manzini et al regarding specific test configurations the binary gender evaluation utilizes word lists from the caliskan tests caliskan et al which examine stereotypes linking gendered names to careers mathematics and science greenwald et al for the multiclass religion evaluation we adapt the caliskan tests employed by may et al by incorporating the lexicons defined by manzini et al debiasing setup this section details the application of the sent debias framework to two prominent sentence encoders bert devlin et al and elmo peters et al it is important to note that the pre trained bert encoder requires fine tuning on task specific data consequently the final bert encoder utilized for debiasing varies depending on the specific task to account for these variations we report two distinct sets of metrics bert involves debiasing the pre trained bert encoder directly bert post task involves first fine tuning and post processing e g normalization the bert encoder on a specific task prior to applying the debiasing procedure to the resulting representations

semantic role labeling and graph construction the objective of semantic role labeling srl is to delineate the semantic relationships between a predicate and its associated arguments and modifiers effectively capturing the who what and to whom dynamics within a sentence márquez et al for each sentence predicate argument tuples are extracted utilizing state of the art srl toolkits specifically the bert based model implemented within the allennlp framework shi and lin each extracted tuple is instantiated as a subgraph wherein individual elements—such as arguments locations and temporal markers—serve as nodes inter tuple edges are subsequently established between nodes across different tuples to denote inclusive relationships or potential coreference to the same entity dependency based semantic graphs alternatively a dependency based dp semantic graph is constructed by employing a biaffine attention model to generate a dependency parse tree for each sentence dozat and manning this tree undergoes refinement through the removal of non essential constituents e g punctuation and the merging of consecutive nodes that constitute a unified semantic unit to form a cohesive semantic graph inter tree edges are added between semantically similar nodes across different parse trees as illustrated in figure dp based graphs typically model more granular and sparse semantic relations compared to srl based counterparts a distinction further elaborated in appendix a a comparative performance analysis of these two formalisms is provided in section semantic enriched document representations to generate semantic enriched document representations for question generation the input document d and the constructed semantic graph g are encoded independently and subsequently fused the document is processed using a recurrent neural network rnn based passage encoder while the graph is processed via a novel attention based graph gated neural network attggnn document encoding given an input document d a bidirectional gated recurrent unit gru cho et al is utilized to encode contextual information the resulting hidden states are represented as x d where each x i denotes the context embedding of word w i formed by concatenating its forward and backward hidden states node initialization both srl based and dp based semantic graphs are defined within a unified framework to facilitate consistent node initialization

feedback systems for educational programming environments generally fall into three distinct categories each characterized by varying degrees of instructor interaction condition specification complexity and scalability automated hint generation systems this category typically minimizes instructor api interaction limiting the instructor s role to revealing specific error locations or full code edits the underlying logic relies on program synthesis to identify the next viable edit with the system outputting either a location or a suggested correction by automating the response generation this approach eliminates the labor intensive process of crafting customized feedback for a vast array of potential error states instructor authored feedback systems in contrast instructor authored systems facilitate deep tailored feedback designed to address significant conceptual breakdowns these systems require substantial instructor engagement via a comprehensive api instructors either define specific conditions directly or select from a system defined set of conditions to author corresponding responses the condition detection mechanisms in these systems are diverse often incorporating unit testing program synthesis and static program analysis while this approach allows for highly specific custom responses it is inherently limited in scalability covering the breadth of common mistakes is difficult and expanding the number of supported conditions is time consuming notable examples include mistake browser fix propagator csf and related research implementations unit testing based systems systems such as webcat and gradescope represent a middle ground between the previous two categories in these environments the api consists primarily of assertions and output checks which instructors use to compose conditions and define feedback for specific assertion failures this methodology enables instructors to cover a wider variety of conditions than purely instructor authored systems while maintaining a level of specificity that automated hint generation cannot achieve however they lack the granular customization of fully instructor authored systems and the broad reusability of automated common mistake detection infrastructure requirements based on an analysis of these exemplar systems three core infrastructure requirements emerge support for diverse services and condition specifications the infrastructure must accommodate the varied service requirements of different feedback approaches for instance while hint generation systems like itap utilize program synthesis with minimal api overhead instructor authored systems like mistake browser require more verbose response specification mechanisms inter tool communication efficient communication between tools is essential to prevent redundant analysis this is particularly critical for instructor authored systems which often need to leverage analytical results from multiple condition finding methodologies simultaneously override capability the system must provide the ability to override default behaviors or automated responses allowing for necessary customization in complex scenarios

while the specific relationship between narrative turning points and summarization has not been previously established prior research has underscored the significance of narrative structure in summarizing both literary works mihalcea and ceylan and social media content kim and monroy hernández more recently papalampidi et al demonstrated a method for identifying turning points in feature length screenplays by projecting synopsis level annotations distinct from previous approaches our methodology avoids the manual annotation of turning points within csi episodes instead we automatically approximate narrative structure by pretraining on the annotations from the tripod dataset papalampidi et al and utilizing a variant of their model our findings indicate that narrative structure representations derived from feature length films transfer effectively across diverse cinematic genres and computational tasks we propose an end to end training framework wherein narrative structure is treated as a latent variable within the summarization process furthermore we extend the csi dataset frermann et al by incorporating binary labels that indicate scene inclusion in summaries and we present experimental results utilizing both supervised and unsupervised summarization models an overview of this approach is provided in figure our primary contributions are as follows a the development of methods to integrate narrative structure knowledge into generic supervised and unsupervised summarization algorithms b the provision of a new annotation layer for the csi corpus to support research in long form summarization and c the demonstration that narrative structure facilitates screenplay summarization with analysis confirming that key events identified in the latent space correlate significantly with essential summary content related work a substantial body of existing literature has focused on the computational analysis of narratives mani richards et al

students from diverse backgrounds often encounter challenges in articulating key concepts orally or in writing the use of manipulatives offers an alternative mode of expression that renders student understanding observable thereby enabling those with limitations in expressive language to demonstrate their knowledge by observing student engagement with these tools educators can make student thinking explicit creating opportunities to scaffold instruction and enhance learning outcomes furthermore manipulatives can assist students in identifying and resolving their own misconceptions in the present study the game scratch charades was employed to elicit students interpretations of scratch scripts this section outlines the study design the methodology of observation and the subsequent analysis of the observational data the study involved fourth and fifth grade students from two public schools within a major metropolitan district these classrooms were selected from schools characterized by a high proportion of students from low socioeconomic status ses backgrounds and underrepresented minority populations there were no specific eligibility criteria for student participation however while all students in the five participating classes were invited to join data collection was limited to those whose parents provided signed consent forms prior to the study none of the participants had received formal instruction in scratch within their classrooms although some may have encountered the platform in other settings researchers functioned as observer participants their primary objective was observation yet they also engaged in interaction with the students observers focused on one or two groups at a time documenting significant events such as interpersonal interactions debugging strategies and instances where physical actions did not align with the corresponding scratch blocks each observation was recorded in narrative form noting whether the event involved the actor or the builder the specific script number and details regarding any discrepancies given that observers monitored multiple groups simultaneously the resulting dataset may not capture every instance of divergence between student interpretation and script execution additionally observers provided conceptual explanations to students who were unable to self correct errors this paper focuses on two primary aspects of the gameplay first it analyzes instances where the actor s movements failed to match the script or where the builder s interpretation of the code did not align with the actor s performance second it details the revisions made to the scratch charades game design based on the findings from these trials
