no method definitively improves forward and counterfactual simulatability together on the text task though our prototype and composite methods perform the best on average it appears that users’ quality ratings of explanations are not predictive of how helpful the explanations are with counterfactual simulation while users rate composite explanations as among the best in quality these combined explanations do not overtly improve simulatability in either data domain background and related work what does “interpretable” mean survey papers use key terms in varying ways rudin draws a distinction between interpretability and explainability suggesting that a model is interpretable if it performs computations that are directly understandable posthoc explanations on the other hand are potentially misleading approximations of the true computations gilpin et al also distinguish between the two concepts though they define them differently in this paper we do not distinguish between interpretability and explainability rather we adopt the conceptual framework of doshivelez and kim who consider interpretability in terms of downstream desiderata one can assess models with respect to our terminology is as follows we will say that explanation methods may improve the interpretability of a model in the sense that an interpretable model is simulatable explanation methods several taxonomies have been proposed for categorizing methods for interpretability we organize methods below into the categories of feature importance estimation casebased reasoning and latent space traversal feature importance estimation feature importance estimates provide information about how the model uses certain features most prominent among these methods are the gradientbased approaches first introduced for vision by simonyan et al which li et al show may be translated for use with text data these approaches have since been demonstrated to sometimes behave in counterintuitive ways a number of alternative methods have been proposed for quantifying feature importance across data domains in our study we choose to evaluate two domainagnostic approaches lime and anchor these methods use simple models i e sparse linear models and rule lists to approximate complex model behavior locally around inputs

both feature based and neural approaches have been proposed recently xia et al adopted transformer encoder augmented with position information and integrated global prediction embedding to improve performance fan et al incorporated sentiment and position regularizers to restrain parameter learning hu et al exploited external sentiment classification corpus to pretrain the model in other research lines some work extracted emotion causes in the context of microblog with multiuser structure besides kim and klinger and bostan et al addressed emotions as structured phenomena and studied the semantic roles of emotions including trigger phrases experiencers targets and causes as well as the reader’s perception emotioncause pair extraction all previous studies on emotion cause analysis need to take known emotion clauses as model input the pioneer work first put forward emotioncause pair extraction task they proposed a twostep approach to extract emotion and cause clauses separately and then train a classifier to filter out negative pairs unlike their work our work is a onestep solution for endtoend emotioncause pair extraction via effective interclause modeling achieving significantly better performance conclusion and future work in this paper we propose the first onestep neural approach r ank cp to tackle the problem of emotioncause pair extraction which emphasizes interclause modeling from a ranking perspective our approach effectively models interclause relationships to learn clause representations and integrates relative position enhanced clause pair ranking into a unified neural network to extract emotioncause pairs in an endtoend fashion experimental results on the benchmark dataset demonstrate that r ank cp significantly outperforms previous systems and further analysis verifies the effectiveness of each component in our model in future work we shall explore the following directions first current studies on emotion cause analysis mainly focus on clauselevel extraction which is relatively coarsegrained and it is desirable to further design finegrained methods that can extract spanlevel or phraselevel emotion expressions and causes

joint alignment across multiple recipes building upon pairwise alignments we derive a joint alignment at the dish level encompassing multiple text and video recipes for each dish we construct a graph wherein each node represents an instruction from a text recipe or a transcript sentence from a video recipe edges are established between nodes based on pairwise alignments with edge weights corresponding to alignment probabilities to ensure robustness only edges exhibiting an alignment probability exceeding are retained it is noted that these pairwise alignments are inherently directed mapping from the source recipe to the target recipe empirical analysis indicates that extending the alignment window beyond five steps results in diminished performance consequently we transform the directed graph into an undirected structure by averaging the weights of reciprocal edges between any two nodes this process may yield a graph containing multiple connected components as certain recipe pairs may lack any instructions with an alignment probability surpassing the threshold our objective is to identify a set of instructions that are jointly alignable across diverse recipes to achieve this we convert the cyclic graph into a forest by applying the maximum spanning tree algorithm an illustrative example of such a derived tree is presented in figure within this structure a path containing at most one node from each recipe constitutes a set of jointly alignable instructions for instance in the magnified section of figure the unique colored nodes along the path connecting the yellow node to the green node represent such a set experimental results this section details the evaluation methodology for the pairwise alignment algorithm described in section our experimental design addresses the following research questions how does the proposed alignment model perform when evaluated against human aligned recipe pairs does the unsupervised alignment model demonstrate superior performance compared to simpler non learning baselines how does performance vary when alignments are learned using only nouns versus a combination of nouns and verbs as opposed to utilizing the full vocabulary human aligned evaluation set the pairwise alignment algorithm is evaluated using two human annotated datasets youcook text video recipe pairs the youcook dataset zhou et al a comprises cooking videos each paired with human written descriptions for individual video segments these data cover distinct dishes

literature review on accent robustness and model analysis in automatic speech recognition research into enhancing the robustness of automatic speech recognition asr systems against accent variation has evolved through several methodological approaches vergyri et al examined the impact of multiple accents on english broadcast news recognition demonstrating that training accent independent acoustic models on diverse multi accented datasets yields performance improvements subsequently sun et al introduced domain adversarial training dat within a time delay neural network tdnn framework by treating native speech as the source domain and accented speech as the target domain their approach aims to generate accent invariant features for recognition further advancements in this domain include the integration of accent classification within multitask learning mtl frameworks jain et al combined an accent classifier with a multi accent tdnn based acoustic model building upon this viglino et al extended the mtl paradigm to an end to end architecture based on deep speech ds incorporating a secondary accent classifier that utilizes representations from intermediate recurrent layers additionally chen et al proposed an alternative methodology employing generative adversarial networks gans to disentangle accent specific and accent invariant components within acoustic features analysis of asr model representations parallel to efforts in improving model robustness significant work has been dedicated to analyzing the internal representations of asr models nagamine et al were among the first to investigate the representations within deep neural network dnn based acoustic models trained for phone prediction by computing selectivity metrics for individual phonemes the proposed neural network architecture is grounded in the wordvec algorithm specifically adapted to incorporate subword information rather than relying solely on whole word representations during the training phase of word embeddings out of vocabulary terms are handled by decomposing them into character n grams thereby enabling the generation of embeddings for words absent from the initial dataset to evaluate the efficacy of various feature types this study employed five distinct machine learning classifiers multilayer perceptron mlp adaboost logistic regression lr random forest rf and support vector machine svm all models were configured using their default hyperparameters logistic regression lr serves as a linear model predicated on the assumption of a linear relationship between input features and the output utilizing the sigmoid function it estimates the probability of a categorical dependent variable specifically distinguishing between abusive and non abusive content empirical evidence suggests that lr yields superior performance in binary classification tasks particularly in the automated detection of abusive language random forest rf is an ensemble learning technique applicable to both classification and regression tasks it employs bagging and feature randomness across diverse data samples to construct multiple decision trees in classification scenarios the final prediction is determined by majority voting among the trees whereas regression tasks utilize the average of the individual tree predictions this aggregation mechanism enhances predictive accuracy compared to single decision trees by randomly selecting features to build individual trees the algorithm effectively mitigates variance and overfitting recent literature confirms the utility of random forest in classifying abusive language support vector machine svm is a predictive analysis algorithm designed for linear and nonlinear classification as well as regression it operates by constructing a hyperplane decision boundary that optimally separates data into distinct classes through the application of the kernel trick svm transforms the input data to identify an optimal boundary with the maximum margin of separation this approach is particularly effective in high dimensional spaces where the number of dimensions exceeds the number of data instances furthermore comparative studies indicate that svm often outperforms other classifiers in the automatic identification of abusive language finally the boosting algorithm specifically adaboost in this context functions as a supervised learning method that iteratively combines multiple weak learners it achieves improved performance by reassigning weights to input data instances thereby focusing subsequent iterations on previously misclassified examples

pressure is defined as the ratio of force to area p f a consequently a reduction in the contact area results in a significant increase in pressure even when the applied force is minimal a common illustration of this principle is a pin which can penetrate hard surfaces with negligible force due to its minute cross sectional area generating high localized pressure figure depicts a fundamental hydraulic system comprising piping filled with an incompressible fluid such as oil or water in such a system pressure is transmitted uniformly throughout the fluid this property allows a relatively small force applied to a piston with a small cross sectional area to support a substantially larger load on a piston with a larger area for example a force sufficient to support a total mass of kg on a disk with a meter diameter can be generated by a much smaller input force this mechanical advantage is accompanied by a displacement trade off the volume of fluid displaced by the movement of the small piston must equal the volume required to elevate the larger piston necessitating a significantly greater travel distance for the input piston in automotive hydraulic braking systems the required displacement of the brake pads is minimal and the piston diameters are correspondingly small often measuring one inch or less the motion of the brake pedal functioning as a lever actuates a piston within the hydraulic system thereby increasing fluid pressure this pressure transmission drives pistons at the wheel assemblies to engage the brakes typically a master cylinder houses the brake fluid reservoir and facilitates pressure transmission in vehicles equipped with power assisted braking pedal actuation drives a pump that amplifies the system pressure in proportion to the applied pedal force most modern vehicles utilize disk brakes for the front wheels and often for the rear as illustrated in figure in this configuration a rotating disk attached to the wheel is clamped by brake pads actuated by hydraulic pressure conversely rear brakes frequently employ a drum mechanism figure wherein a rotating drum is affixed to the wheel a hydraulic cylinder containing two pistons is connected to the brake system increased pressure forces these pistons outward pressing the brake pads against the interior surface of the spinning drum finally the aerodynamic phenomena responsible for lift on an aircraft wing and the propulsion of a sailboat in the direction of the wind are explained by the principles of fluid dynamics specifically these effects are derived from the application of the first law of thermodynamics to fluid flow commonly expressed as bernoulli s equation

students practice a more insightful use of the design techniques rather than just as a mechanical habit iour observations confirm findings from prior studies that students think in terms of a problem’s core tasks where they struggle is in concretizing relationships between tasks which affects their ability to compose tasks’ code implementations especially for tasks they have not seen before using techniques from the design recipe can help uncover some of these relationships some taskrelationships required new patterns students have not seen for example doing a recursion on a modified list suffix or prefix or keeping track of lists using another list this suggests that just because students have seen an instance of a pattern e g a list of numbers recursion on the tail of the list does not mean that students can generalize those patterns to other contexts e g a list of lists recursion on a modified suffix of the list as instructors we should be careful not to assume that students have understood the underlying idea behind a pattern simply because we’ve shown them an instance of it focusing on enforcing students’ understanding of patterns they’re taught may help them navigate between tasks and code better in new situations overall our findings indicate that students need to be explicitly taught techniques for navigating back to highlevel plans when they get stuck and how to identify when current code has diverged from a plan and needs to be rethought both are significant open questions for computing education abstract the students’ skills simultaneously we present a new tls dataset that is larger than previous datasets and spans longer time ranges decades of news timelines related work timeline summarization for news articles has received some attention in the last two decades the task is commonly split into date selection and date summarization subtasks date selection supervised machine learning has been proposed to predict whether dates appear in groundtruth timelines tran et al use graphbased ranking of dates which is reported to outperform supervised methods date summarization several approaches construct date summaries by picking sentences from ranked lists the ranking is based on regression or learningtorank to predict rouge scores between the sentence and a groundtruth summary tran despite our best efforts we could neither obtain code for this method from the authors nor reproduce its reported performance and therefore did not include it in our experiments et al observe that users prefer summaries consisting of headlines to summaries consisting of sentences from article bodies steen and markert propose abstractive date summarization based on graphbased sentence merging and compression other works propose the use of additional data such as comments on social media or images full timeline summarization chieu and lee produce timelines by ranking sentences from an entire document collection the ranking is based on summed up similarities to other sentences in an n day window nguyen et al propose a pipeline to generate timelines consisting of date selection sentence clustering and ranking

the definition of vignettes employed in this study refines the conceptualization proposed by finch specifically vignettes are defined as incomplete short narratives designed to mirror real life scenarios with reduced complexity thereby facilitating discourse and the exploration of multiple potential solutions to complex problems while the domain specific application of vignettes is well documented in certain fields—such as the work of brovelli et al who utilized them to assess the pedagogical content knowledge of prospective science teachers—their use within computer science teacher education remains underexplored yadav et al conducted a qualitative study employing vignettes to evaluate teachers responses to student misconceptions regarding programming constructs their findings highlighted a critical need to enhance teacher education concerning the management of programming errors however the methodology for vignette development was not detailed in their report nor were the vignettes themselves provided it can be inferred nonetheless that the instruments were specifically tailored to basic programming constructs a common thread among the aforementioned studies is the utilization of vignettes primarily for assessment purposes in contrast the present study aimed to develop vignettes intended to stimulate discussion among prospective teachers within a seminar setting at a teacher training center the objective was to foster peer reflection which may be moderated by an in service advisor as necessary such reflective practices if sustained beyond the initial training period are expected to support the development of reflective teaching reflective teaching has been empirically linked to professional growth the transition of novice teachers to expert status and the effective management of classroom diversity educational context this study was conducted within a federal state of the federal republic of germany the secondary teacher training system in this jurisdiction adheres to the guidelines established by the standing conference of the ministers of education and cultural affairs of the länder the training pathway mandates that all prospective teachers complete both a bachelor of education b ed and a master of education m ed degree focusing on two subject areas and educational sciences this initial phase of training includes a mandatory six month internship at a secondary school

we consider four implementations bm a common keywordbased scoring model for retrieval methods roberta base we use a finetuned roberta base model as text encoder rnn we use a recurrent neural network rnn with simple recurrent unit recurrence as text encoder together with a fasttext word embedding layer and rnn selfattn the same rnn neural model with a multihead selfattention layer • random interaction at each turn the system randomly selects a question to present the user after t turns the classification label is chosen according to the belief p y x t • no initial query interaction the system selects questions without conditioning on the initial user query using maximum information criterion this is equivalent to using a static decision tree to pick the question always asking the same first question • variants of our approach we consider several variants of our full model first we replace the policy controller with two termination strategies end the interaction when max p y x t passes a threshold or end the interaction after a fixed number of turns second we disable the parameterized estimator ˜ p r k q j y i by setting λ evaluation we use human evaluation and further analyze performance using our simulator for human evaluation users interact with our systems and baseline models using a webbased interactive interface each interaction starts with a user scenario a bird image or a devicetroubleshooting scenario described in text the user types an initial query and answers followup questions selected by the system once the system returns its prediction we measure its accuracy and the user is asked to rate the whole interaction according to rationality and naturalness the user does not know the correct target label we use a fivepoints likert score for the followup questions

the following two examples demonstrate the proposed methodology in the isear statement when i made the same mistake that i had accused someone else of and this was obvious to a third person the senticnet concepts make mistake obvious and to person were identified the classifier assigned the labels sadness joy and surprise to these concepts respectively it is important to note that these labels are assigned to concept types rather than to specific textual occurrences in a second example the statement when friends try to put me down or hurt me yielded the concepts friend put down and hurt which were assigned the labels joy sadness and fear respectively two primary sources of error were identified first the algorithm occasionally failed to account for the co occurrence of related words appearing in different sentences thereby hindering the transfer of contextual information between terms second word sense ambiguity presented a significant challenge to address these limitations the emotion labels from the semeval wordnetaffect lists were extended to encompass concepts from the senticnet resource to the best of our knowledge this constitutes the largest emotion labeled resource and the first quantitatively marked emotion lexicon furthermore the impact of various psychological features on emotion classification was analyzed this work establishes several avenues for future research priority directions include utilizing diverse corpora to enhance feature coverage and label a broader range of concepts as well as employing advanced classification techniques that integrate syntactic and psychological cues to improve accuracy finally the developed resource will be applied to real world opinion mining tasks such as the analysis of patient data building upon the demonstrated efficacy of senticnet in similar domains

deep learning for the authorship attribution task in one work the authors propose a framework for learning distributed representations of attributes the attributes correspond to a wide variety of similar to the work from spacco et al who explored the correlation between amount of exercises and exam scores our work adds a perspective of behavior consistency to the understanding of the relationship between exercises and exam scores the analysis of micro and macro persistence patterns to some extent is related to spaced learning effect and selfregulated learning srl the spaced learning effect suggests that a learning process spaced over a period of time is more effective than the one occurs in a single session of time srl emphasizes the importance of metacognitive skills which regulate students’ learning process in this study we use the study persistence patterns to model the way students practice on the selfassessment platform this variable implicitly reflects how a student schedule his learning process for example active students keep the amount of practices similar in every week we also found that pattern consistency is positively correlated to exam performance it is possible that keeping a certain practicing behavior over a period of time is helpful for students to achieve higher performance although this study has provided a preliminary evidence for this relationship however it still requires further research following the techniques in literature of srl and spaced learning effect to cross validate the performance of hp students is relatively stable in all alterations except for ic and is the scores of is students drop dramatically compared to ic students who have quite stable performance if the cramming pattern is a sign of reviewing exam content it is possible that is students do no have effective reviewing as ic students have it is also possible that is students simply do not have enough practices to keep their performance either way the current dataset is not sufficient for providing clear explanations behind this phenomenon this analysis has shown that a single source of information from a selfassessment platform may not be enough to explain all variance of exam scores in the class this study explores the use of a probability mixture model for modeling study persistence on a selfassessment platform we investigate how micro and macro persistence patterns are related to students’ exam performance four micro and several macro persistence patterns are identified the correlation analysis shows a positive correlation between exam scores and consistency of persistence patterns

achieving and verifying certified robustness is highly challenging even if the synonym table used by the attacker is known during training because it requires to check every possible synonymous word substitution whose number is exponentially large various defense methods against synonymous word substitution attacks have been developed most of which however are not certified robust in that they may eventually be broken by stronger attackers recently jia et al huang et al proposed the first certified robust methods against word substitution attacking their methods are based on the interval bound propagation ibp method which computes the range of the model output by propagating the interval constraints of the inputs layer by layer however the ibpbased methods of jia et al huang et al are limited in several ways first because ibp only works for certifying neural networks with continuous inputs the inputs in jia et al and huang et al are taken to be the word embedding vectors of the input sentences instead of the discrete sentences this makes it inapplicable to characterlevel and subwordlevel model which are more widely used in practice in this paper we propose a structurefree certified defense method that applies to arbitrary models that can be queried in a blackbox fashion without any requirement on the model structures our method is based on the idea of randomized smoothing which smooths the model with random word substitutions build on the synonymous network and leverage the statistical properties of the randomized ensembles to construct provably certification bounds similar ideas of provably certification using randomized smoothing have been developed recently in deep learning but mainly for computer vision tasks whose inputs images are in a continuous space our method admits a substantial extension of the randomized smoothing technique to discrete and structured input spaces for nlp we test our method on various types of nlp models including text cnn charcnn and bert

to ensure a fair comparison with existing systems the evaluation protocol for each bridging anaphor a involves mapping the top span predictions generated by the barqa system to the corresponding gold standard or system generated mentions the mention associated with the highest confidence score is subsequently selected as the predicted antecedent a predicted span s is mapped to a mention m if they share the same head word and s is a substring of m where m is derived by removing all post modifiers from m for example the span total potential claims is mapped to the mention the total potential claims from the disaster predicted spans that cannot be mapped to any valid mention are excluded from the evaluation following the methodology of hou a predictions are further filtered based on semantic type consistency specifically if the anaphor a is a temporal expression only predictions classified as time are retained this procedure effectively utilizes gold standard mentions and their semantic attributes to prune the initial span predictions of barqa tables and present the comparative performance of barqa against prior studies on bridging anaphora resolution for the isnotes and and exploiting this artifact particularly within the synthetic hans dataset a critical question arises regarding whether the model relies on the same artifact when processing natural language examples to address this we applied influence functions to a set of natural mnli examples which were not controlled for specific artifacts as illustrated in figure c the average quadratic coefficient across these natural examples is times this value is substantially lower than those observed in the hans dataset indicating that the model relies significantly less on lexical overlap when processing natural artifacts while lexical overlap is known to serve as an artifact for entailment predictions in hans other features such as negation may signal non entailment as noted by mccoy et al we therefore features compete for influence during the prediction process

to augment existing security protocols a supplementary data preprocessing mechanism has been implemented for police systems prior to data sharing this methodology employs a whitelisting strategy that redacts all tokens absent from a curated lexicon of approximately common words notably this lexicon excludes proper names thereby further mitigating the risk of personal data disclosure although this approach may be less optimal than alternative methods it offers distinct advantages in terms of determinism and interpretability data governance is maintained through three core pillars secure environments all data are stored within modern secure infrastructures access is strictly limited to a designated secure area accessible solely by authorized project team members vetted personnel all research team members undergo rigorous vetting by the relevant police force to ensure compliance with necessary data handling standards collaborative insight sharing all research insights are shared with police partners furthermore all publications detailing the research undergo a multi party review process involving representatives from both law enforcement and academia prior to submission it is acknowledged that these security measures will influence the characteristics of the received data potentially affecting the generalizability of natural language processing nlp applications to specific data types such as witness statements nevertheless this framework provides a foundational approach to evaluating the utility of nlp within problem oriented policing pop processes while machine learning text mining and data science are increasingly recognized as valuable tools by crime scientists a recent review of the intersection between criminology and artificial intelligence highlights a significant disparity although various ai and machine learning methods are present in criminological literature there remains a notable paucity of nlp specific research compared to other domains consequently this section focuses exclusively on the analysis of free text police data current literature on free text crime analysis is predominantly characterized by unsupervised learning approaches centered on crime linkage rather than crime reduction crime linkage aims to identify offenses committed by the same individual s whereas pop typically necessitates grouping crimes based on enabling characteristics prominent examples of unsupervised nlp applied to police free text data include the work of birks et al and kuang et al who utilize these techniques to cluster crimes based on modus operandi rather than traditional classifications specifically birks et al conducted this analysis within a single crime classification while kuang et al extended the methodology across multiple crime classifications

we find that the average gaps between confidence and accuracy are much larger in inference than in training i e the study of calibration on classification tasks has a long history from statistical machine learning to deep learning however calibration on structured generation tasks such as neural machine translation nmt has not been well studied recently m ¨ uller et al and kumar and sarawagi studied the calibration of nmt in the training setting and found that nmt trained with label smoothing is wellcalibrated we believe that this setting would cover up a central problem of nmt the exposure bias – the traininginference discrepancy caused by teacher forcing in the training of autoregressive models in response to this problem this work focuses on the calibration of nmt in inference which can better reflect the generative capacity of nmt models to this end we use translation error rate ter to automatically annotate the correctness of generated tokens which makes it feasible to evaluate calibration in inference experimental results on several datasets across language pairs show that even trained with label smoothing nmt models still suffer from miscalibration errors in inference figure shows an example while modern neural networks on classification tasks have been found to be miscalibrated in the direction of overestimation i e confidence accuracy nmt models are also underestimated i e confidence accuracy on lowconfidence predictions in addition we found that miscalibrated predictions correlate well with the translation errors in inference specifically the overestimated predictions correlate more with overtranslation and mistranslation errors while the underestimated predictions correlate more with undertranslation errors this demonstrates the necessity of studying inference calibration for nmt by investigating the linguistic properties of miscalibrated tokens in nmt outputs we have several interesting findings • frequency lowfrequency tokens generally suffer from underestimation

the implementation of course mappings and assessment protocols may be executed on a modular course by course basis eliminating the necessity for immediate curriculum wide integration consequently the initiative commenced with core computer science courses with plans for gradual expansion contingent upon available resources this phased approach mitigates semesterly administrative burdens thereby ensuring the sustainability of the assessment framework even when competing institutional priorities arise furthermore tailoring the assessment system to the specific needs of its primary stakeholders—faculty and staff—fosters greater adoption and engagement by incorporating customization utilities that align with individual professional objectives the system garners increased attention and utility for instance a specialized knowledge domain was integrated to address departmental preferences regarding journal article literacy additionally data collection was structured around course section entities and a mechanism was established for students to provide or withhold research consent on a per quiz basis thereby facilitating faculty led pedagogical research these features elevate the system beyond a mere compliance instrument for accreditation it is imperative to recognize that this assessment framework functions strictly as an analytical tool while it effectively identifies trends it does not inherently elucidate their underlying causes however consistent application accelerates the detection of such trends narrowing the scope required for causal analysis this allows investigators to focus on specific semesters and courses rather than dissecting the entire curriculum conversely the system s reliance on direct assessment scores means it may not capture all critical nuances of instruction or the broader strategies employed by educators to meet student needs therefore caution is advised when utilizing this system or similar methodologies for the evaluation of individual performance in its current iteration the cads framework is deemed suitable for field testing nevertheless the full potential of the accumulated data remains underutilized future objectives include generating a more diverse array of reports that explicitly leverage prerequisite chains student demographics and additional data points beyond this the development of a real time visualization system is planned this tool will allow researchers to interactively adjust parameters to facilitate deeper causal analysis finally and perhaps most critically the collection of comprehensive feedback following a sufficient trial period will inform the refinement and expansion of the system s architecture

the objective is to compute the arithmetic mean of all non negative values within a provided list terminating the process upon encountering the sentinel value it is noted that the list may contain other negative integers representing erroneous data points which must be excluded from the calculation in the event that the average cannot be determined due to insufficient valid data or other constraints the function shall return the recommended implementation strategy involves a single traversal of the input sequence during this pass the algorithm should accumulate the sum and count of all non negative values ceasing operations immediately upon reaching the sentinel value or the end of the list following the traversal the system must verify that the count of valid entries is non zero to prevent division by zero before calculating and returning the final average alternative approaches involving multiple traversals or the construction of intermediate data structures are also viable but less efficient this problem contextualized within a rainfall data analysis scenario presents a distinct pedagogical challenge while students are typically proficient in discrete operations such as summing counting and filtering lists based on criteria they often lack experience with termination conditions defined by specific sentinel values rather than list exhaustion the primary difficulty lies in the composition of these subtasks unlike structurally recursive problems that follow standard design templates this task requires decomposing the computation into distinct sub procedures and integrating them cohesively a related more complex problem involves processing a list of maximum temperatures this task necessitates restructuring the input into a list of sublists by omitting delimiters followed by a recursive computation of the maximum value for each inner list valid solutions for this scenario require advanced techniques such as maintaining state via additional parameters or modifying the input structure during traversal which may exceed the scope of standard introductory coursework although nested data structures are a new concept for learners the underlying design principles remain consistent with previously studied recursive patterns

we present an approach for the construction of text similarity functions using a parameterized resemblance coefficient in combination with a softened cardinality function called soft cardinality our approach provides a consistent and recursive model varying levels of granularity from sentences to characters therefore our model was used to compare sentences divided into words and in turn words divided into qgrams of characters experimentally we observed that a performance correlation function in a space defined by all parameters was relatively smooth and had a single maximum achievable by hill climbing our approach used only surface text information a stopword remover and a stemmer to tackle the semantic text similarity task at semeval the proposed method ranked rd average th normalized correlation and th aggregated correlation among systems submitted by teams similarity is the intrinsic ability of humans and some animals to balance commonalities and differences when comparing objects that are not identical although there is no direct evidence of how this process works in living organisms some models have been proposed from the cognitive perspective on the other hand several similarity models have been proposed in mathematics statistics and computer science among other fields particularly in ai similarity measures play an important role in the construction of intelligent systems that are required to exhibit behavior similar to humans for instance in the field of natural language processing text similarity functions provide estimates of the human similarity judgments related to language in this paper we combine elements from the perspective of cognitive psychology and computer science to propose a model for building similarity functions suitable for the task of semantic text similarity we identify four main families of text similarity functions i resemblance coefficients based on sets e g jaccard s and dice s coefficients ii functions in metric spaces e g cosine tfidf similarity iii the edit distance family of measures e g levenstein distance lcs and iv hybrid approaches all of these measures use a subdivision of the texts in different granularity levels such as qgrams of words words qgrams of characters syllables and characters among hybrid approaches mongeelkan s measure and soft cardinality methods are recursive and can be used to build similarity functions at any arbitrary range of granularity

thirty years have seen many advances in our understanding of issues that impede diversity as well as interventions that can remove or mitigate those impediments the advances are generally presented in psychology journals and are underconsumed by computing educators leading to the need for diversity training tailored to said educators we have hundreds of hours of experience providing diversity training to computing educators and have learned many lessons about how training should and should not be delivered to engage participants in understanding diversityimpeding forces and implementing interventions to improve the attraction and retention of diverse students this paper is a collection of those lessons learned we focus on concrete approaches to presenting material rather than on training organization or content including a dozen specific practices that have proven particularly effective or ineffective in reaching diverse audiences examples include how how to avoid offending participants how to help experts not lose their audience how to pace and organize material for maximum impact how we tie many topics into a single cognitive framework and how we’ve learned to handle the absence of a fixed word order implies that the syntactic position of a constituent does not inherently determine its semantic role case instead these roles are explicitly marked by postpositional case particles table outlines typical sentence constituents in japanese their associated particles their canonical ordering and the corresponding sections of this paper dedicated to their analysis it should be noted that postpositional case particles are occasionally omitted or substituted with alternative particles such as adverbial particles see section these linguistic characteristics introduce significant complexity into the factors governing word order thereby posing substantial challenges for the automatic analysis of japanese syntax evaluation methodologies for word order hypotheses and associated challenges linguistic research primarily employs two methodological frameworks for evaluating word order hypotheses human based methods which rely on the observation of human responses and data driven methods which utilize the analysis of text corpora human based methods a standard approach within this framework involves measuring human reactions such as reading times in response to specific word order variations shigenaga bahlmann et al while these approaches offer direct empirical observation of human processing they are constrained by scalability issues furthermore concerns persist regarding potential participant bias and the replicability of experimental results data driven methods an alternative approach involves quantifying the frequency of targeted phenomena within large scale corpora this count based methodology operates on the premise that a correlation exists between canonical word order and the frequency of occurrence in extensive datasets this correlation has been extensively debated arnon snider bresnan et al and serves as the foundation for numerous studies sasano okumura kempen harbusch a primary advantage of this approach is its suitability for large scale experimentation allowing for the consideration of a vast number of instances however this method often necessitates the use of preprocessing tools such as predicate argument structure parsers e g sasano okumura to identify and count the phenomena of interest a significant limitation arises when these preprocessors fail to accurately identify the targeted structures thereby restricting the scope of analysis for instance sasano and okumura restricted their analysis to simple examples where case markers were explicitly present and extracted only the head noun of the argument to mitigate preprocessing errors consequently phenomena failing to meet these specific conditions remained unanalyzed

weighting instance weighting has been broadly adopted for reducing bias for example the inverse propensity score ips method has been successfully applied for causal effect analyses selection bias the analysis phase of the design process is likely to be the most familiar component for students given that a significant portion of engineering curricula focuses on analytical disciplines such as circuit structural and thermal analysis upon the completion of this analysis and the implementation of any requisite design modifications the construction phase commences as illustrated in figure the design process is inherently nonlinear reflecting the nature of creative endeavor and contrasting with the linear logic characteristic of mathematical analysis and scientific inquiry the internal feedback loops depicted in the figure indicate that the process is iterative at any stage it is permissible to revisit previous steps to incorporate new information refine specifications or adjust constraints it is common for the conceptual design to undergo modifications during construction such deviations are not only acceptable but are an integral aspect of the creative process subsequent to construction rigorous testing is conducted to verify that the device effectively addresses the problem statement this testing must adhere to scientific protocols to ensure the validity and reliability of the resulting data this stage determines whether the final product meets the specified requirements and constraints in a commercial context this evaluation informs a management decision regarding the viability of proceeding to manufacturing while it may seem counterintuitive to terminate a project following substantial creative investment such decisions are often necessitated by factors including budget exhaustion personnel reallocation extended development timelines affecting market viability or the emergence of superior competing technologies regarding project execution students may engage in individual or collaborative efforts however in industrial settings the end to end responsibility for conceiving designing and manufacturing a product is rarely assigned to a single individual due to the scale and complexity of such undertakings instead these functions are distributed among specialized engineering disciplines in traditional organizational structures research and development divisions establish design feasibility while design engineers are tasked with generating alternative solutions selecting the optimal design within defined constraints and producing the detailed drawings and specifications required for manufacturing implementation

we thus extend fillmore’s slogan to include all kinds of conceptual content beyond scenes the broader communicative context and the effect of choices made as part of the construal process meanings are relativized to content context and construal below we elaborate on how each of these interrelated factors affects construed meaning conceptual content we assume that linguistic units can evoke and combine all kinds of conceptual content including openended world knowledge entities actions events relations etc as well as more schematic structures often associated with grammar and function words crucially concepts must also be amenable to certain kinds of transformation e g shifts in perspective or granularity as part of construal see below communicative context we take meaning to encompass scenelevel entities and events discourselevel information about the interlocutors and their communicative intents and other phenomena straddling the fuzzy semanticpragmatic boundary related to attention e g profiling and perspective and conditions of usage falling under what fillmore dubbed “usemantics” in contrast to truthoriented “tsemantics” contextual factors e g the interlocutors’ identity beliefs goals conceptual repertoire cultural backgrounds can radically alter construed meaning on this view meaning is not arbitrarily subjective or merely intersubjective it is also constrained by all aspects of the communicative context construal we define construal as a dynamic process of meaning construction in which speakers and hearers encode and decode respectively some intended meaning in a given communicative context to do so they draw on their repertoire of linguistic and conceptual structures composing and transforming them to build coherent interpretations consistent with the speaker’s lexical grammatical and other expressive choices we take construal to be fundamental to all language use though how much construal and what we are not here concerned with precisely how concepts are represented or learned since we believe the insights related to construal apply broadly across theoretical frameworks for example only usemantics can explain why “the children are on the bus” is preferred over “the children are in the bus” if the bus is in transit despite referring to the same spatial relationship both speakers and hearers engage in construal speakers in choosing how to present the idea experience or other content they wish to convey hearers in reconstructing that intended meaning

however the alignment error rate aer is limited in its ability to evaluate explanation methods for target words that lack corresponding source word alignments in human annotations to address this limitation this paper presents an initial framework for assessing explanation methods in neural machine translation nmt based on the second dimension of interpretability which encompasses all target words the core principle of our approach is fidelity an explanation method is deemed effective if the subset of words it identifies as most relevant can be used to construct an optimal proxy model that closely aligns with the nmt model s translation decisions section consequently we formalize a principled evaluation metric as an optimization problem aimed at minimizing the expected disagreement between the optimal proxy model and the nmt model section given that exact calculation of this principled metric is computationally intractable we propose an approximate metric to resolve the optimization problem drawing inspiration from statistical learning theory vapnik we reformulate the optimization task as a standard machine learning problem addressed via a two step strategy first optimizing empirical risk through empirical risk minimization and second validating the optimized parameters on a held out test dataset furthermore we construct diverse proxy model architectures utilizing the identified relevant words to generate translation decisions resulting in various implementations of the approximate metric section we apply this approximate metric to evaluate four prominent explanation methods attention mechanisms bahdanau et al vaswani et al gradient norm li et al weighted gradient ding et al and prediction difference li et al extensive experiments were conducted across three standard translation tasks using two widely adopted translation models our findings yield two key insights gradient norm and prediction difference methods demonstrate strong efficacy in interpreting nmt behavior and the prediction difference method outperforms the other evaluated approaches

achieving and verifying certified robustness against synonym substitution attacks remains a significant challenge even when the attacker s synonym table is known during the training phase jia et al this difficulty arises from the necessity of evaluating every possible synonymous substitution a task complicated by the exponential size of the search space while various defense mechanisms have been proposed to mitigate such attacks e g wallace et al ebrahimi et al the majority lack certified robustness and remain vulnerable to more sophisticated adversaries recently jia et al and huang et al introduced the first certified robust methods specifically targeting word substitution attacks these approaches utilize interval bound propagation ibp dvijotham et al which computes output ranges by propagating input interval constraints layer by layer however these ibp based methods face notable limitations primarily because ibp is designed for neural networks with continuous inputs the aforementioned studies treat inputs as word embedding vectors rather than discrete sentences consequently these methods are inapplicable to character level zhang et al and subword level bojanowski et al models which are widely adopted in practical applications wu et al to address these constraints this paper proposes a structure agnostic certified defense method applicable to arbitrary models accessible via black box queries without imposing requirements on model architecture our approach leverages randomized smoothing wherein the model is smoothed through random word substitutions derived from a synonym network by exploiting the statistical properties of these randomized ensembles we construct provable certification bounds although the concept of provable certification via randomized smoothing has recently gained traction in deep learning e g cohen et al salman et al zhang et al lee et al existing applications have predominantly focused on computer vision tasks involving continuous input spaces cohen et al our work represents a substantial extension of randomized smoothing techniques to the discrete and structured input spaces characteristic of natural language processing nlp we evaluate the efficacy of our method across diverse nlp architectures including textcnn kim charcnn zhang et al and bert devlin et al

discriminative classifier illustrated in figure the algorithm initially samples the vicinity of the original input to identify instances that cross the decision boundary from this set a counterfactual input is selected by prioritizing the instance requiring the minimal number of feature modifications tokens or variables ties are resolved based on the euclidean distance between latent representations subsequently a trajectory connecting the original and counterfactual inputs is constructed by greedily selecting edits that minimally alter the model s evidence margin defined as the difference between positive and negative class scores the resulting explanations presented to users comprise the original input the sequential steps leading to the counterfactual and the evidence margin at each stage for trajectories exceeding four steps only the final four are displayed composite approach we hypothesize that the aforementioned explanation methods offer complementary insights due to their distinct analytical approaches consequently we evaluate a composite method that integrates lime and anchor with our decision boundary and prototype based explanations two specific adjustments are implemented during this integration first the decision boundary explanation is truncated to display only the final step representing the specific set of changes required to invert the prediction second the prototype model is trained with feature extraction layers initialized from the neural task model and subsequently frozen this strategy ensures that the generated prototypes accurately reflect the characteristics of the target task model thereby enhancing the fidelity of the behavioral explanation experimental design this section outlines the datasets task models student identification is verified through a cryptographic hashing mechanism wherein student names are hashed to generate a value stored in congruence with their unique identifiers verification is achieved by cross referencing a student s supplied id and name hash nh against database records a match confirms the validity of the id with a high degree of certainty in instances of mismatch the system flags the discrepancy and provides uploaders with the opportunity to manually rectify the affected data entries the final university provided document which details enrollments and course grades largely reiterates information found in the student data spreadsheet and is processed using a similar methodology given that preceding documents have already been ingested this file primarily serves a verification function for existing data however as the sole repository of final course grades it is indispensable for analyzing the impact of various factors such as prerequisite knowledge on overall student performance in contrast to the provided institutional documents the custom spreadsheets are designed to contain only essential information functioning as static information hubs that require no routine updates post upload while the initial creation of these documents entails a moderate investment of effort they represent a one time cost as only specific intentional modifications are subsequently required the course and standard spreadsheet the first of the two custom documents aggregates knowledge topics kt student learning objectives slo course details and taxonomic data although this workbook comprises eight sheets in total four are dedicated exclusively to organizational purposes a readme sheet providing documentation a list of knowledge areas kas as defined by the acm ieee cs joint task force a comprehensive catalog of all knowledge topics kt a registry of department specific kts added to supplement the standard acm list the remaining four sheets establish concrete relational mappings within the dataset student learning objectives this sheet details slos their descriptions institutional dates and their current active status taxonomic data utilizing both the matrix taxonomy and the revised bloom s taxonomy this sheet assigns a unique numeric id and textual description to each taxonomic level these ids facilitate the mapping processes described in the final sheet course information this sheet outlines the complete course structure including disciplines course ids titles and prerequisite or auxiliary course relationships effectively mapping the curriculum through prerequisite chaining mapping sheet this final sheet utilizes the taxonomic ids to define the specific relationships between the various data elements

to account for shortest dependency path sdp information we include an sdp tree depth task sdptreedepth which tests if the learned sentence embedding stores information about the syntactical link between the relation arguments again we group sdp tree depth values into bins in this case only classes since the sdp trees are generally more shallow than the original sentence dependency parse tree the argument ordering task argord tests if the head argument of a relation occurs before the tail argument in the token sequence an encoder that successfully addresses this challenge captures some information about syntactic structures where the order of a relation’s arguments is inverted e g in constructions such as “the acquisition of monsanto by bayer” as compared to default constructions like “bayer acquired monsanto” we also include tasks that test for the partofspeech tag of the token directly to the left or right of the relation’s arguments posheadl posheadr postaill postailr these tasks test whether the encoder is sensitive to the immediate context of an argument some relation types e g per nationality or org top member can often be identified based on the immediate argument context e g “us presidentnn donald trump” or “google ’sposs ceonn larry page” representing this type of information in the sentence embedding should be useful for the relation classification argument information finally we include probing tasks that require some understanding of what each argument denotes the argument entity type tasks typehead typetail ask for the entity tag of the head and respectively the tail argument entity type information is highly relevant for relation extraction systems since it strongly constrains the set of possible relation labels for a given argument pair we treat these tasks as multiclass classification problems over the set of possible argument entity tags see section our last task concerns the grammatical function of relation arguments the grammatical role tasks grhead grtail ask for the role of each argument as given by the dependency label connecting the argument and its syntactic head token the motivation is that the subject and object of verbal identically distributed training set and test set the remaining two letters of the paid acronym refer to the practice of evaluating success on classification tasks using training and test set generated using the same process typically a single data set is collected and is randomly split into a training portion and test portion while this may seem reasonable from a machine learning perspective it has become clear that this form of evaluation obscures possible mismatches between the generalizations that we as humans believe a system performing the task should acquire and the generalizations that the system in fact extracts from the data consider for example crowdsourced natural language inference nli data sets in which workers are asked to generate a sentence that contradicts the prompt shown to them one strategy that crowdworkers adopt when generating a contradiction is to simply negate the prompt for example by inserting the word not this strategy is often effective the man is sleeping contradicts the man is not sleeping conversely it is much less likely that the worker would use the word not when asked to generate a sentence that is entailed by the prompt taken together such worker choices lead to a strong correlation between the presence of the word not in the hypothesis and the label contra diction it would be surprising if lowbias learners such as neural networks did not notice such a correlation and indeed they do leading them to respond contradiction with high probability any time the hypothesis contains a negation word of course relying on the presence of the word not is not a generally valid inference strategy for example the man is awake entails rather than contradicts the man is not sleeping numerous generalization issues of this sort have been documented for nli and for other tasks

and stab to detect threatening language the natural language processing community has been focusing on online platforms like youtube twitter facebook instagram and blogs multiple studies relied on chisquare feature selection as well as used lexicon based techniques to study automatic threatening language detection in addition many researchers used character ngrams word ngrams and their combinations for threatening language detection a few studies investigated automatic detection of threatening language in multilingual datasets and used machine learning techniques several studies have utilized support vector machines svm and logistic regression lr classifiers to detect threatening speech in tweets articles blogs facebook and reddit similarly naive bayes nb was used to classify threatening comments in user generated youtube comments and newsgroups while decision tree dt was used to detect threatening language in turkish tweets and instagram content moreover one study used knearest neighbors knn on the dataset of tweets and instagram posts or comments written in turkish language recent studies also explored deep learning based models for the detection of threatening language for example convolutional neural networks cnns were used to classify potentially threatening content posted online on twitter and facebook in german and english tweets these studies showed that cnns outperform other neural networks based models moreover a set of studies on detecting threatening language utilized recurrent neural networks rnn and lstm models to classify threatening content on facebook in bengali language bilstm and graph convolutional network were applied on english twitter content a few researchers explored threatening language detection in languages including english italian german turkish bengali japanese danish indonesian arabic portuguese and spanish no prior work has been conducted for the urdu language to the best of our knowledge

interaction design ixd patterns oneclick purchase to speed up the buying process swiftshop let registered users buy with just one click personalized recommendations swiftshop used machine learning algorithms to suggest products that customers might like based on what they’ve bought before results after making these changes swiftshop saw great results more people buying with the new features more people ended up buying products from swiftshop happier shoppers customers spent more time on the website which meant they liked shopping there more more repeat customers because it was easier to shop people kept coming back to swiftshop again and again so by using smart design tricks like breadcrumb navigation progress indicators card sorting faceted search oneclick purchase and personalized recommendations swiftshop made their online store a better place to shop two heuristics are inherent to the design of business solutions and the creation of business solution architectures these heuristics are known as layering and componentization and can be thought of as design approaches followed with an intent of architectural concerns componentization has already been introduced as a computational thinking and adaptive design reuse technique layering in business solution architecture involves creating distinct layers that abstract specific aspects of the overall architecture this heuristic enables the separation of concerns between layers and improves modularity each layer comprises components and a layer may consist of multiple components this hierarchical structure helps organize and separate different functionalities or responsibilities within the architecture making it easier to manage and understand the layering strategy is based on the concept of separating different concerns this method structures software design into distinct stacked layers with each layer tasked with specific functions commonly business solution architectures are organized into three main layers the presentation business logic and data management layers the presentation layer is the user’s touchpoint handling the user interface ui and delivering the user experience ux which is the overall experience that a person has when interacting with a product service or system the business logic layer holds the business logic for the business solution or application separating the ui ux from the businesscentric computations this separation affords the flexibility to adjust business operations as needs evolve without disrupting other system components although not tied to a specific domain the data management layer is responsible for interacting with persistent storage systems like databases and various data processing mechanisms

other version control systems support similar capabilities but due to its popularity i concentrate on git specifically in this report project management tools have also grown in popularity especially in support of agile software development frameworks agile emphasizes deliver working software frequently from a couple of weeks to a couple of months with a preference to the shorter timescale and promotes sustaining a constant pace of development to plan and track progress of development in short intervals software engineering teams often adopt kanban boards kanban boards keep track of tasks with columns representing their statuses to do in progress or done kanban boards are specifically integrated into some agile frameworks and built into online tools including github the popular git repository host while kanban boards generally dedicate a card to a particular task that needs to be completed some agile frameworks advocate for cards to be written as user stories user stories describe software features from the perspective of the customer or enduser so that it explains a goal they want to achieve stories usually follow the format as a i want to so that teams prioritize and plan which stories should be completed during an iteration in order to deliver the most value to the customer for the least effort the team also assigns points to represent how difficult it will be to develop relative to other stories these points can be used to monitor pace of development as stories are implemented and completed they are moved along the kanban board to indicate their progress github projects supports creation of kanban boards dedicated to a project’s repository in particular it provides features to assign developers to specific cards and story points can be indicated on the card’s description or applied as a label additionally github projects provide automation so that when a code commit satisfies the requirements for a user story the commit message can use keywords tenses of fix resolve and close paired with the card number to automatically move the corresponding user story card to the done column on the automated kanban board with user story and git log acknowledgments and disclaimer this research was partially supported by the national science foundation under grants due and due the opinions findings conclusions and recommendations expressed in this material are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the views of the national science foundation abstract while extensive research has established the superiority of active learning pedagogies over traditional passive lectures further investigation is required to delineate the tradeoffs among various active learning approaches this study examines these differences within the context of a computer architecture course at midwestern university which historically utilized active lectures supplemented by clicker questions in fall n short video lectures were introduced as supplemental resources subsequently in spring the course was flipped mandating that students view video lectures and complete preparatory assignments prior to class attendance two concurrent sections of the flipped course were implemented utilizing identical homework machine problems and examinations but differing in their in class pedagogical strategies the first section sppi n employed peer instruction while the second spcp n utilized collaborative problem solving wherein students were organized into teams of three data collection included student surveys assessing perceptions of time investment course difficulty stress levels and sense of belonging by comparing midterm examination performance and non cognitive outcomes this study evaluates the relative efficacy of these pedagogies the results indicate that both flipped models yielded benefits superior to the traditional active lecture format specifically peer instruction enhanced learning efficiency whereas collaborative problem solving fostered greater social support and eliminated gender based grade disparities keywords applied computing education collaborative learning computer assisted instruction e learning course design publication details in the st acm technical symposium on computer science education sigcse march – portland or usa publisher acm new york ny usa pages doi introduction research in stem and computing disciplines has consistently demonstrated that active learning pedagogies yield superior learning outcomes compared to traditional passive lecturing active learning is broadly defined as instructional methods that require students to actively construct their understanding of content rather than passively receiving information in their seminal meta analysis freeman et al argue that the methodology and contributions this study employs the sampling and occlusion soc algorithm a model agnostic post hoc feature importance estimation technique jin et al to address the problem of bias in hate speech detection the methodology is applied to two distinct datasets the gab hate corpus which is annotated for hate based rhetoric kennedy et al and an annotated corpus derived from the stormfront white supremacist online forum de gibert et al analysis of the soc generated explanations revealed a significant model bias toward group identifiers to mitigate this we propose a novel regularization based approach designed to enhance model sensitivity to the contextual features surrounding these identifiers by applying regularization during the training phase to the explanation derived importance scores of group identifiers the model is constrained to prioritize contextual information over mere token presence empirical results indicate that this regularization strategy effectively reduces reliance on group identifiers while increasing the weight assigned to more generalizable indicators of hate speech such as dehumanizing and insulting language furthermore evaluation on an out of domain test set comprising news articles containing group identifiers—heuristically classified as non hate speech—demonstrates a substantial reduction in the false positive rate concurrently in domain out of sample classification performance is either maintained or improved related work the conceptual framework of this research is grounded in the work of warner and hirschberg who defined hate speech detection as the task of disambiguating offensive terminology within abusive versus non abusive contexts subsequent research has expanded upon this foundation by applying supervised models to a broad typology of hate speech waseem et al utilizing either manually annotated datasets e g waseem and hovy de gibert et al or heuristically labeled data wulczyn et al olteanu et al however these approaches are frequently limited by the highly skewed linguistic distributions inherent in such datasets wiegand et al additionally this work draws upon literature concerning bias in classification models dixon et al investigated and mitigated bias in toxicity classifiers regarding social groups specifically addressing the issue of false positives on innocuous sentences containing demographic tokens similarly mostafazadeh davani et al observed that annotator biases toward specific social groups can be amplified during the classifier training process

performance evaluation and ablation analysis the experimental results substantiate the efficacy of incorporating the intensity function demonstrating a minimum improvement of in accuracy and a reduction of in mean squared error mse relative to vanilla recurrent neural networks rnn and their variants the ablation study indicates that the removal of the neighborhood context component yields the most significant performance degradation among all tested components thereby confirming the critical role of social influence in opinion prediction furthermore the exclusion of either the variational autoencoder vae for topic extraction or the intensity function reverting to a gru only architecture results in marginal declines in stance prediction accuracy accompanied by more pronounced performance gaps in temporal prediction tasks notably the integration of graph convolutional networks gcns to model higher order social influence did not yield performance gains this outcome is likely attributable to the introduction of extraneous noise within the model architecture visualization of topical attention to evaluate the effectiveness of context attention mechanisms driven by topic queries representative topics were selected from the topic word matrix generated by the vae topic labels were manually assigned based on the top ten associated words for each topic subsequently the topic distribution of a target tweet was analyzed alongside the distributions of its neighboring tweets with attention weights assigned to three specific neighborhood tweets visualized for comparative analysis figure illustrates the selected example topics the corresponding topic distributions and the attention signals directed toward context tweets in this visualization x and x denote the user s second and fourth tweets respectively while d d and d represent the three most recent neighborhood tweets the leftmost column displays attention weights in blue while the rows above t t and t depict the topic distributions the analysis reveals a shift in the user s topical focus from immigration to boris johnson over two time steps a trend that is also reflected in the neighboring tweets crucially higher attention weights are consistently assigned to neighborhood tweets exhibiting topical distributions similar to those of the user these observations suggest that the topic vector effectively facilitates the selection of the most relevant neighborhood context
