in twelve states the proportion of black students participating in ap computer science ap cs examinations exceeded their overall representation across all ap exams this figure also surpassed the representation of hispanic students within the broader ap examinee population and the general high school student demographic however this aggregate trend was significantly influenced by a cohort of hispanic ap cs examinees in florida while the representation of hispanic students on ap cs exams was lower than their participation rate across all ap exams it remained higher than their prevalence in the general high school population this discrepancy may indicate the efficacy of state specific initiatives between and the representation of black hispanic and female students demonstrated a statistically significant upward trend p for each group it is important to note that the line of best fit utilized in this analysis measures representation defined as the percentage of ap cs examinees from a specific group divided by the percentage of high school students from that same group consequently this metric isolates increases in participation attributable to factors beyond mere demographic shifts within the state the rate of growth as indicated by the slope of the line of best fit was most pronounced for hispanic students followed by female students and black students specifically each passing year was associated with a increase in the representation metric for black students conversely asian examinees consistently participated in both general ap and ap cs examinations at rates exceeding their proportion within the general high school student population

for example the abstraction of string data enables programmers to write programs that operate on humanreadable letters digits punctuation or spaces without having to determine how to delve into each of these concepts programming languages allow us to define abstractions for representing ideas in a computer the study of data structures and algorithms focuses on identifying what is known as a canonical algorithm a wellknown algorithm that showcases design principles helpful across a wide variety of problems in this chapter rather than focusing on the programming details we will instead focus on algorithms and the ideas behind them for many realworld problems the ability to design an algorithm depends on how the data is represented a data structure is a complex data type with two equally important parts a specific representation or way of organizing a collection of more than one element which is an individual value or data point and a specific functionality or operations such as adding retrieving and removing elements in our previous example a string is a data structure for representing sentences as a sequence of characters it has specific functionality such as character insertion or deletion string concatenation and string comparison although the values for complex data are often diverse computer scientists have designed data structures so that they can be reused for other problems for example rather than designing a specialized data structure for sentences in every human language we often use a single universal string data structure to represent sentences including characters from different languages in the same sentence we will later see some drawbacks of generalizing assumptions in the design of data structures and algorithms in addition computers take time to execute algorithms so computer scientists are concerned about efficiency in terms of how long an algorithm takes to compute a result among the different types of universal data structures computer scientists have found it helpful to categorize data structures according to their functionality without considering their specific representation an abstract data type adt consists of all data structures that share common functionality but differ in specific representation common abstract data types for complex data follow and list and set types are shown in we will discuss each abstract data type in more detail together with their data structure implementations

generated responses a metric that primarily reflects the marginal entropy of the output distribution however for dialogue systems diversity conditioned on the query i e conditional entropy is of greater significance conversely measuring diversity based on responses randomly sampled from a model given a single query often results in low response quality caccia et al to address this the current conditional response entropy it is imperative to constrain the diversity of the augmented queries to ensure they remain semantically proximate to the original query consequently the objective of controlled augmentation is to minimize variance in both semantic meaning and lexical usage while avoiding the submission of identical inputs to the dialogue model to achieve this two augmentation strategies are employed wordnet substitution ws and conditional text generation ctg wordnet substitution ws is a lexical level manipulation technique that replaces specific tokens with synonyms defined in the wordnet database miller

similarity measures such as nmi can be computed on any contingency table obtained from these methods an advantage of the text overlap approach is that it is fast because it only involves text processing and a few counts the downside is that an identical dataset can only be identified with similarity if each word always has the same label another issue is that only a fraction of each dataset is used illustrating examples are provided in appendix a for the actual comparison as the plain text overlap approach does not consider the ratio of shared vocabulary it is possible to have a “false positive” i e a high similarity is reported for two datasets although they share only one word to fix this we combine the nmi value and the ratio of shared vocabulary sv via the harmonic mean into our text overlap to measure to · nmi · sv nmi sv with the shared vocabulary sv v ∩ v ′ v ∪ v ′ where v and v ′ are the sets of all unique words in the two datasets d and d ′ when constructing the contingency table e g table with the text overlap approach the sequence information of labelword pairs i e the context cannot be captured in the counts with the usage of contextual embeddings this issue can be mitigated sufficiently vector space similarity word embeddings allow representing words in the form of dense vectors within a vector space instead of a specific character sequence in the language’s vocabulary thus it is possible to perform mathematical operations on these vectors and compute e g the semantic similarity of two words by computing their cosine similarity within the vector space these word vector techniques can be used to tackle the problems of the previously shown text overlap approach a first extension allows incorporating words not occurring in both datasets vector representations are obtained for each unique word in the datasets instead of ignoring words contained only in one dataset the closest word from the other dataset is chosen via cosine similarity for the pairwise label comparison the remaining process and similarity measure computation stays the same in the vector space approach all tokens are compared

students are typically tasked with investigating correlations between variables such as academic performance and regional income levels or between educational provision and demographic profiles within the united kingdom the final submission consists of a single microsoft word document adhering to standard industry practices with a strict word limit of words accompanying technical work is submitted as a jupyter notebook in an appendix this component serves solely for validation purposes and does not contribute to the final grade the final report is evaluated based on five core criteria understanding of relevant legal issues identification of practical research questions derived from the data and their subsequent resolution appropriate preparation and storage of data application of suitable analytical techniques and the overall quality of the final report assessment for each criterion evaluates both the technical execution of the task and the student s capacity for critical reflection regarding their methodological decisions for instance while the technical ability to clean a dataset e g utilizing python to address missing values is assessed equal weight is placed on the student s ability to independently articulate the potential impact of their chosen cleaning strategies on the final analysis since the inaugural presentation of module tm the assessment rubric has been made available to all students to ensure transparency regarding grading criteria and their interpretation despite this early cohorts demonstrated significant challenges in producing high quality in contrast sequence level training utilizing reinforcement learning rl offers a robust solution to this challenge by framing text generation as a sequential decision making problem by directly optimizing evaluation metrics as cumulative rewards ranzato et al this approach has yielded state of the art results across various text generation tasks paulus et al rennie et al however this framework faces significant limitations primarily rl training suffers from sparse rewards as scalar feedback is typically available only upon the completion of an entire sequence furthermore recurrent models often prioritize local fluency which can lead to semantic inconsistencies in long form generation regarding implementation most existing rl based text generation methods rely on model free frameworks which have been criticized for high variance and poor sample efficiency sutton and barto conversely while model based rl methods mitigate these specific issues they are often difficult to train in complex environments and the learned policy is frequently constrained by the capacity of the environment model recent advancements in model based rl gu et al kurutach et al nagabandi et al have sought to combine the strengths of both paradigms achieving improved performance by learning a model free policy assisted by an environment model additionally model based rl has recently been applied to address problems characterized by extremely sparse rewards through curiosity driven methods pathak et al in this paper we propose a model based imitation learning method designed to overcome the aforementioned challenges in text generation tasks our core approach involves employing an explicit guider network to model the generation environment within the feature space of sentence tokens this network emits intermediate rewards by aligning predicted features with those of generated sentences trained to encode the global structural information of training sentences the guider network effectively guides next token prediction during the generative process to further support the guider network we introduce a novel self attention mechanism designed to provide high level forward looking planning information and ensure consistent semantic meaning throughout the generated text

to further prove the effectiveness of the proposed normbased sentence weight we explore the model integrating normbased sentence weight with competencebased cl and find that it can also strike the right balance between translating simple and mediumdifficulty sentences a case study table shows an example of a translation of a difficult sentence consisting of several similar clauses in the normbased difficulty bucket we observe that the translation by the vanilla model omits translating the last clause but nmt with normbased cl translates the entire sentence the proposed method enhances the representation learning of nmt leading to better understandings of difficult sentences thus yielding better translations related work the norm of a word embedding has been sufficiently validated to be highly correlated with word frequency schakel and wilson and wilson and schakel train a simple word embedding model on a monolingual corpus and find that the norm of a word vector is relevant to the frequency of the word and its context sensitivity frequent words and words that are insensitive to context will have word vectors of low norm values for language generation tasks especially nmt there is still a correlation between word embedding and word frequency gong et al observe that the word embedding of nmt contains too much frequency information considering two frequent and rare words that have a similar lexical meaning to be far from each other in terms of vector distance gao et al regard this issue as a representation degeneration issue that it is hard to learn expressive representations of rare words due to the bias in the objective function nguyen and chiang observe a similar issue during nmt decoding given two word candidates with similar lexical meanings nmt chooses the more frequent one as the final translation they attribute this to the norm of word vector and find that target words with different frequencies have different norms which affects the nmt score function in the present paper for the sake of obtaining an easy and simple word vector norm requirement we use the norm derived from a simple word embedding model in the future we would like to test norms of various sorts

instead of trying to align two different vector spaces we propose to work directly in the shared vocabulary space we take the neighbors of a word in a vector space to reflect its usage and consider words that have drastically different neighbours in the spaces induced by the different corpora to be words subjected to usage change the intuition behind this approach is that words that are used significantly differently across corpora are expected to have different contexts and thus to have only few neighboring words in common in order to determine the extent of the usage change of a word we simply consider its topk neighbors in each of the two corpora and compute the size of the intersection of the two lists the smaller the intersection is the bigger we expect the change to be the words are ranked accordingly the advantages of our method are the following simplicity the method is extremely simple to implement and apply with no need for space alignment hyperparameter tuning and vocabulary filtering except for simple frequency cutoffs stability our method is stable outputting similar results across different word embeddings trained on the same corpora in contrast to the alignmentbased approach interpretability the ranking produced by our method is very intuitive to analyze looking at the neighborhood of a word in the two corpora reveals both the meaning of the word in each and the extent to which the word has changed locality the interpretability aspect is closely linked to the locality of the decision in our approach the score of each word is determined only by its own neighbours in each of the spaces in contrast in the projection based method the similarity of a pair of words after the projection depends on the projection process which implicitly takes into account all the other words in both spaces and their relations to each other as well as the projection lexicon itself and the projection algorithm this makes the algorithmic predictions of the projectionbased methods opaque and practically impossible to reason about we demonstrate the applicability and robustness of the proposed method by performing a series of experiments in which we use it to identify word usage changes in a variety of corpus pairs reflecting different data division criteria

course overview and instructional summary this session addressed the course objectives regarding two dimensional spatial concepts specifically contrasting standard cartesian grids with screen space coordinates to reinforce these concepts students engaged in an interactive exercise plotting points within both coordinate systems the curriculum then transitioned to the project scope a two dimensional game wherein a protagonist traverses the screen to collect descending objects assignment conceptual design students were tasked with designing the primary protagonist for their game adhering to specific constraints regarding the quantity and geometry of shapes utilized participants utilized a provided grid to sketch and label their designs following this conceptual phase the instruction shifted to the implementation of these designs using the processing programming environment the lecture covered fundamental commands related to screen dimensions rgb color models and geometric primitives drawing explicit parallels between these coding operations and traditional visual art techniques a key pedagogical focus was the significance of command sequencing the instruction highlighted that analogous to physical painting where color selection precedes application programming requires the color command to be executed prior to shape rendering furthermore the lesson demonstrated how the order of shape commands dictates the layering of compound figures to facilitate coding in an offline environment students were provided with a reference worksheet containing essential commands assignment implementation students were required to translate their individual hero designs into executable code lesson functions and animation subsequent instruction focused on the architecture of functions detailing the built in setup and draw loops essential for two dimensional animation in processing alongside the creation of custom functions for specific tasks preparatory measures and efficiency to optimize instructional time and ensure uniform progress the instructor pre completed the majority of the hero drawing code initiated in the previous lesson leaving only the final commands for student completion additionally a foundational code structure containing the necessary animation loops was provided with the hero code integrated into the draw function this preparatory strategy served a dual purpose it allowed for the efficient introduction of function concepts at the outset of the lesson and provided students with the opportunity to practice reading and analyzing code authored by others finally a supplementary example program was developed to demonstrate the use of three distinct functions for rendering varied background scenes

abstract the rapid expansion of computer science enrollments has intensified the challenge of maintaining academic integrity in out of class assignments current academic misconduct policies exhibit significant variability across institutions courses and individual instructors furthermore there is a notable lack of consensus regarding the definition of academic misconduct among students faculty and industry professionals these policies are often difficult to interpret and enforce consuming considerable administrative resources while potentially fostering a learning environment characterized by a deficit of trust and respect one proposed strategy to mitigate academic misconduct is the integration of frequent in class assessments such as laboratory exams or quizzes however faculty often perceive this approach as creating a passive testing environment that encroaches upon valuable instructional time conversely students may view such assessments as excessive stressful and indicative of the competitive unwelcoming atmosphere frequently associated with stem disciplines consequently the design of in class assessments must directly address these concerns to ensure their efficacy and acceptance this paper presents the findings of a study focused on optimizing the design and facilitation of frequent in class assessments the proposed model features sequential thirty minute assessments administered at the conclusion of laboratory sessions the primary objective was to transform these assessments into active learning experiences thereby enhancing their perceived value reducing student anxiety and optimizing the utilization of class time the study evaluated student performance on these newly designed active assessments and identified the formats students found most beneficial to facilitate active learning the assessments incorporated peer partnering two distinct partnership models were examined collaborative partnerships modeled after traditional pair programming a widely recognized and effective strategy for out of class programming tasks cooperative partnerships a hybrid approach wherein students collaborate during the assessment but submit and are evaluated on individual work this model serves as an intermediate structure bridging the gap between solitary and fully partnered work the results provide insights into the efficacy of these active assessment strategies in balancing academic integrity with a supportive and engaging learning environment

in theory pos tagging errors increase the further an author’s demographic attributes differ from the average wsj author of the s and s on whom many pos taggers were trained – a selection bias discussed next work by sap et al shows error disparity from a different origin namely unfairness in hate speech detection they find that annotators for hate speech on social media make more mistakes on posts of black individuals contrary to the case above the disparity is not necessarily due to a difference between author and annotator population a selection bias instead the label disparity stems from annotators failing to account for the authors’ racial background and sociolinguistic norms source and target populations an important assumption of our framework is that disparities are dependent on the population for which the model will be applied this assumption is reflected in distinguishing a separate “target population” from the “source population” on which the model was trained in crossvalidation over random folds models are trained and tested over the same population however in practice models are often applied to novel data that may originate from a different population of people in other words the disparity may exist as a model property for one application but not for another quantifying disparity given the definitions of the two types of disparities we can quantify bias with wellestablished measures of distributional divergence or deviance specifically we suggest the loglikelihood ratio as a central metric d y ˆ y a log p y a − log p ˆ y a where p y a is the specified ideal distribution either derived empirically or theoretically and p ˆ y a is the distribution within the data for error disparity the ideal distribution is always the uniform and ˆ y is replaced with the error kl divergence d kl can be used as a secondary more scalable alternative our measure above attempts to synthesize metrics others have used in works focused on specific biases for example the definition of outcome disparity is analogous to that used for semantic bias

one this problemdomain is more difficult to solve than uo as the distinction between dialogues is in the position of only one utterance utterance replacement ur we randomly replace one of the utterances in a dialogue with another utterance that is also randomly selected from another dialogue the original dialogue is preferred over the dialogue generated by ur unlike the other problemdomains which perturb the structure of a dialogue this problemdomain perturbs the coherence of a dialogue at its semantic level even utterance ordering euo this problemdomain is similar to uo but here we rearrange the order of utterances that are said by one speaker and keep the order of the other utterances which are said by the other speaker fixed therefore euo is more challenging and dialoguerelevant than uo this problemdomain assesses to what extent coherence models capture the coherence among utterances that are said by one of the speakers in a dialogue problemdomain datasets to create dialogue pairs for each problemdomain we use the splits provided by the dailydialog corpus and for switchboard we take of dialogues for the training for the validation and for the test sets following cervone et al for any dialogue in each set we create perturbations where each of which makes two pairs with the original dialogue given dialogue dial i and its perturbation dial j we define two dialogue pairs dial i dial j with preference coherence label l c and dial j dial i with label l c in problemdomain evaluation in this evaluation we train finetune and evaluate our models on the the chance accuracy level is established at at first glance this finding appears to contradict the results reported by östling and tiedemann who observed lower perplexity in language models trained on a small set of highly similar languages e g danish swedish and norwegian compared to models trained on a single language from that group however their study also noted that perplexity increased significantly when models were trained on a larger number of languages or on languages with lower mutual similarity a condition analogous to the present study bert and mbert table presents the accuracy scores of mbert across all stimuli performance on the clams dataset was generally high for languages utilizing the latin script english french and german specifically for english accuracy remained consistently high across all conditions ranging from to for sentences containing relative clauses and from to for remaining conditions german also demonstrated high accuracy exceeding for all constructions except the across subject relative clause where the score was french accuracy exhibited greater variability while high for most conditions it was notably lower for the across subject relative clause and across prepositional phrase constructions in all latin script languages accuracy on the across object relative clause condition was substantially higher than that observed in our lstm models however these results are not directly comparable for two primary reasons first consistent with goldberg we excluded examples where the focus verbs were absent from mbert s vocabulary such exclusions occurred frequently see appendix d for statistical details second and perhaps more significantly unlike the unidirectional lstm language models mbert utilizes bidirectional context in across object relative clause sentences e g the farmers that the lawyer likes smile smiles the terminal punctuation may signal to the bidirectional model that the preceding verb belongs to the main clause rather than the relative clause thereby necessitating agreement with the subject of the main clause farmers rather than the subject of the relative clause lawyer in contrast to the latin script languages mbert demonstrated noticeably lower accuracy for hebrew and russian even on simple agreement cases which present no syntactic complexity this surprisingly poor syntactic performance in these languages may be attributed to the fact that mbert employs a shared vocabulary of tokens across all languages combined with a training corpus in which a substantial proportion of the data is written in the latin script

text structure previewing helps students set goals for reading and activates prior knowledge when reading example code containing a new concept students might scan the code to quickly identify familiar and unfamiliar concepts they could think about their prior knowledge of the concepts predict how the new concept might work and inspect the syntax of the new concept text structure prepares students to recognize disciplinaryspecific text structures and use this knowledge to plan for reading and guide comprehension in cs programming languages and environments have specific structures that students must be able to discover to comprehend code and must be able to differentiate as they learn new languages and environments we drew from these reading comprehension strategies in designing tipp see which will be discussed in more depth in the next section tipp see the strategy is specifically designed for use with scratch a popular programming language and development environment used in elementary schools inspired by previewing strategies the first half tipp guides students in previewing different aspects of a new scratch project before looking at any code as a last step they run the code with very deliberate observations of the events and actions that occur the second half see draws from text structure strategies see provides a roadmap for finding code in the scratch interface clicking on the sprite and finding the event and proceduralizes the process by which they can learn how code works by methodical exploration deliberate tinkering fifteen teachers were recruited from a large urban school district and underwent the same professional development to teach the scratch act curriculum to th grade students ages a total of classrooms participated in the study including six of bilingual classrooms teachers were randomly assigned to either the tipp see or the control condition resulting in five englishonly and three bilingual classrooms in each condition

this warrants a sentence representation that reflects the sentiment of the given aspect to achieve this we first concatenate aspect to all the words in the sentence in order to propagate the context information within the sentence we feed the modified sentence to a gated recurrent unit gru to amplify the sentimentally relevant words to the aspect we employ an attention layer to obtain the aspectaware sentence representation we feed the aspectaware sentence representations to a gru to propagate aspect information among the aspectaware sentence representations this partially helps to model the dependency among aspects in a sentence after this in order to further interaspect dependency modeling we employ memory networks where the targetaspect representation is supplied as the query the target aspect representation is transformed into internal query state with a fully connected layer input memory representation all the aspects in the sentence are stored in memory each aspect is represented by their corresponding aspectaware sentence representation an attention mechanism is used to read these memories we compute the match between the query and the memory slots with inner product where the attention score measures the relatedness between the target aspect and each aspect output memory representation we choose the output memory vectors to be a refined version of the input memory vectors obtained by applying a gru on the input memory the response vector is obtained by summing output vectors weighted by the relatedness measures final classification single hop in the case of single hop target aspect representation is added with memory output to generate refined target aspect representative this sum is passed to a softmax classifier to produce the estimated sentiment polarity negative positive or neutral multiple hops we use a total number of hops in our model each hop generates a finer aspect representation after all hops the final targetaspectaware sentence representation vector is used for the final classification to produce the estimated sentiment polarity negative positive or neutral we train the network for epochs using categorical cross entropy with lregularizer as loss function where the regularization weight is set to to the power of negative as optimization algorithm stochastic gradient descent sgd based adam algorithm is used with learningrate due to its parameterwise adaptive learning scheme we employed gridsearch to obtain the best hyperparameter values we evaluate our model with semeval absa dataset

students acquire practical hands on experience by applying their cumulative skill set within the data science capstone project in this course learners are tasked with analyzing data to support the business capstone cohort distinct from programs at technical universities this curriculum does not include engineering courses focused on cloud based big data platforms or specialized hardware as such offerings necessitate dedicated engineering staff support furthermore the program minimizes instruction on vendor specific tools prioritizing instead general purpose programming languages such as python and mysql this pedagogical design was intentionally implemented to cultivate versatile academic concepts and competencies that remain applicable across diverse platform environments the major s learning objectives reflect the interdisciplinary nature of the data science profession and include the following formulating effective mathematical solutions to analytical problems developing effective solutions to computing challenges within analytical projects organizing and managing datasets effectively for analytical purposes critically analyzing problems to identify appropriate analytical solutions communicating analytical problems methodologies and findings effectively through oral visual and written mediums critically evaluating ethical privacy and security challenges inherent in data analytics each course integrates at least two of these learning objectives success in the data science field requires a synthesis of technical analytical and communication skills all of which are rigorously practiced through projects and presentations notably ethical considerations and privacy concerns are interwoven into discussions and assignments across the entire curriculum this section highlights several required courses detailing the integration of engagement capacity and continuity into their structures alongside a report on student evaluations of both the courses and the major the introduction to data science course serves as the foundational experience in the field offered twice with an enrollment of eight students per semester the course targets students who have completed programming prerequisites but have not yet applied these skills specifically to data science the curriculum focuses on the comprehensive data science lifecycle data collection organization analysis machine learning and the visualization and communication of results students are introduced to algorithms for downloading iterating parsing cleaning summarizing graphing and analyzing data while the course provides practical intuition regarding the broad categories of machine learning techniques to aid in algorithm selection it does not yet cover the algorithmic implementation of these models instruction utilizes python pandas jupyter notebooks and scikit learn to ensure the material is both approachable and engaging the course is structured in two parts with the first half dedicated to covering all stages of the data science process

annotator selection and data quality following the methodology established by gretz et al the annotation task was restricted to a pre selected cohort of trusted annotators who had demonstrated high performance in prior projects conducted by our team the annotation process was conducted independently for each argument annotators were permitted to associate multiple key points to evaluate inter annotator agreement we analyzed argument key point pairs using a binary label indicating whether a match was established the resulting fleiss kappa fleiss was while the average pairwise cohen s kappa landis and koch was these metrics indicate moderate agreement a level consistent with findings in previous computational argumentation studies boltužić and šnajder ein dor et al furthermore the accuracy of the stance selection component was reflecting high overall annotation quality data cleansing and filtering to ensure dataset integrity specific annotations were excluded based on the following criteria instances where the stance selection response was incorrect instances containing illegal key point selections specifically where the none option was selected concurrently with one or more specific key points the error rates for these categories were low remaining below and respectively across all annotators notably unlike the approach taken by toledo et al performance analysis was conducted post task and annotators were not informed of their success or failure during the process following the application of these quality control measures any argument with fewer than seven valid judgments was removed from the dataset consequently the final dataset comprises labeled arguments consolidation of annotation results individual annotations were aggregated using the following consensus rule an argument a is considered mapped to a key point k if at least of the annotators assigned that mapping it is noted that a single argument may be mapped to multiple key points under this threshold

for the proposed strategy it is necessary to download the posts xml file that corresponds to a gb until this year dataset regarding all questions and answers in english ever made on stackoverflow since its creation posts xml file is an xml file where each line corresponds to either a question or an answer identified by the posttypeid attribute each record contains the following data about a question creation date score view count question text question title last edition date tags used number of replies number of comments number of favorites and closing date of the question when the question were flagged as resolved the stackoverflow provides questions and answer logs in portuguese spanish japanese russian and english the english dataset has approximately questions and answers the posts xml file must undergo several cleaning and formatting processes so it can be used for topic modelling for our purposes the most important piece of information is the text of asked questions to get the text of questions it is necessary to separate all the records from posts xml into two other datasets questions dataset those questions with attribute posttypeid ‘’ and answers dataset those questions with attribute posttypeid ‘’ due to the size of posts xml file the separation process was performed using a python script capable of generating two other files questions xml and answers xml for our approach only the questions xml file is used for analysis from questions xml it is necessary to extract the excerpt in natural language corresponding to questions and title all other xml attributes were discarded it was observed that questions usually have code snippets in it in several different programming languages html tags and words that do not contribute to the semantics of a question such as articles and prepositions grammar the first level of datacleaning is performed to remove html tags email links newline characters single and double quotes accents though rare in english language and special characters this process results in a txt file where each line corresponds to a question and its title we define a second level of datacleaning where each word of each line in the txt file is lemmatized and stopwords are removed one more time after lemmatization the lemmatization is the process of replacing a word by its lemma which removes inflectional endings

particularly when utilizing crowdsourced annotations for complex natural language processing nlp tasks such as relation extraction re analysis of model errors following the dataset revision this study addresses two primary research questions which residual errors are attributable to the models and what are the underlying causes of these misclassifications to investigate these issues we first established an annotation protocol directing linguists to identify potential causes for model errors section subsequently we categorized and analyzed these causes to formulate testable hypotheses capable of automatic verification section for the automated analysis phase we implemented a baseline model alongside three state of the art architectures section misclassification annotation the primary objective of the annotation process is to identify linguistic factors contributing to incorrect model predictions we initiated a manual exploratory analysis of the revised control and challenging test instances that were misclassified by the majority of the models beginning with individual observations we iteratively developed a categorical framework based on the presence or absence of contextual and entity specific features likely to mislead models e g entity type errors or distracting phrases following this exploration a final set of categories and corresponding guidelines was defined two annotators were then instructed to assign the most relevant error category to each misclassified instance within the revised test subset in cases where multiple categories applied the most pertinent one was selected consistent with the validation phase any discrepancies between the two annotators were resolved by a third expert error hypotheses formulation and adversarial rewriting in the subsequent phase the misclassification categories were expanded into testable hypotheses or groups verifiable across the entire dataset split for instance if a model is suspected of being distracted by an entity in the context that shares the same type as one of the relation arguments a hypothesis group labeled has distractor is formulated

figure illustrates the system architecture what was written we describe the features that we used to model the content generated by the news media analyzing both the articles they publish on their website as well as relevant activity on social media articles on the news medium website given a target news medium we first collect a number of articles it has published then we extract various types of features from the text of these articles below we describe these features in more detail linguistic features these features focus on language use and they model text structure topic sentiment subjectivity complexity bias and morality they have proved useful for detecting fake articles as well as for predicting the political bias and the factuality of reporting of news media we extracted such features using the news landscape nela toolkit and we will refer to them as the nela features in the rest of this paper we averaged the nela features for the individual articles in order to obtain a nela representation for a news medium using arithmetic averaging is a good idea as it captures the general trend of articles in a medium while limiting the impact of outliers for instance if a medium is known to align with leftwing ideology this should not change if it published a few articles that align with rightwing ideology we use this method to aggregate all features that we collected at a level of granularity that is finer than the mediumlevel embedding features we encoded each article using bert by feeding the first wordpieces from the article and then averaging the word representations extracted from the secondtolast layer in order to obtain representations that are relevant to our tasks we finetuned bert by training a softmax layer on top of the output vector to predict the label bias or factuality of news articles that are scrapped from an external list of media to avoid overfitting the articles’ labels are assumed to be the same as those of the media in which they are published a form of distant supervision

papert’s ideas have been expanded beyond the realm of mathematics and physics to areas such as the social sciences where interactive data visualization can help students identify interesting correlations and patterns that precipitated social change and turning points in history while also learning new data fluencies and the limits of databased approaches yet despite these roots in aspirations for computers as a medium for learning anything and everything the study of computer science education emerged in the s as a field narrowly concerned with producing more effective software engineers highereducation computer science faculty motivated by the demand for b naimipour m guzdial and t shreiner helping social studies teachers to design learning experiences around data participatory design for new teachercentric programming languages in proceedings of the acm conference on international computing education research icer association for computing machinery new york ny usa doi software engineers designed their computer science curricula to teach the concepts that early computer companies such as ibm desperately needed these courses had an emphasis on efficiency performance and scalability because a university computer science education was only intended to produce software engineers we live with the consequences of this design even today the structure of this textbook inherits the borders between concepts originally imagined in the s when university computer science education was only intended to prepare students for software development jobs we now know that there are many more roles for computer scientists to play in society—not only software engineers but also data analysts product managers entrepreneurs political advisors or politicians environmental engineers social activists and scientists across every field from accounting to zoology although the role of computers expanded with the introduction of the internet in the late s papert’s vision for computation as a learning medium has been challenging to implement at least partly because of funding constraints but as computers evolve primary and secondary education in the united states is striving for ways to help teachers use computers to more effectively teach all things—not just computers for their own sake but using computers to learn everything our histories so far have centered the interests of white american men in computer science but there are also countless untold marginalized histories of people of other backgrounds races ethnicities and genders in computing

on the inaugural day of the summer camp instructors facilitated a minute tutorial on the snap programming environment this session aimed to familiarize participants with the interface and guide them through their initial assignment draw square following the tutorial students engaged in two of the six crescendo challenges—specifically loops and conditionals —as a preparatory exercise subsequently participants completed two open ended laboratory exercises within the standard snap environment daisy design which emphasized loop structures and guessing game which focused on conditional logic and foundational loop concepts the day concluded with the completion of the remaining four crescendo challenges loops conditionals loops and conditionals observations indicated that students remained highly engaged and productive throughout the session effectively applying newly acquired concepts although fatigue was evident by the conclusion of the programming activities in a parallel context a cohort of undergraduate non major students from the host university completed the cs crescendo homework assignment this course introduces fundamental programming concepts in snap by integrating the beauty and joy of computing curriculum unlike the camp participants these students received only a minute introductory tutorial prior to utilizing the tool although the instructor was the designer of the crescendo system the pedagogical approach for this group involved providing an instruction sheet and assigning the tasks for independent completion outside of class hours for the cs component the instructor curated a specific subset of challenges focused exclusively on the concept of loops two required tasks loops and loops and one optional task loops given that these activities were conducted asynchronously data collection included students programming logs as well as likert scale ratings assessing the system s difficulty helpfulness and engagement levels to evaluate whether crescendo successfully achieved its objective of providing a scaffolded and engaging learning experience this report presents quantitative findings derived from system log data alongside qualitative observations from the summer camp deployment and rating data from the cs homework a primary design goal of crescendo was to establish a scaffolded progression of tasks that are rapidly completable yet incrementally increase in difficulty under this hypothesis it is expected that students would allocate a relatively consistent and brief duration to each task to test this we calculated the average time required to complete each crescendo task including standard errors see figure

card and smith also mentioned the importance of calibration when generating a training corpus for nlp tasks as noted above numerous postprocessing calibration techniques have been developed traditional binning methods set up bins based on the predicted posterior ˆ p recalculate calibrated posteriors ˆ q per each bin on a validation set and then update every ˆ p with ˆ q if ˆ p falls into the certain bin on the other hand scaling methods rescale the predicted posterior ˆ p from the softmax layer trained on a validation set recently kumar et al pointed out that such rescaling methods do not actually produce wellcalibrated probabilities as reported since the true posterior probability distribution can not be captured with the often low number of samples in the validation set to address the issue the authors proposed a scalingbinning calibrator but still rely on the validation set in a broad sense our endtoend training with the calibration reduction loss can be seen as sort of regularization designed to mitigate overfitting just as classical explicit regularization techniques such as the lasso penalize models large weights here we penalize models with posterior outputs that differ substantially from the estimated true posterior shows that the effectiveness of recalibration decreases when the size of the validation set is small posterior calibrated training in general most of existing classification models are designed to maximize the likelihood estimates mle its objective is then to minimize the crossentropy xent loss between the predicted probability and the true probability over k different classes during training time poscal minimizes the crossentropy as well as the calibration error as a multitask setup while the former is a taskspecific objective the latter is a statistical objective to make the model to be statistically wellcalibrated from its data distribution such dataoriented calibration makes the taskoriented model more reliable in terms of its data distribution compared to the prior postcalibration methods with a fixed and often small validation set poscal dynamically estimates the required statistics for calibration from the train set during training iterations

there is no doubt that museums and art galleries hold immense cultural value shaping how we view the world around us this raises the question of whether these institutions should focus mainly on showcasing their own country‚äôs art rather than including works from abroad personally i believe it‚äôs important to find a balance‚äîhighlighting national art while also giving people the opportunity to experience foreign artworks to some extent if local museums only displayed pieces by national artists this could have several positive outcomes it would bring recognition to local artists and instill a sense of pride among citizens fostering patriotism and strengthening national identity increased interest in national art could also lead to more government support and funding for local artists helping the country‚äôs art scene to flourish however art thrives on new influences and without exposure to creative ideas from around the world the growth of local art might be limited people are drawn to art because it resonates with their sense of beauty and evokes deep emotions regardless of whether the artist is local or foreign artistic talent isn‚äôt confined by borders so the main focus should be on the impact and enjoyment the artwork provides not its origin by appreciating both local and international art we avoid having to choose between the two limiting museum collections to only national works in my view would be detrimental and contrary to the inclusive spirit of art in conclusion i believe that allowing people to appreciate art from other countries does not diminish a nation in any way the best approach would be for governments to support their own artists while also welcoming exhibitions from abroad benefiting both the local art community and the public as a whole

although the present study indicates that human feedback may enhance student outcomes the findings lack statistical significance due to a limited sample size consequently it is recommended that future francis l grim g halloran j mitchell z monroe j nixon and n pizzato special acknowledgment is due to kyle yohler whose development of a web interface for the grading tool significantly streamlined the process for both students and evaluators this initiative constitutes a research to practice partnership rpp involving two middle schools and two universities the primary objective is to investigate challenges and identify solutions aimed at increasing student participation and interest in computer science cs this is achieved through the identification experimentation and refinement of methodologies designed to foster computational thinking ct skills the research utilizes a stem ecosystem model to establish a support structure intended to mitigate barriers and positively influence student progression within stem disciplines while the rpp remains an ongoing endeavor this report presents data derived from the first year of collaboration with one of the partner middle schools although the guiding research questions are designed to be iterative and subject to annual revision the initial data offers insights into four key areas barriers to establishing a stem ecosystem that supports cs ct for all students through integration into science mathematics and language arts curricula the necessary factors or interventions for developing a school wide cs ct focused ecosystem indicators of success for such an ecosystem and the extent to which the ecosystem prepares and engages students for advanced cs ct coursework in high school the year one data is analyzed within the context of the stem ecosystem framework to inform subsequent phases of the partnership ultimately this project contributes to the broader understanding of how to prepare future generations for a workforce where foundational knowledge of cs ct is essential for success over the past decade industries and services reliant on computational tools and stem professionals have experienced unprecedented growth however the economic and professional benefits of this expansion have not been equitably distributed across all demographic groups

as detailed in section model resetting is performed utilizing a checkpoint obtained after t iterations given a total training duration of iterations this reset phase constitutes of the overall training process thereby remaining within the threshold established by frankle et al additional experiments conducted with t in yielded results consistent with those reported herein it is noteworthy that in the specific case where t sparse lottery ticket slt pruning effectively degenerates into magnitude pruning mp as no reset mechanism is applied we recommend a more comprehensive hyperparameter search to determine the optimal t value as a direction for future research furthermore it is observed that the magnitude of parameters in both the initial and final models increases with each pruning step consequently at sparsity weight values exceed rendering the model unsuitable for checkpoint averaging due to heightened sensitivity to minor perturbations while yu et al report successful application of checkpoint averaging this discrepancy may be attributed to their selection of theta t from a dense training run for resetting whereas our methodology selects theta t from the most recent sparse training phase regarding the core lottery ticket clt hypothesis the underlying premise posits that an untrained network inherently contains a sparse subnetwork capable of independent training zhou et al demonstrated that the signs of the remaining parameters are critical whereas their specific random values are not although zhou et al conducted their experiments on mnist and cifar we evaluate this hypothesis on the wmt english to german translation task using a deep transformer architecture surprisingly clt pruning outperforms slt pruning across most sparsity levels see table while frankle and carbin previously established that lottery ticket lt pruning relies on specific parameter values rather than merely learning a sparse topology—evidenced by performance degradation upon shuffling or reinitializing parameters—the robust performance of our clt experiments suggests that parameter values are of secondary importance provided their signs are preserved to verify the necessity of retaining original signs we conducted an experiment in which signs were randomly reassigned to parameters following pruning to sparsity

the abstraction level indicates the extent to which a design is distanced from tangible technological details this level of abstraction can differ across various architectural fields but four main levels are widely recognized presentation at this level architecture is simplified into a form to streamline the communication of essential ideas particularly to business executives distilling complex architectural diagrams into more straightforward highlevel visuals ensures that the core messages are conveyed effectively conceptual this level offers a more structured view of architecture deliberately omitting many specifics the rationale for not including certain details is that they may not be relevant to the diagram’s intended purpose or are yet to be decided logical the architecture is depicted in detail but remains uncertain of specific technologies it aims to give a full outline without committing to the particular technologies that will be employed for the final build physical this level focuses on the actual technological specifics used or to be used in the architecture’s realization making it the most concrete representation of the four the application architecture is a subset of the enterprise solution architecture that includes a process to architect and design the application architecture as well as the actual application architecture model which represents the content of the application architecture application architecture helps organizations decide how to invest in new software and systems it makes sure that any new software being looked at designed or put into use can work well with the systems the organization already has this includes brandnew software updates to old software making old software better and buying and updating software packages illustrates a conceptual application architecture model of the sample trading business model that was introduced earlier in this section the goal of a conceptual application architecture is to demonstrate how members of the organization interact with the application architecture from different locations when invoking business processes this example illustrates three distinct offices accommodating a diverse range of users including management trading it and others takes the conceptual trading application architecture a bit further and describes what one of the alternative application architectures may look like at the logical level the goal of the logical application architecture is to identify the various functional blocks within the architecture without getting into the details of how they connect with and or use each other

prior to the advent of deep learning dl graph based parsing relied heavily on handcrafted features and diverged from its neural counterparts in two primary dimensions first structural learning—defined as the explicit incorporation of tree structure constraints during the training phase—was deemed essential the majority of non neural graph based parsers utilized the max margin training algorithm this approach involves predicting the highest scoring tree based on the current model and subsequently adjusting feature weights to ensure the correct tree achieves a higher score than the predicted one second high order modeling yielded significant accuracy improvements while basic first order models factorize the score of a tree into independent scores of single dependencies mcdonald et al a second order models were subsequently introduced to incorporate scores of dependency pairs such as adjacent siblings mcdonald and pereira and grandparent child relationships carreras koo and collins although these models demonstrated notable gains in accuracy they incurred costs in terms of reduced efficiency and increased decoding complexity ¹ in contrast the evolution of neural graph based dependency parsing has followed a divergent trajectory pei et al proposed the use of feedforward neural networks to automatically learn combinations of atomic features and compute subtree scores demonstrating that the integration of second order scores for adjacent sibling subtrees significantly enhanced performance subsequently both wang and chang and kiperwasser and goldberg introduced architectures utilizing bidirectional long short term memory bilstm networks as encoders employing minimal feature sets to score single dependencies within a first order framework these three representative studies all adopted global max margin training later dozat and manning introduced a robust and efficient biaffine parser achieving state of the art accuracy across diverse datasets and languages this biaffine parser operates as a first order model and employs a simpler more efficient non structural training strategy based on local head selection for each token zhang et al given these contrasting developmental trends this work seeks to establish a connection between pre deep learning and deep learning techniques in graph based parsing specifically the primary objective is to address the following question can previously effective techniques such as structural learning and high order modeling further enhance the performance of the state of the art biaffine parser and if so in what specific aspects do they contribute ¹ note third order and fourth order models have shown minimal accuracy improvements likely attributable to feature sparseness issues koo and collins ma and zhao

word and part of speech levels failed to discriminate between ftld subtypes or between patient cases and control subjects when considered alongside prior findings these results suggest that language models trained on transcripts elicited through defined tasks e g the cookie theft task are more effective at distinguishing between cases and controls than models trained on broader unstructured utility in predicting transitions to ad furthermore wankerl et al proposed a methodology combining two distinct perplexity estimates one derived from a model trained on transcripts from healthy this study presents the development and evaluation of a suite of hands on cs unplugged activities designed to introduce fundamental concepts of computer animation to k– students these activities cover keyframe interpolation particle effects hierarchical transformations for human modeling and animation and perspective projection each module is explicitly aligned with k– common core mathematics standards and utilizes readily available materials given established correlations between mathematical proficiency and success in computing this research investigates the feasibility of rendering advanced computer graphics concepts—typically reserved for higher education—accessible to younger learners successfully achieving this objective could foster greater engagement with and proficiency in mathematics throughout the k– curriculum the paper is structured as follows section provides background on media consumption computer generated imagery cgi the mathematical foundations of cgi and relevant prior research section details the pedagogical design of the learning activities while section outlines the experimental methodology section presents the empirical results the study concludes with a discussion of limitations future research directions and final conclusions the motivation for this work is grounded in the significant volume of screen time consumed by children and adolescents research indicates that television remains a dominant medium with of tweens ages – and of teens reporting regular viewership averaging nearly two hours daily furthermore a substantial portion of this engagement is characterized by passive or interactive consumption accounting for of media time for tweens and for teens among younger demographics ages months to years device ownership and usage are pervasive by age four of children possess their own television own a mobile device and have utilized a mobile device with most initiating use before their first birthday additionally studies on advertising efficacy suggest that children exhibit heightened receptivity to animated and cartoon based content consequently films featuring cgi special effects a primary application of computer graphics constitute a significant segment of contemporary media consumption

here we study the impact of both namely i what was written i e what was published by the target medium and how it describes itself on twitter vs ii who read it i e analyzing the readers of the target medium on facebook twitter and youtube we further study iii what was written about the target medium on wikipedia the evaluation results show that what was written matters most and that putting all information sources together yields huge improvements over the current stateoftheart introduction the rise of the web has made it possible for anybody to create a website or a blog and to become a news medium undoubtedly this was a hugely positive development as it elevated freedom of expression to a whole new level thus allowing anybody to make their voice heard online with the subsequent rise of social media anybody could potentially reach out to a vast audience something that until recently was only possible for major news outlets one of the consequences was a trust crisis with traditional news media stripped off their gatekeeping role the society was left unprotected against potential manipulation the issue became a general concern in a year marked by microtargeted online disinformation and misinformation at an unprecedented scale primarily in connection to brexit and the us presidential campaign these developments gave rise to the term “fake news” which can be defined as “false often sensational information disseminated under the guise of news reporting ” it was declared word of the year by macquarie dictionary and of year by the collins english dictionary in an attempt to solve the trust problem several initiatives such as politifact snopes factcheck and full fact have been launched to factcheck suspicious claims manually however given the scale of the proliferation of false information online it became clear that it was unfeasible to factcheck every single suspicious claim even when this was done automatically not only due to computational challenges but also due to timing in order to factcheck a claim be it manually or automatically one often needs to verify the stance of mainstream media concerning that claim and the reaction of users on social media accumulating this kind of evidence takes time but time flies very fast and any delay means more potential sharing of the malicious content on social media

and lambda serves as a hyperparameter to balance the competing objectives candidate generation to construct the candidate source list sc c di for a given claim c di based on the text t c di we employ an off the shelf semantic role labeling srl tool he et al this tool parses the sentences in t c di to identify semantic roles specifically determining who did what to whom all text spans tagged as arg the agent are extracted as potential source candidates although theoretically only agents associated with specific verbs such as say or claim constitute valid sources we adopt a broad extraction strategy by including all arg instances the final identification of the correct source is delegated to the proposed truth extraction te model it is important to note that the srl tool is utilized solely for candidate generation we do not rely on the alignment between the claim and the arg the theme span to determine the source due to three primary factors the inherent noise in relationships produced by the srl parser the potential for cross sentence relationships between the source and the claim and the possibility that a single claim may be paraphrased across multiple sentences a comparative analysis of this approach is presented in the evaluation section training data augmentation in addition to the sources and claims provided by the annotated dataset the documents contain numerous sentences exhibiting the who says claims what pattern which are valuable for model training to extract these examples we apply the off the shelf srl tool to parse all sentences within the document we then compute the semantic similarity between the verb in each parsed sentence and the verbs associated with the annotated sources if the average similarity exceeds a predefined threshold the corresponding arg and arg spans are treated as positive training examples for the source and claim respectively negative examples are generated by randomly substituting either the arg or the arg in the parsed sentences with alternative sources or claims thereby creating mismatched pairs for contrastive learning

information technology is taking up more space in our lives every day we use technology at home at work and even during our free time these tools help us connect with people around the world and access information quickly however if we rely on them too much they can also make us lazy in this essay i will discuss how we can make the most of information technology so that its benefits outweigh its drawbacks humans are social beings and technology helps us stay connected with others globally computers and the internet are key tools that let us keep in touch with friends family and people everywhere no matter where we live today‚äôs technology allows us to talk to our loved ones almost whenever we want as someone living abroad i find this aspect of technology especially helpful it makes my life easier and helps reduce feelings of homesickness moreover thanks to advances in technology we can now find the information we need online in less than a minute search engines like google and yahoo are very useful for this purpose instead of spending time in public libraries we can quickly look up information on these platforms this speed allows us to learn and improve our knowledge more efficiently on the downside we might end up spending all day sitting in front of our computers at home or work completing tasks with minimal physical effort this habit can make us lazy and inactive our bodies need regular exercise to burn calories and stay healthy becoming too dependent on technology might take away this need and lead to health problems in the future in conclusion while technology addiction can contribute to an unhealthy lifestyle i believe the advantages‚äîsuch as faster access to information and better ways to connect with others‚äîoutweigh the drawbacks

analysis of the pre quiz and post quiz data reveals a significant correlation between prior knowledge and learning outcomes specifically all students who correctly answered the initial two pre quiz questions demonstrated improved overall performance in the post quiz in contrast among students who made errors on these initial questions only exhibited improvement after interacting with the system these findings suggest that learners possessing a foundational understanding of the subject matter derive greater benefit from the system furthermore the system demonstrated particular efficacy in teaching the concept of infinite loops accuracy on the corresponding quiz item increased from in the pre quiz to in the post quiz this substantial improvement may be attributed to the system s ability to provide tangible feedback students could physically observe system latency during the execution of infinite loops thereby reinforcing their conceptual understanding current limitations of the system include its restriction to for loops while loops and if statements as well as its capacity to trace programs containing only a single variable future development plans involve expanding the code generator to support more complex constructs such as method calls and recursion and enhancing the tracing engine to handle multiple variables per program additionally the system will be augmented with diverse types of effective tracing visualizations a comprehensive evaluation of this enhanced version is scheduled for a large scale introductory computer science course this study will incorporate both performance metrics pre and post quizzes and detailed logs of user activity to analyze interaction patterns upon validation of the system s effectiveness it will be made publicly available as a module within the chiqattutor suite while the current version is not yet hosted online it is available free of charge upon request interested parties are invited to contact the authors for further details finally future research will investigate the impact of complex tracing problems on novice programmers code writing and debugging skills although prior studies have established that solving tracing problems enhances programming proficiency most existing literature has focused exclusively on simple tracing scenarios this research aims to address the gap by exploring the pedagogical value of more complex tracing exercises

a persistent challenge within computer science education involves the diverse difficulties encountered by students in introductory courses the heterogeneity of these struggles complicates the identification of optimal pedagogical interventions while extensive research exists regarding student misconceptions in traditional text based programming environments across the fields of computer science education educational data mining and learning analytics there remains a paucity of literature addressing novice misconceptions specifically within block based programming contexts concurrently the adoption of block based programming environments has become increasingly prevalent in undergraduate introductory courses for both majors and non majors these environments are designed to mitigate common introductory challenges particularly for learners with limited prior programming experience by reducing cognitive load enhancing structural comprehension and improving task completion efficiency however many such environments offer limited intrinsic support relying heavily on instructors and teaching assistants whose availability is often constrained this paper presents a data driven investigation of student programs developed within prime a block based environment tailored for undergraduate computer science education by applying bayesian gaussian mixture models to cluster unsuccessful student attempts at two specific programming activities we conducted a qualitative analysis of the resulting groups this analysis uncovered distinct patterns of student misconceptions and offered insights into how targeted supports—such as remedial activities or concept specific interventions—could be leveraged by instructors and adaptive learning systems to address individual student needs this study builds upon a substantial body of work concerning student code analysis traditionally viewed through the lenses of automated assessment and plagiarism detection existing methodologies include static analysis evaluating code without execution dynamic analysis evaluating code based on test case outputs and hybrid systems combining both approaches furthermore clustering techniques have been employed to visualize programming behaviors generate formative feedback for students and provide instructional insights extending these established techniques to the domain of block based programming this research shifts the focus from automated assessment to the enhancement of instructional strategies the increasing prevalence of block based environments has in turn spurred a growing corpus of research dedicated to the analysis of such programs
