this paper presents a system for tweet level sentiment analysis designed to classify microblogging content as either positive or negative based on overall polarity while supervised learning classifiers are widely employed for this task they frequently encounter difficulties with tweets containing specific linguistic constructs such as conjunctions e g but and conditionals e g if furthermore these models often generate decision scores proximate to the classification boundary for a significant portion of the dataset indicating uncertainty rather than definitive error to address these limitations the proposed methodology integrates linguistic rules and sentic computing resources to augment standard supervised learning approaches the efficacy of this system is demonstrated through evaluation on two publicly available twitter corpora the proliferation of social media platforms has generated vast quantities of unstructured opinionated data offering valuable insights into public sentiment analyzing this data enables the derivation of informed conclusions and predictions regarding diverse subjects however the sheer volume of data renders manual analysis infeasible thereby necessitating the adoption of automated sentiment analysis techniques polarity classification a core subtask of sentiment analysis involves categorizing text into positive negative or neutral sentiments this task can be approached at various granularities document level assessing the entire text sentence level assessing individual sentences or aspect based identifying specific opinion targets and their associated sentiments tweets characterized by their brevity historically limited to characters and informal nature present unique challenges for automated analysis they frequently contain multiple sentences alongside non standard orthography including misspellings slang urls character elongations repeated punctuation emoticons abbreviations and hashtags these features complicate the extraction of sentiment making tweet level polarity classification a significant area of research this study specifically focuses on binary classification positive versus negative excluding the neutral category conventional supervised learning methods for this domain rely on feature vectors extracted from the text to represent its salient characteristics the proposed system seeks to enhance this foundation through the incorporation of domain specific linguistic knowledge

best parse tree the syntaxaware approach despite of recent advances in dependency parsing parsers still cannot output parse trees with high accuracy on outofdomain or irregular data in this work we exploit rich syntactic information contained in the edgeweighted graphs to mitigate the effects of parsing errors specifically we firstly employ graph convolutional networks gcn to encode the edgeweighted graphs and then integrate them into different processing levels of orl with implicit parser hidden states finally we employ novel mtl frameworks to alleviate the error propagation problem further dependency graph convolutional networks d ep gcn in this subsection we propose dependency graph convolutional networks d ep gcn to better encode the syntactic information from the edgeweighted graphs on the one hand compared with explicit best parse trees edgeweighted graphs convey richer structural information by providing all latent syntactic structures and avoid error propagation as well on the other hand compared with the implicit hidden states of the parser encoder an edgeweighted graph denoted as an attention matrix explicitly captures the modification strength of word pairs the original gcn is designed for directly modeling graphstructured data although each node only receives information from its immediate neighbors through edges in one gcn layer multilayer gcn can propagate information more globally if there exist connected paths formally the output of node i at the l th layer of gcn is computed by the following equation h l i f j a ij w l h l − j b l where a is the adjacency matrix of a graph with n nodes w l and b l are the model parameters f is an activation function h i is the initial input vector as shown by figure e we apply d ep gcn to connect the parser model and the orl model

this study advances the field of disaster centric emotion detection through three primary contributions the introduction of a sufficiently large dataset to facilitate the training of supervised classifiers an investigation into various pretraining strategies designed to instill robust inductive biases and the establishment of domain adaptation baselines by leveraging emotive samples acquired through distant supervision dataset construction this section details the construction of hurricane emo an annotated corpus comprising english tweets collected during hurricanes harvey irma and maria the following subsections outline the dataset s components including the initial preprocessing pipeline § the annotation methodology § and the formulation and calculation of inter annotator agreement § preprocessing the foundational data for this study was derived from the large scale twitter repository released by chowdhury et al herein referred to as hurricane ext extended this repository contains tweets streamed during the aforementioned hurricane events and served as the starting point for our dataset construction the preprocessing phase involved two distinct stages first to ensure data privacy and consistency all usernames and urls were replaced with the tokens and respectively followed by the removal of duplicate entries second filtering techniques were applied to ensure the retention of tweets containing emotive content this process relied on a lexical prior requiring that each candidate tweet contain at least one word derived from emolex mohammad and turney emolex comprises crowdsourced words associated with various emotion categories it is important to note that while these words frequently appear in emotional contexts they are not exclusively emotion terms for instance the word payback is associated with anger but is also prevalent in financial discourse the utilization of this lexicon for bootstrapping emotion datasets is well established in prior literature bravo marquez et al majumder et al giatsoglou et al primarily because alternative approaches—such as utilizing raw unlabeled tweets or employing a pre trained model for initial classification—present significant challenges initial attempts to construct the dataset without emotion specific filtering i e using raw tweets resulted in a high volume of spurious content including news snippets unrelated to emotional expression the resulting noise level rendered the annotation process prohibitively costly and inefficient necessitating the adoption of the lexical filtering strategy described above

computer science classes at both institutions attended a presentation introducing the concept of technology and computing for social good this presentation illustrated how computing applications have been utilized to address community challenges emphasizing the capacity of developers to identify local needs and engineer technological solutions subsequent to the presentation students received an official invitation via email to participate in the innovation for humanity iah initiative which comprised a series of surveys to facilitate response tracking and the administration of extra credit within the domain of hate speech and abusive language detection prior research has extensively addressed issues of gender and racial bias notably park et al and sap et al have highlighted critical challenges specifically the underrepresentation of dialectal variations and the presence of imbalanced training data respectively established methodologies for mitigating such biases include data augmentation utilizing less biased datasets term substitution during the training phase e g swapping gendered terms and the integration of debiased word embeddings bolukbasi et al building upon these foundations this study proposes a novel approach that directly manipulates a model s contextual representation of identifier terms through the regularization of their explanatory outputs specifically we employ post hoc explanation algorithms to interpret and modulate fine tuned language models such as bert devlin et al which have demonstrated state of the art performance in hate speech detection tasks macavaney et al mandl et al our focus lies on post hoc interpretability techniques which elucidate model predictions without necessarily revealing the internal mechanistic processes of the model guidotti et al these methods quantify the importance of input features at either the word level ribeiro et al sundararajan et al or the phrase level murdoch et al singh et al to validate our approach we selected two public corpora that emphasize the rhetorical dimensions of hate speech moving beyond the mere identification of slurs and explicitly offensive language davidson et al the first is the gab hate corpus ghc kennedy et al comprising a large random sample n drawn from the pushshift io data dump of the gab social network this dataset has been annotated according to a typology of hate based rhetoric a construct informed by international hate speech criminal codes and social science research on prejudice and dehumanization gab is recognized as a platform with a high prevalence of hate speech zannettou et al lima et al and a user base associated with the alt right movement anthony benson the second dataset consistent in domain and definitional framework was compiled by de our model takes as input a document represented as a sequence of tokens x x k and a knowledge graph g consisting of nodes v i x and g are separately consumed by a document encoder and a graph encoder as presented in importantly we present two types of graphs d oc g raph focusing on the global context and s eg g raph which additionally captures topic shift the summary decoder then generates an abstractive summary by attending to both the document and the graph in we formulate a maximum likelihood training objective which leverages the detection of salient nodes in the graph encoders document encoder we first feed input x to roberta and take the last layer output as token embeddings we then employ a singlelayer bidirectional lstm bilstm over token embeddings producing encoder hidden states h k at time step k graph encoder built on the graph constructed in we create nodes for predicates as done in previous graphtosequence work to reduce model parameters directed unlabeled edges are added from subject to predicate and from predicate to object we further add reverse edges and selfloops to enhance the information flow and this forms the graph g node initialization each node often contains multiple mentions of an entity we thus initialize node representation v i by using the average embedding of its tokens we leverage document encoder hidden states h k as the contextual representation of tokens number of mentions in the node is added as an extra encoding to v i to signify entity salience contextualized node encoding our graph encoder improves upon graph attention networks gats by adding residual connections between layers as discussed in koncelkedziorski et al

table presents both type frequency the number of distinct lexical items in the lexicon utilizing that suffix and token frequency the aggregate occurrence of words bearing that suffix within the corpus german nouns are categorized into three grammatical genders—masculine feminine and neuter—a lexical feature that exhibits a strong correlation with plural class assignment specifically the majority of feminine nouns adopt the e n suffix whereas nouns utilizing e or the zero plural ∅ are predominantly masculine or neuter furthermore the phonological structure of a noun influences its plural classification for instance nouns terminating in a schwa typically take the e n suffix elsen despite these statistical tendencies no absolute rules govern plural formation and no single suffix constitutes a majority across the lexicon scholarly debate persists regarding the productivity of various plural markers and the specific contexts in which they apply historically this discourse has centered on the infrequent s class although rare s appears across a diverse array of linguistic environments including proper names e g der bader → die bader versus die baders acronyms and truncated or quoted nouns e g der asi → die asis a shortening of asozialer additionally s frequently serves as the plural marker for recent loanwords and empirical evidence suggests that children extend this suffix to novel nouns clahsen et al based on these observations m posit that s functions as the default plural marker they argue that it applies in a heterogeneous set of elsewhere conditions that do not constitute a cohesive similarity space effectively serving as an emergency form when other plural markers are inapplicable

distribution inclusion of members with high gpas and practical experience related to the project they found that these factors did not appear to influence performance in a statistically significant way in spite of measurable influence in our study we allowed the students to form their own groups and we did not interfere in the group making decisions except to limit the group size we realize that undergraduate courses in postsemester feedback students always identified ggas as one of the utilized methods that helped their learning in addition our experience with this approach is that the any immediate feedback on their answers

however training semantic parsers from weak supervision poses difficulties and in addition the generated logical forms are only used as an intermediate step prior to retrieving the denotation in this paper we present t a p as an approach to question answering over tables without generating logical forms t a p as trains from weak supervision and predicts the denotation by selecting table cells and optionally applying a corresponding aggregation operator to such selection t a p as extends bert’s architecture to encode tables as input initializes from an effective joint pretraining of text segments and tables crawled from wikipedia and is trained endtoend we experiment with three different semantic parsing datasets and find that t a p as outperforms or rivals semantic parsing models by improving stateoftheart accuracy on sqa from to and performing on par with the stateoftheart on w iki sql and w iki tq but with a simpler model architecture we additionally find that transfer learning which is trivial in our setting from w ik i sql to w iki tq yields accuracy points above the stateoftheart introduction question answering from semistructured tables is usually seen as a semantic parsing task where the question is translated to a logical form that can be executed against the table to retrieve the correct denotation semantic parsers rely on supervised training data that pairs natural language questions with logical forms but such data is expensive to annotate in recent years many attempts aim to reduce the burden of data collection for semantic parsing including paraphrasing human in the loop and training on examples from other domains one prominent data collection approach focuses on weak supervision where a training example consists of a question and its denotation instead of the full logical form

experimental scope the experimental evaluation is constrained to an extended pretraining regime utilizing fewer than half the training steps originally employed by bert specifically batches with a batch size of background this section provides a concise overview of the bert pretraining strategy devlin et al and delineates the specific modifications implemented to accommodate our novel span selection auxiliary task architecture and configuration bert employs a transformer architecture devlin et al comprising l layers where each block utilizes a self attention heads and a hidden dimension of h the model input consists of the concatenation of two segments x dots x m and y dots y n separated by special delimiter tokens the resulting sequence is structured as follows x dots x m y dots y n this configuration adheres to the constraint m n s where s denotes the maximum permissible sequence length during training the model undergoes an initial pretraining phase on a large corpus of unlabeled data followed by fine tuning on downstream tasks utilizing labeled datasets objective functions during the pretraining phase bert optimizes two primary objective functions masked language modeling mlm and next sentence prediction nsp masked language modeling mlm in this task a random subset of tokens within the input sequence is replaced with a special token the model is trained to minimize the cross entropy loss associated with predicting these masked tokens specifically bert selects of the input tokens uniformly for modification of these selected tokens are replaced with are retained in their original form and the remaining are substituted with a random token drawn from the vocabulary next sentence prediction nsp this objective involves a binary classification task designed to determine whether two input sentences are consecutive in the original text training examples are sampled with equal probability positive pairs consist of consecutive sentences while negative pairs are constructed by pairing sentences from distinct documents span selection pretraining having outlined the bert framework and its associated objective functions in the preceding section we now proceed to detail the proposed span selection pretraining methodology

such work has been conducted on the basis of the conventional rnn architectures and may not be fully applicable to the advanced transformer recently bapna et al introduced a transparent network into nmt models to ease the optimization of models with deeper encoders to improve gradient flow they let each decoder layer find an unique weighted combination of all encoder layer outputs instead of just the top encoder layer wang et al found that adopting the proper use of layer normalization helps to learn deep encoders a method was further proposed to combine layers and encourage gradient flow by simple shortcut connections zhang et al introduced a depthscaled initialization to improve norm preservation and proposed a merged attention sublayer to avoid the computational overhead for deep models researchers have also explored growing nmt models in two stages in which shallow encoders and decoders are trained in the first stage and subsequently held constant when another set of shallow layers are stacked on the top in concurrent work xu et al studied the effect of the computation order of residual connection and layer normalization and proposed an parameter initialization method with lipschitz restrictions to ensure the convergence of deep transformers our method significantly differs from these methods solving the problem by associating the decoder with the encoder with multigranular dependencies in different spacescales exploiting deep representations have been studied to strengthen feature propagation and encourage feature reuse in nmt all of these works mainly attend the decoder to the final output of the encoder stack we instead coordinate the encoder and the decoder at earlier stage conclusion and future work in this paper we propose a multisacle collaborative framework to ease the training of extremely deep nmt models specifically instead of the topmost representation of the encoder stack we attend the decoder to multigranular source information with different spacescales we have shown that the proposed approach boosts the training of very deep models and can bring improvements on translation quality from greatly increased depth experiments on various language pairs show that the m sc achieves prominent improvements over strong baselines as well as previous deep models

ideally an indoor farming system adhering to the principles outlined in this study should maximize the density of growth stations within the available spatial constraints while a central management entity hereafter referred to as the farmer should coordinate overall operations individual growth stations must maintain functional autonomy and not rely on continuous connectivity to the central node this architectural decoupling is critical to ensure system resilience it prevents localized failures or connectivity issues at the central level or within specific stations from propagating across the entire network the growth station gs controller was implemented using a raspberry pi model b to facilitate robust system communication and local data storage the primary function of the gs controller is to execute the communication protocol developed by the engineering students with a specific focus on regulating lighting cycles the implemented protocols included a cycle hours of illumination followed by hours of darkness and a cycle hours of illumination followed by hours of darkness furthermore the system was configured to test various spectral compositions including white light red blue light and a combination of red blue white light in terms of data management the gs controller aggregates sensor readings and stores them locally before periodically transmitting the data to the farmer for centralization and backup to manage hardware interfacing an arduino mega was employed although the raspberry pi supports digital i o the inclusion of analog sensors necessitated a microcontroller capable of handling both signal types leading to the selection of the arduino platform sensor readings were stored locally using a timestamped key value structure upon transmission to the farmer these attributes were preserved with the addition of a unique identifier specifying the originating growth station beyond sensor integration the hardware controller managed the illumination systems the implementation utilized two neopixel rings each comprising addressable rgb leds as the light source for every growth station the adoption of addressable leds was driven by the requirement for dynamic lighting control allowing for the execution of complex lighting conditions that are not constrained by the hardware s static initial configurations

for every class c l j k situated at level l j within the hierarchical structure a raw textual definition is retrieved from an external dictionary to generate a vector representation hereafter referred to as the class definition vector cdv these cdv representations are subsequently integrated into the classification framework via two distinct strategies parent node conditioning pnc given a document d the objective is to classify it within the target set of classes c c l k dots c l m k where m denotes the total number of layers in the taxonomy the proposed approach operates sequentially first the highest level class c l k is predicted subsequently the cdv corresponding to this predicted class cv l k is utilized as an auxiliary input alongside the encoder outputs within the attention mechanism to facilitate the prediction of the class at the next hierarchical level c l k this iterative process is repeated for all subsequent layers of the class hierarchy adapted beam search while beam search is a decoding strategy traditionally employed in neural machine translation freitag and al onaizan it is applicable to any problem involving sequential token by token decoding this study evaluates the efficacy of applying beam search to hierarchical text classification htc and introduces an adapted variant that leverages the computed cdv representations the scoring function is modified as follows text score sum i t log p y i mid x y dots y i text cd z y i quad during each step of the decoding phase the model predicts a class corresponding to the appropriate level of the hierarchy at a given time step i the beam search expands all k beam size potential class candidates and ranks them based on their cumulative logarithmic probability in addition to the standard probability calculation the cosine distance is computed between the cdv of each candidate class and the average word embedding vector derived from the textual description z of the document to be classified the text cd component in equation this distance metric is added to the logarithmic probability of each candidate the candidates are then re ranked according to this composite score and the top k candidates are retained for the subsequent step

accustomed to drawing information from the literature this discordance was less an issue but based on our observations and student feedback they likewise would have benefitted from a central text unseen labels in the ner target domain the bilstm crf model lample et al employs a unified label set for both source and target domains it frames the task as token level entity classification rendering it applicable to both zero shot and few shot scenarios training configuration the architecture employs a two layer bidirectional long short term memory bilstm network with a hidden dimension of and a dropout rate of for both the template encoder and the utterance encoder notably the parameters for these two encoders are not shared a separate single layer bilstm with a hidden size of is used to encode the hidden states of slot entity tokens ensuring the output dimension matches that of the concatenated word level and character level embeddings optimization is performed using the adam optimizer with a learning rate of the training objective utilizes cross entropy loss for the initial three way classification step followed by specific slot type predictions in the second step for model selection samples from the target domain are allocated to the validation set with the remaining data reserved for testing to ensure a fair comparison the ct and rzt baselines are implemented using the same hyperparameter settings as the proposed model results and discussion cross domain slot filling quantitative analysis as demonstrated in table the proposed models achieve performance significantly superior to the current state of the art approach rzt the ct framework exhibits limitations in capturing complete slot entities whereas the proposed framework effectively recognizes slot entity tokens by sharing parameters across all slot types

research that uses human subjects as the unit of analysis would infer demographics via surveys however with the proliferation of the web and social media social research now includes largescale observational data that would be challenging to obtain using surveys this type of data typically lacks demographic information but may contain large amounts of text written by participants from which demographics can be extracted in this space some researchers are specific about the confounders they want to extract such as an individual’s ideology or mood other researchers condition on all the text they have available and assume that lowdimensional summaries capture all possible confounders for example researchers might assume that text encodes all possible confounders between alcohol use and college success or psychiatric medication and anxiety we dissect and comment on this assumption in section open problems nlp systems have been shown to be inaccurate for lowresource languages and exhibit racial and gender disparity furthermore the ethics of predicting psychological indicators such as mental health status from text are questionable it is unclear how to mitigate these the sentiment or topical content of text as causal confounders

introduction contemporary state of the art approaches for natural language processing nlp rely on language models pretrained on tasks that do not necessitate labeled data peters et al howard and ruder devlin et al yang et al liu et al sun et al fine tuning these pretrained models for downstream applications such as question answering or broader natural language understanding tasks has been established as a robust and effective strategy bert a recently introduced and highly successful architecture for language understanding exemplifies this paradigm the standard bert adaptation protocol involves modifying the pretraining model while preserving the underlying transformer encoder layers specifically the layers dedicated to final prediction in the pretraining phase are discarded and replaced with layers tailored to the target task followed by end to end fine tuning of all parameters on the downstream dataset bert is grounded in the transformer architecture vaswani et al and is trained on two primary unsupervised objectives masked language model mlm predicting masked word pieces based on surrounding contextual information next sentence prediction nsp determining whether two provided sequences occur sequentially within a text the masked language modeling task often referred to as the cloze task taylor alongside next sentence prediction functions as an auxiliary objective ando and zhang that necessitates language comprehension thereby enabling the model to acquire effective linguistic representations however the cloze pretraining task frequently presents instances that rely on shallow prediction mechanisms or rote memorization rather than deep reasoning in many cases successful completion of cloze instances requires only syntactic or lexical proficiency for instance as illustrated in table the first two examples necessitate syntactic and lexical understanding respectively while the third example primarily involves the completion of collocations conversely certain instances demand access to memorized factual knowledge such as the final example which queries the location of hadrian s death similar challenges are observed in other language modeling architectures in gpt radford et al entities present within a language generation prompt are expanded table types of cloze instances type example syntactic in the th century the blast furnace spread into what is now belgium where it was improved

this study identifies four primary learning objectives prevalent in current research on computing accessibility accessibility awareness technical knowledge empathy for individuals with disabilities and potential endeavors while these objectives demonstrate consistency across the courses examined they are predominantly concentrated within a limited selection of elective offerings conversely existing literature provides scant guidance on integrating these topics into core curricula consequently this research underscores two critical needs in the domain of computing accessibility education first there is a requirement to develop a comprehensive research roadmap that establishes clear learning objectives and evaluation methodologies for reinforcing accessibility knowledge across both core and elective courses second to operationalize this roadmap it is necessary to develop and empirically evaluate the efficacy of accessible instructional materials designed to support faculty implementation this work is supported by the henry luce foundation through the clare boothe luce fund furthermore industry internships serve as a vital mechanism for computer science cs students to acquire authentic disciplinary experience assess professional interests and secure future employment despite their significance there remains a paucity of empirical data regarding cs students participation rates in internships and the preparatory processes employed to secure them this paper presents findings from a multi institutional study designed to examine internship participation among cs undergraduates and to analyze disparities between students who intern and those who do not the study surveyed undergraduate cs students across three united states universities utilizing descriptive and inferential statistical methods for quantitative analysis and thematic analysis for open ended responses the results indicate that of respondents have participated in at least one internship demographically internship participation rates were equivalent between male and female students however a positive correlation was observed between higher socioeconomic status and the likelihood of securing an internship academically no significant differences were found between interns and non interns nevertheless thematic analysis revealed distinct differences in preparation strategies students who secured internships engaged in explicit preparatory activities such as practicing interview questions and dedicating time to career development whereas non interns demonstrated less engagement in the application process or relied primarily on coursework these quantitative findings corroborate qualitative evidence suggesting that factors external to academic coursework significantly influence a student s ability to obtain industry internships ultimately internships facilitate experiential learning that fosters intellectual personal professional and ethical growth

the proposed model projects senators onto a one dimensional continuum successfully distinguishing them by political affiliation without utilizing party labels as input features relying exclusively on speech data the model identifies an interpretable ideological spectrum senator bernie sanders is positioned as liberal senator mitch mcconnell as conservative and senator susan collins as moderate for comparative analysis figure presents ideal points derived from the voting records of the same cohort demonstrating a strong correlation between linguistic patterns and legislative behavior furthermore the text based ideal point tbip model identifies latent topics represented as vocabulary length intensity vectors which characterize the issues addressed within the speeches for each topic the model incorporates two components a neutral intensity vector and an ideological adjustment vector the latter quantifies how topic intensities vary as a function of the author s political position as illustrated in table the model successfully discerns topics related to immigration healthcare and gun control in the context of gun control the neutral intensities emphasize terms such as gun and firearms however as the author s ideal point shifts toward the negative liberal end of the spectrum the intensity of terms like gun violence and background checks increases conversely as the ideal point shifts toward the positive conservative end terms such as constitutional rights exhibit higher intensity methodologically the tbip is a bag of words framework that synthesizes concepts from ideal point models and poisson factorization topic models canny gopalan et al the model s latent variables comprise the authors ideal points the topics present in the corpus and the functional relationship between topic expression and ideological position to approximate the posterior distribution we employ an efficient black box variational inference algorithm utilizing stochastic optimization ensuring scalability to large corpora this paper details the theoretical underpinnings of the tbip and its inference algorithm alongside an evaluation of its performance across three sessions of the u s senate

subsequently length normalization is applied to ensure that the word embeddings possess unit length clique extraction the bron–kerbosch bk algorithm a recursive backtracking procedure designed to identify all maximal cliques within a given graph g is employed for efficient clique extraction the pruning operation detailed in section results in a sparse word graph for such graphs the bk algorithm achieves a time complexity of o dn d eppstein and strash where n denotes the number of vertices in g and d represents the graph s degeneracy an optimized public c implementation of the bk algorithm is utilized with the extraction process restricted to cliques containing a minimum of three words empirical observations indicate that clique extraction is completed within seconds using this implementation mapping of clique and word embeddings in the experimental phase the mapping of clique embeddings across two languages is performed using the methodology proposed by artetxe et al b implemented via their publicly available codebase the transformation matrix w is initialized as a random orthogonal matrix following the construction of the seed dictionary the procrustes problem equation is solved subsequently followed by a refinement process main results baselines a selection of supervised and unsupervised methods is established as baselines for comparison the supervised baselines comprise the iterative procrustes method proposed by smith et al the multistep framework introduced by artetxe et al a and the geometric approach developed by jawanpuria et al

in this paper we propose a cascaded method that uses unsupervised learning to ascertain the stance of twitter users with respect to a polarizing topic by leveraging their retweet behavior then it uses supervised learning based on user labels to characterize both the general political leaning of online media and of popular twitter users as well as their stance with respect to the target polarizing topic we evaluate the model by comparing its predictions to gold labels from the media bias fact check website achieving accuracy introduction online media and popular twitter users which we will collectively refer to as influencers often express overt political leanings which can be gleaned from their positions on a variety of political and cultural issues determining their leaning can be done through the analysis of their writing which includes the identification of terms that are indicative of stance performing such analysis automatically can be done using supervised classification which in turn would require manually labeled data alternatively leanings can be inferred based on which people share the content blogs tweets posts etc on social media as social media users are more likely to share content that originates from sources that generally agree with their positions here we make use of this observation to characterize influencers based on the stances of the twitter users that share their content ascertaining the stances of users also known as stance detection involves identifying the position of a user with respect to a topic an entity or a claim for example on the topic of abortion in usa the stances of leftvs rightleaning users would typically be “prochoice” vs “prolife” respectively in this paper we propose to apply unsupervised stance detection to automatically tag a large number of twitter users with their positions on specific topics the tagging identifies clusters of vocal users based on the accounts that they retweet

when detecting contentious topics and viewpoints in political debates such biases potentially leading to models only detecting certain viewpoints are especially unwelcome we do not purport to solve these issues but we do want to highlight them in this paper we presented an important objective for societal impact of nlp viewpoint diversity in news recommendation to support a healthy democratic debate going further than previous work we connect diversity in news recommendation to democratic theory and to findings in communication science on individual user differences in acceptance of diversity we conclude that to foster a healthy democratic public debate we should detect viewpoints but also detect individual latitudes of diversity nlp can play a pivotal role in these tasks vector space models would allow us to place different users or user representations and news articles in a multidimensional space where diversity is operationalized as distance and variance thereby we could personalize different users’ latitudes of diversity and accordingly deliver diverse recommendations that support a healthy public debate while still keeping the user satisfied however we also point out several technical conceptual and ethical problems that show this objective needs more than the “plug and play” of nlp solutions but rather requires further research and careful reflection this research is funded through open competition digitalization humanities and social science grant nr d awarded by the netherlands organization of scientific research nwo we would like to thank the anonymous reviewers whose detailed comments helped improve the paper all opinions and remaining errors are our own information extraction and question answering have the potential to introduce a new paradigm for how machine learning is applied to criminal law existing approaches generally use tabular data for predictive metrics an alternative approach is needed for matters of equitable justice where individuals are judged on a caseby case basis in a process involving verbal or written discussion and interpretation of case factors such discussions are individualized but they nonetheless rely on underlying facts information extraction can play an important role in surfacing these facts which are still important to understand we analyze unsupervised weakly supervised and pretrained models’ ability to extract such factual information from the freeform dialogue of california parole hearings with a few exceptions most f scores are below we use this opportunity to highlight some opportunities for further research for information extraction and question answering

in the lt sequence the majority of beginning level and intermediate level conceptual goals cgs are presented in parallel without directional dependencies this structural arrangement suggests that theoretically mastery of beginning level concepts specifically regarding the precision and completeness of instructions is not a prerequisite for attaining intermediate level understanding regarding the order of operations the sole exception is the intermediate cg creating working programs requires considering both appropriate commands and their order which is computer based and necessitates prior knowledge however as the items sampled for this sequence did not require students to construct computer programs an analysis of this specific cg falls outside the scope of the present study in contrast to the sequence items where all student responses demonstrated a uniform level of understanding the repetition items elicited responses reflecting varying degrees of conceptual grasp specifically of the nine responses analyzed six demonstrated intermediate level understanding of the principle that repeating things can have a cumulative effect two reflected beginning level understanding equating step times with step step step and two indicated no understanding of repetition within a programming context for instance in an item requiring students to determine the outcome of repeating move steps and sound meow five times two participants responded that the character would take steps and meow times while the correct answer is move steps and sound meow times these responses indicated an intermediate level albeit incomplete understanding of cumulative effects furthermore in an item asking students to identify the script that counts up by from to while displaying the results participant initially inquired about the definitions of the repeat and say scratch blocks upon receiving clarification the participant correctly selected the appropriate script from four options consequently this participant was classified at the intermediate level this classification was justified because the relevant cg is considered an offline or unplugged concept implying that the ability to apply the logic of cumulative repetition does not strictly depend on prior familiarity with specific software syntax

the basic approach does not take into account different word orders commonly found in different languages as it reuses the position embeddings in l for l we relax this restriction by learning a separate set of position embeddings for l in step along with l token embeddings we treat the symbol as a special case in the original implementation bert treats as a regular word with its own position and segment embeddings even if it always appears in the first position however this does not provide any extra capacity to the model as the same position and segment embeddings are always added up to the embedding following this observation we do not use any position and segment embeddings for the symbol noised finetuning the transformer body in our proposed method is only trained with l embeddings as its input layer but is used with l embeddings at test time to make the model more robust to this mismatch we add gaussian noises sampled from the standard normal distribution to the word position and segment embeddings during the finetuning step step adapters we also investigate the possibility of allowing the model to learn better deep representations of l while retaining the alignment with l using residual adapters adapters are small taskspecific bottleneck layers that are added between layers of a pretrained model during finetuning the original model parameters are frozen and only parameters of the adapter modules are learned in step when we transfer the l transformer to l we add a feedforward adapter module after the projection following multiheaded attention and after the two feedforward layers in each transformer layer similar to houlsby et al

although retrieval based methods are capable of generating knowledge grounded responses the knowledge they utilize is often limited in scope and occasionally inappropriate furthermore standard sequence to sequence seqseq models frequently fail to achieve the intended conversational objectives unlike the proposed knowledge aware generator which fully leverages available information seqseq models underutilize contextual knowledge resulting in dialogues that lack diversity and often appear monotonous the following example illustrates a multi turn conversation designed to transition through specific goals question answering regarding cecilia cheung → chitchat about celebrities → movie recommendation specifically left right love destiny → farewell comparison of dialogue outputs baseline model seqseq user who is the main actress in the movie wu yen bot do you have a favorite star user i like cecilia cheung bot it s a song by wu yen user what are you talking about bot yes there is another movie called the love winner have you seen it user not yet bot okay goodbye user bye bot okay goodbye analysis the baseline model fails to answer the initial query provides factually incorrect information regarding the entity type confusing a movie with a song and abruptly terminates the conversation without successfully executing the recommended movie goal proposed knowledge aware generator mgcg user who is the main actress in the movie wu yen bot it is cecilia cheung user thank you you are very smart bot indeed you have excellent taste she is considered the number one beauty in hong kong user beyond her beauty i admire her selflessness and dedication to her acting roles bot have you seen her performance in the movie wu yen user well she is very beautiful bot you will likely enjoy it user i haven t seen it yet bot okay goodbye user well i like it very much bot goodbye analysis the proposed model correctly identifies the actress engages in relevant chitchat a total of three different instructors taught across the four sections of the course offered during the spring semester each instructor conducted the class in their own respective style with the content and pedagogical approach used across the four sections being the same announcements were made periodically during the semester by each instructor reminding and encourage participating with the nonmajor peer mentors in addition to this the peer mentors were added to the moodle sites of each course to allow for interaction through that online portal with the students directly nonmajor peer mentors contact information was provided to all of the students in the course at the beginning of the semester and was also posted on the respective moodle sites for future reference anonymous surveys were conducted throughout all sections of the course all of the surveys conducted were voluntary in nature and were not required as part of the course students were given time at the beginning of lecture to complete the survey’s and they were collected at the end of class the surveys were given on paper hardcopies as previous semesters’ had indicated that they were more likely to respond with the actual paper in front of them then being directed to do so in an online portal an argument against voluntary surveys is that the students had no incentive to complete them we found that while this argument has validity students were much more willing and eager to respond when they felt that their voice was not only being heard but changes were actively made based upon sharing their insight the surveys were distributed throughout the semester at the beginning prior post midterm and before the final exam the purpose of distributing the surveys in this fashion was to gauge how opinions shifted over the course of the semester the thought process here was that students may not use the nonmajor peer mentors at the beginning of the semester when they deemed the content “easier” but may make more use of them later in the semester when the content became more “difficult ” with the final student survey conducted at the conclusion of the course regular meetings between the nonmajor peer mentors and the primary instructor were held and this provided both the instructors and the nonmajor peer mentors with an opportunity for a productive feedback loop in which corrections could be made in the moment

our dataset is focused we wrote a simple script to take as input the plot type as a special slot and other slotvalue pairs to generate the actual plot image using matplotlib this script is included in the released dataset on a new domain which is data plots natural language interfaces to structured languages such as sql have been explored in databases db programming languages pl and nlp while the problem of language to sql is different from language to plots both problems need to deal with the difficulty of automatically interpreting natural language and mapping it to an unambiguous structured representation closer to our work is the task of conversational image editing whose aim is to enable queries like “can you please fix the glare on my dog’s eyes” although both focus on image manipulation the images and manipulations are different in the two domains additionally we provide structured representations from which the plot images are generated our experiments show that such representations provide useful information for model training in contrast the structured representation is not available in conversational image editing furthermore our dataset contains over dialogs in comparison to the dialogs for image edits lastly our task is different from fullfledged program synthesis which takes natural language as input and produces computer programs in a language such as python our task is simpler and more structured data collection to facilitate data collection we make use of structured representations which we call text plot specifications definition text plot specification tpspec let s t be the set of all relevant slots for a given plot type t where t takes on plot type values such as histogram scatter etc for each slot s i ∈s t let the set of values it can take be v t i

grammatical error is an important type of noise since it naturally and frequently occurs in natural language especially in spoken and written materials from nonnative speakers dealing with such a noise reflects model robustness in representing language and grammatical knowledge it would also have a positive social impact if language encoders can model texts from nonnative speakers appropriately recent work on evaluating model’s behaviors against grammatical errors employs various methods including manually constructing minimal edited pairs on specific linguistic phenomena labeling or creating acceptability judgment resources and simulating noises for a specific nlp task such as neural machine translation sentiment classification these studies either focus on specific phenomena and mainly conduct experiments on designated corpora or rely heavily on human annotations and expert knowledge in linguistics in contrast our work automatically simulates natural occurring data and various types of grammatical errors and systematically analyzes how these noises affect downstream applications this holds more practical significance to understand the robustness of several language encoders against grammatical errors specifically we first propose an effective approach to simulating diverse grammatical errors which applies blackbox adversarial attack algorithms based on real errors observed on nus corpus of learner english nucle a grammatical error correction benchmark this approach transforms clean corpora into corrupted ones and facilitates debugging language encoders on downstream tasks we demonstrate its flexibility by evaluating models on four language understanding tasks and a sequence tagging task we next quantify model’s capacities of identifying grammatical errors by probing individual layers of pretrained encoders through a linguistic acceptability task we construct separate datasets for eight error types then we freeze encoder layers and add a simple classifier on top of each layer to predict the correctness of input texts and locate error positions this probing task assumes if a simple classifier behaves well on a designated type of error then the encoder layer is likely to contain knowledge of that error

the annotation guidelines provided comprehensive definitions and illustrative examples for each evaluation category specifying the criteria required to assign particular scores e g the conditions necessary to award a score of for maintains context these instructions were designed to minimize subjectivity thereby establishing clear and consensus driven definitions for the topicalchat dataset the annotation process involved dialog contexts randomly sampled from the frequent test set resulting in responses evaluated across six distinct quality dimensions similarly for the personachat dataset dialog contexts were drawn from the convai validation set yielding responses assessed on the same six dimensions each response was independently evaluated by three annotators who were randomly assigned to specific dialog contexts analysis inter annotator agreement across both datasets is summarized in table the reported values represent the average correlation computed across all pairs of annotations for each metric correlation coefficients were utilized rather than cohen s kappa to better accommodate the ordinal nature of the ratings where a score of is expected to correlate more strongly with than with and to ensure consistency with the evaluation of automatic metrics all correlations presented in table are statistically significant p the majority of inter annotator correlations exceed indicating moderate to strong agreement the notably lower agreement observed for the understandable metric in the personachat dataset is likely attributable to the dataset s reliance on simple language while most responses are inherently understandable those requiring specific background knowledge e g recognizing cod as an acronym for call of duty present challenges given the varying background knowledge among annotators discrepancies in identifying these specific instances of incomprehensibility result in reduced agreement conversely the overall quality metric demonstrates relatively high agreement for topicalchat and for personachat suggesting that ambiguity in specific dialog qualities is mitigated when annotators provide a holistic assessment table details the system scores across the six evaluated qualities

nevertheless it remains a subject of scholarly debate whether the grammatical knowledge induced by current models is sufficiently robust to handle syntactically complex constructions such as long distance subject verb agreement to date empirical results regarding recurrent neural network language models rnnlms trained exclusively on raw text have been largely inconclusive prior research indicates that these models exhibit poor performance on challenging test cases marvin linzen even when scaled to massive data and model sizes van schijndel et al consequently some scholars argue that architectural modifications are necessary to explicitly track syntactic structures wilcox et al b kuncoro et al the evaluation task in this domain involves determining whether a model assigns a higher likelihood to a grammatically correct sentence a compared to a minimally altered ungrammatical counterpart b linzen et al a the author that the guards like laughs b the author that the guards like laugh in this study we seek to gain new insights into the syntactic capabilities of neural language models specifically rnnlms by conducting a series of experiments under conditions distinct from previous work specifically we extensively analyze the performance of models exposed to explicit negative examples—defined here as sentences or tokens that are grammatically incorrect such as example b while it is anticipated that performance may improve given that negative examples provide a direct learning signal aligned with the evaluation task we posit that the primary motivation for this experimental setup is to deepen the understanding of the limitations and capacities inherent in current architectures we hypothesize that such strong supervision will reveal the upper bounds of these models furthermore this work is driven by engineering considerations the strategic exploitation of negative examples could yield practical advancements in developing language models and generators that are robust against specific linguistic constructions accordingly our primary research question addresses this latter objective what constitutes an optimal methodology for utilizing negative examples to enhance the robustness of language models regarding target syntactic constructions

given a query sequence q q dots q n comprising n tokens and a context sequence c c dots c n comprising n tokens the proposed hybrid architecture initially generates contextualized representations for the concatenated input langle q c rangle this input consisting of n n tokens is processed by a pretrained language model e g bert devlin et al to yield a representation matrix l text lm q c subsequently l is fed into a set of specialized output heads each implemented as a small neural network designed to estimate the conditional probability p a mid q c h where h denotes a specific answer type the architecture incorporates the following heads context span head utilizes a feedforward network text ff c to compute a probability distribution over all possible spans within the context question span head employs a feedforward network text ff q to compute a distribution over spans within the query count head applies a feedforward network text ff text cnt to generate a distribution over the integer set dots arithmetic head leverages a feedforward network text ff text cmb to compute a distribution over all signed combinations of numerical entities identified within the context during a preprocessing phase while the span based heads are standard in extractive question answering the count and arithmetic heads are specialized modules designed to address answers that are not explicitly present in the input text to determine the appropriate head for a given input a dedicated type head text ff text typ outputs a probability distribution p text head h mid q c over the set of available heads consequently the overall probability of an answer a given the query and context is defined as the marginal probability across all heads p a mid q c sum h in text heads p text head h mid q c cdot p a mid q c h the model is trained by enumerating all valid derivations of the target answer across the available heads and maximizing this marginal probability although existing models demonstrate strong performance on benchmarks such as drop the aforementioned architecture exhibits limitations regarding flexibility

to address the increased complexity of the task it is observed that while speech intonation serves as a significant feature for general question detection the discrimination of specific question types is facilitated by access to transcribed keywords the inclusion of the auxiliary binary classification task multiqtmt did not yield statistically significant improvements over the base multiqt model this outcome is hypothesized to result from training on a subset of the available questions thereby introducing unlabelled instances into the training data that act as noise for the binary classification objective furthermore the application of tensor fusion as opposed to the concatenation of unimodal representations failed to produce significant performance gains for multiqt a finding that contrasts with the results reported by zadeh et al given that tensor fusion inherently subsumes concatenated unimodal representations by incorporating all element wise products it is concluded that the multimodal interactions captured by these products either already exist within the unimodal representations due to correlation are readily learnable from them or are too complex for the multiqt architecture to capture the most probable explanation is that these interactions are easily learnable from the unimodal inputs comparative analysis of multiqt variants against instance and timestep f scores indicates that the instance metric is more lenient generally yielding higher values additionally performance differentials between various modality combinations are more pronounced when evaluated per instance rather than per timestep the random forest rf and feedforward neural network fnn baseline models demonstrated clear underperformance relative to multiqt notably both rf and fnn achieved f scores of approximately when evaluated per input utterance aligning with their training conditions on this specific metric the fnn model outperformed the rf model however both baselines exhibited significant performance degradation when transitioning from the training setting to the streaming setting as measured by the instance and timestep metrics this discrepancy was most severe for the fnn model regarding real time tracking a primary application of multiqt involves the real time labeling of streamed audio sequences and their associated transcripts consequently the system must be capable of processing audio segments in a duration shorter than the temporal span of the audio itself statistical note all reported significance levels were determined using an approximate randomization test with r iterations and bonferroni correction dror et al boldface values denote the highest f score within each respective metric and multiqt model group

alternatively if the objective is to prevent an algorithm from generating specific outputs the mechanism by which the system identifies and excludes such scenarios must be defined the concept of correctness primarily addresses the consistency between an algorithm and its underlying theoretical model rather than its alignment with real world contexts for instance a previously discussed autocomplete model returned all word completions matching a partial input string while technically consistent with its model this output is often impractical a user typing the letter a would be presented with an exhaustive list of all words beginning with that letter in the absence of a defined ordering mechanism the prevalence of irrelevant suggestions could lead to user frustration one potential remedy involves implementing a relevancy metric wherein the frequency of a word s completion by users increases its priority in future suggestions however as safiya noble argues in algorithms of oppression determining relevance through such universalizing metrics can yield detrimental social consequences for example search engine autosuggestions driven by popularity metrics have historically propagated harmful stereotypes including assertions that women cannot drive hold religious office or be trusted and that they should remain domestic or be controlled noble s critique further examines the intersection of social identities such as race and gender within the outputs of algorithms that underpin daily life furthermore as input data volumes expand algorithms often require increased time and storage resources for execution this phenomenon known as computational complexity is defined by the relationship between the resources an algorithm consumes and the size of its input increased computational time frequently correlates with higher energy consumption given the exponential growth in demand for data processing the design of efficient algorithms holds not only practical significance but also existential importance as the computing sector contributes directly to global warming and the resulting climate crisis consideration of content moderation online social media platforms facilitate interpersonal relationships by enabling users to create and share content consequently the management of this user generated content necessitates the implementation of moderation protocols to regulate interactions and material shared within the platform ecosystem

we show that this rescoring leads to consistent improvement in model performance across several tasks at no additional training or data requirements calibration framework for structured prediction nlp models background structured prediction refers to the task of predicting a structured output y for an input x in nlp a wide array of tasks including parsing information extraction and extractive question answering fall within this category recent approaches towards solving such tasks are commonly based on neural networks that are trained by minimizing the following objective l θ d − i log p θ y i x i r θ where θ is the parameter vector of the neural network and r is the regularization penalty and d is the dataset y i x i d i the trained model p θ can then be used to produce the output ˆ y argmax y ∈y p θ y x here the corresponding model probability p θ ˆ y x is the uncalibrated confidence score in binary class classification the output space y is the confidence score for such classifiers can then be calibrated by training a forecaster f y → which takes in the model confidence f y p θ y x to produce a recalibrated score a widely used method for binary class calibration is platt scaling where f y is a logistic regression model similar methods have also been defined for multiclass classification however extending this to structured prediction in nlp settings is nontrivial since the output space y is often too large for us to calibrate the output probabilities of all events related work calibration methods for binary multi class classification has been widely studied in related literature

effective professional communication requires a clear distinction between fact and opinion with the latter rigorously supported by empirical evidence for example asserting that a specific calculator model is superior constitutes an opinion this claim must be substantiated by factual data such as pricing independent consumer testing reports and technical specifications writers must prioritize clarity and precision to prevent misinterpretation arising from ambiguous or vague phrasing ambiguity undermines the persuasiveness of an argument and may lead to multiple conflicting interpretations the objective is to persuade the reader through a consistent well supported viewpoint rather than presenting assertions that contradict the broader document consider the following example of ambiguity before accepting materials from new subcontractors they should meet our requirements it is unclear whether they refers to the materials or the subcontractors a revised unambiguous version would read before we accept them the materials from the new subcontractors must meet our requirements similarly specificity is essential for effective problem solving phrases such as several weeks are vague and necessitate further clarification whereas three weeks provides precise information directness is further enhanced by the use of active verbs which facilitate quicker comprehension compared to the passive voice for instance heated wires vaporize water molecules is more engaging and direct than water molecules are vaporized by heated wires likewise thin metal caps close the cylinder at both ends is preferable to the cylinder is closed at both ends by thin metal caps coherence is fundamental at the sentence paragraph and document levels each sentence should logically connect to the preceding and succeeding sentences while every paragraph must begin with a clear main point supported by linked reinforcing details furthermore the aesthetic presentation of a document significantly influences reader perception standard formatting practices include maintaining one inch margins on all sides and utilizing a ragged right margin rather than full justification to improve readability finally the selection of a familiar legible typeface—whether serif or sans serif—is critical for ensuring the text is easily accessible to the audience

formal overview of concurrent engineering and design optimization the production phase frequently necessitates modifications to initial plans incurring both direct costs and manufacturing delays concurrent engineering serves as a strategic methodology to mitigate these inefficiencies by compressing the manufacturing cycle thereby accelerating the reliable introduction of new products to the market as illustrated in figure while concurrent engineering demands a higher rate of expenditure during the early stages of the cycle the aggregate cost—represented by the area under the curve—remains lower than that of traditional sequential engineering models adopting concurrent engineering requires a fundamental paradigm shift in management this transition extends beyond financial considerations to encompass a collaborative decision making framework that integrates personnel from diverse functional areas consequently organizations must allocate resources for training and collaborative meetings these activities are essential for dismantling traditional hierarchical structures and fostering an environment where meaningful input is solicited and valued from design manufacturing and sales departments this heightened emphasis on cross functional teamwork represents a defining characteristic of modern engineering enterprises rendering the concept of the isolated engineer obsolete by leveraging collective creativity organizations can enhance the design process from conceptualization through to finalization in the realm of design optimization selecting a superior solution is straightforward only when a single option clearly dominates across all criteria however engineering challenges often present competing designs with distinct advantages and disadvantages necessitating a rigorous analysis of trade offs among cost material selection reliability and performance this multi criteria approach contrasts sharply with evaluation models relying on a single metric such as cost alone to elucidate the principles of optimum design one may apply the framework to the evaluation of consumer products such as selecting a preferred potato chip beverage or confectionery while not all technical design phases apply directly to this context the core analytical steps remain relevant the problem statement is defined as identifying the optimal product the investigation phase is typically minimal relying on established consumer preferences during the ideation phase the focus shifts to specific attributes generating a criteria list that may include saltiness texture thickness dimensions nutritional content cost appearance and flavor this structured evaluation mirrors the complex trade off analysis required in professional engineering design

smith involves assigning values to multiple interdependent variables certain joint assignments may be prohibited by constraints designed by domain experts as a simple example in the problem of extracting entities and relations from text a constraint could disallow the relation “married to” between two entities if one of the entity is not a “person” it has been shown that carefully designed constraints can substantially improve model performance in various applications especially when the number of training examples is limited designing constraints often requires taskspecific manual effort in this paper we ask the question can we use neural network methods to automatically discover constraints from data and use them to predict structured outputs we provide a general framework for discovering constraints in the form of a system of linear inequalities over the output variables in a problem these constraints can improve an already trained model or be integrated into the learning process for global training a system of linear inequalities represents a bounded or unbounded convex polytope we observe that such a system can be expressed as a twolayer threshold network i e a network with one hidden layer of linear threshold units and an output layer with a single threshold unit this twolayer threshold network will predict or − depending on whether the system of linear inequalities is satisfied or not in principle we could try to train such a threshold network to discover constraints however the zerogradient nature of the threshold activation function prohibits using backpropagation for gradientbased learning instead in this paper we show that a construction of a specific twolayer rectifier network represents linear inequality constraints this network also contains a single linear threshold output unit but in the hidden layer it contains rectified linear units relus pan and srikumar showed that a twolayer rectifier network constructed in such a way is equivalent to a threshold network and represents the same set of linear inequalities as the threshold network with far fewer hidden units the linear constraints thus obtained can augment existing models in multiple ways

this study adopts the definition of computational thinking ct established by the k– computer science framework characterizing ct as the thought processes involved in expressing solutions as computational steps or algorithms that can be carried out by a computer guided by learning trajectories lts the research team operationalized ct across four core conceptual domains sequence repetition conditionals and decomposition consequently this study defines ct as the cognitive processes students employ when formulating computational solutions within these specific domains with the development of these lts alongside the k– cs framework and csta standards a critical subsequent step involves investigating how students demonstrate ct proficiency within these structures such inquiry is essential for educators curriculum developers and tool designers to delineate grade level expectations analyze the intersection of ct concepts as expertise develops and identify prevalent understandings and misconceptions during elementary computer science instruction accordingly the primary objective of this study was to employ cognitive interviewing techniques to qualitatively examine elementary students ct understandings utilizing the ct learning trajectories proposed by rich et al given the significant variability among elementary learners and the scarcity of theoretical and empirical evidence regarding their conceptual grasp of ct the scope of this investigation was limited to a single grade level rather than a cross grade analysis drawing upon the work of maloney confrey and nguyen regarding learning trajectories the study was guided by the following research questions how do fourth grade students express and articulate ct understanding within the domains of sequence repetition decomposition and conditionals to what extent do students ct understandings align with the hypothetical cognitive progression outlined in the learning trajectories in the context of mathematics research and practice a learning trajectory lt is defined as a hypothetical sequence of milestones that learners encounter as they advance toward greater sophistication the hypothetical nature of an lt arises because such progressions are typically conceptualized based on observations of specific often small cohorts since prior knowledge and engagement levels cannot be fully predicted an lt serves as an a priori model of potential learning progression for diverse student populations

the proposed methodology proceeds through three distinct stages first continuous importance scores are heuristically discretized into binary labels second an extraction module is employed to generate a refined dataset consisting solely of extracted rationales this module may be implemented either as a parameterized sequence tagging model trained on the pseudotargets derived in the initial stage or as a heuristic function operating directly on the importance scores the resulting dataset comprises pairs of extracted rationales and their corresponding labels third a final classifier is trained exclusively on this rationale based dataset thereby ensuring that the model s decision making process relies solely on the extracted explanatory features this approach addresses significant challenges within current natural language processing nlp explainability frameworks recent scholarship has critically examined the efficacy of methods that generate token level heatmaps to justify model predictions specifically existing feature attribution techniques have been shown to lack robustness and faithfulness feng et al jain and wallace wiegreffe and pinter serrano and smith brunner et al zhong et al pruthi et al in response wiegreffe and pinter propose a taxonomy distinguishing between faithfulness and plausibility in model interpretability regarding the quality of rationales lei et al establish that ideal explanations should be sufficient concise and coherent yu et al expand upon these criteria by introducing comprehensiveness asserting that a rationale must encompass all relevant and useful information to achieve faithful explanations prior research lei et al yu et al bastings et al has utilized a two model architecture wherein a generator network imposes hard attention on inputs before passing them to a secondary prediction model yu et al further refine this framework by incorporating a third adversarial model however the joint training of such systems presents optimization challenges due to the discrete nature of hard attention often necessitating the use of reinforcement learning techniques such as reinforce williams or the reparameterization trick bastings et al finally the evaluation of interpretability often relies on human assessment kim et al posit that a method is interpretable if a user can accurately predict its output a hypothesis they validated through user studies similarly other researchers have investigated the plausibility of explanations by measuring human preference for specific rationales ehsan et al
