the first proposal aligns closely with the methodology outlined in which utilized keyword clusters to construct a semantic space for information retrieval tasks however the present approach offers a simplified alternative it eschews linguistic data regarding inter word relationships relying instead exclusively on links derived through statistical analysis of a document corpus the second proposal extends the work found in which employed a composite measure of document similarity similarly our method streamlines this process by applying a logarithmic transformation to term frequencies a technique grounded in the information theoretic dimensions of the problem for the clustering of keywords the majorclust algorithm was employed furthermore this study addresses the limitations inherent in keyword grouping and the necessity for rigorous keyword selection preliminary keyword selection within the traditional vector space model framework the stochastic nature of document presentation is often overlooked word occurrences are treated as fixed values an assumption that holds validity for lengthy documents the standard procedure involves identifying all terms within a collection filtering out stopwords and consolidating words sharing a common lemma using techniques such as the porter stemming algorithm however this approach requires modification when applied to abstracts in such short texts the random occurrence of one or two instances of a low frequency word can artificially double its frequency count consequently due to the stochastic nature of these occurrences the estimation error becomes comparable to the frequency itself to enhance the statistical confidence regarding low frequency terms a word selection process is necessary we adopt the criterion introduced in wherein a word w is included in the index keyword list only if it satisfies the following inequality frac f text dom w f text gen w k here f text dom w and f text gen w represent the frequencies of word w within the specific document collection and a general balanced reference corpus of the target language respectively the parameter k is determined empirically and corresponds to the statistical estimation of the mean error in frequency measurement arising from the limited sample size for low frequency words in short texts a value of k greater than or is generally advisable in our practical application we utilized k to ensure result stability

this work proposes a framework for modeling latent fine grained discrete control states denoted as z z t where each state z t is associated with a label from a set c these states are assumed to be weakly supervised during the training phase via problem specific constraints the primary objective is to induce a generative model for the conditional distribution p y x by marginalizing over the latent states p y x sum z p y z x specifically the experimental evaluation focuses on a data to text generation task in this context x represents a structured data table and y t denotes the corresponding textual description the latent control states z are intended to identify the specific table fields being described at each step while the weak supervision is provided through indicators of known alignments between the text and the data the generative process is modeled as a black box autoregressive decoder that jointly produces the output sequence y and the latent sequence z the joint probability distribution is defined as p theta y z x prod t t p theta y t x y t z leq t cdot p theta z t x y t z t for a neural decoder implementation let h t y t z t represent the hidden state at timestep t the generation of the latent class z t in c and the subsequent token y t is parameterized as follows p theta z t z t y t text softmax w h t b p theta y t z leq t y t text softmax w b in these equations g theta denotes a parameterized embedding function while w and b represent the model parameters contained within theta the model is trained by maximizing the log likelihood of the observed data defined as mathcal l theta log p theta y x a central component of this framework is the posterior distribution p theta z x y which represents the probability of latent state sequences given a known input and output due to the specific parameterization of the decoder computing this posterior distribution is generally intractable

differentiation through the use of the critical incident technique and a delphi process we have identified a small number of scenarios that can be used in seminars at centers for teacher training to foster novice teachers’ reflective thinking while some scenarios might arise in other subjects as well they are described specific to computer science classrooms we have related the scenarios to the literature in computing education research the design implementation and evaluation of such a seminar is subject of future work the findings are not necessarily surprising given that most of the scenarios can be attributed to areas in which research has been conducted however they confirm that these issues exists not only in introductory college courses the main focus of previous research and and to generalizations beyond the context of our study on the other hand the academic and professional background as well as the multiyear experience of the participating expert teachers supports the role our participants were asked to assume when recalling critical incidents may have been less prone to biases than the role assumed in other qualitative studies however we caution the reader to nlp is not however without its drawbacks and chief among these are the technical knowledge required to utilise the models and a need to account for potential biases this all means that the introduction of nlp to police agencies will have to be carefully considered with biases understood quantified and addressed in ways that minimise undue harm generally speaking police agencies do not have the expertise to do this themselves and private providers who might offer such expertise often have a vested interests in protecting their technologies which in turn reduces transparency as such it is incumbent on the academic community to investigate how nlp might support such policing efforts and better understand how the aforementioned challenges might be met prior to them manifesting in negative outcomes if applied correctly and with appropriate safeguards nlp has the potential to unlock the power of preventionfocused policing techniques thereby reducing crime and the diverse societal harms associated with its occurrence we thank the anonymous reviewers for their insightful and helpful comments which have undoubtedly served to enhance the paper this research was supported by the economic and social research council the ongoing covid pandemic resulted in significant ramifications for international relations ranging from travel restrictions global ceasefires and international vaccine production and sharing agreements amidst a wave of infections in india that resulted in a systemic breakdown of healthcare infrastructure a social welfare organization based in pakistan offered to procure medicalgrade oxygen to assist india a nation which was involved in four wars with pakistan in the past few decades in this paper we focus on pakistani twitter users’ response to the ongoing healthcare crisis in india while indianeedsoxygen and pakistanstandswithindia featured among the toptrending hashtags in pakistan divisive hashtags such as endiasaysorrytokashmir simultaneously started trending against the backdrop of a contentious history including four wars divisive content of this nature especially when a country is facing an unprecedented healthcare crisis fuels further deterioration of relations in this paper we define a new task of detecting supportive content and demonstrate that existing nlp for social impact tools can be effectively harnessed for such tasks within a quick turnaround time we also release the first publicly available data set at the intersection of geopolitical relations and a raging pandemic in the context of india and pakistan

our approach to this problem is to use the sampling and occlusion soc explanation algorithm which estimates modelagnostic posthoc feature importance we apply this approach to the gab hate corpus a new corpus labeled for “hatebased rhetoric” and an annotated corpus from the stormfront white supremacist online forum based on the explanations generated via soc which showed models were biased towards group identifiers we then propose a novel regularizationbased approach in order to increase model sensitivity to the context surrounding group identifiers we apply regularization during training to the explanationbased importance of group identifiers coercing models to consider the context surrounding them we find that regularization reduces the attention given to group identifiers and heightens the importance of the more generalizable features of hate speech such as dehumanizing and insulting language in experiments on an outofdomain test set of news articles containing group identifiers which are heuristically assumed to be nonhate speech we find that regularization greatly reduces the false positive rate while indomain outofsample classification performance is either maintained or improved related work our work is conceptually influenced by warner and hirschberg who formulated hate speech detection as disambiguating the use of offensive words from abusive versus nonabusive contexts more recent approaches applied to a wide typology of hate speech build supervised models trained on annotated or heuristicallylabeled data these models suffer from the highly skewed distributions of language in these datasets research on bias in classification models also influences this work dixon et al measured and mitigated bias in toxicity classifiers towards social groups avoiding undesirable predictions of toxicity towards innocuous sentences containing tokens like “ gay ” similarly annotators’ biases towards certain social groups were found to be magnified during classifier training mostafazadeh davani et al

given a document d with n sentences s · · · s n algorithm gives the detailed procedure of constructing the semantic graph based on dependency parsing to better represent the entity connection within the document we first employ the coreference resolution system of allennlp to replace the pronouns that refer to the same entity with its original entity name for each sentence s we employ the allennlp implementation of the biaffine attention model to obtain its dependency parse tree t s afterwards we perform the following operations to refine the tree • identify node types each node in the dependency parse tree is a word associated with a pos tag to simplify the node type system we manually categorize the pos types into three groups verb noun and attribute each node is then assigned to one group as its node type • prune tree we then prune each tree by removing unimportant continents e g punctuation based on predefined grammar rules specifically we do this recursively from top to bottom where for each node v we visit each of its child node c if c needs to be pruned we delete c and directly link each child node of c to v • merge nodes each node in the tree represents only one word which may lead to a large and noisy semantic graph especially for long documents to ensure that the semantic graph only retains important semantic relations we merge consecutive nodes that form a complete semantic unit to be specific we apply a simple yet effective rule merging a node v with its child c if they form a consecutive modifier i e both the type of v and c are modifier and v and c is consecutive in the sentence after obtaining the refined dependency parse tree t s for each sentence s we add intratree edges to construct the semantic graph by connecting the nodes that are similar but from different parse trees

abstract this paper introduces the text based ideal point tbip model an unsupervised probabilistic topic modeling framework designed to quantify the political positions of authors through the analysis of textual data the efficacy of the tbip is demonstrated using two distinct datasets comprising politicized text speeches from the u s senate and tweets from senators although the model operates independently of voting records or explicit party affiliations it successfully differentiates lawmakers by party generates interpretable politicized topics and infers ideal points that closely align with classical vote based estimates a primary advantage of this text centric approach over traditional vote based analysis is its capacity to estimate the ideal points of any entity producing political discourse including non voting actors consequently the model is applied to the democratic presidential primary where it successfully maps candidates along a coherent spectrum ranging from progressive to moderate based solely on their tweet content introduction ideal point models have long served as a standard methodology for characterizing modern democracies by analyzing legislative voting records to estimate the positions of lawmakers on a political spectrum poole and rosenthal however voting behavior represents only one dimension of political expression press releases social media posts and speeches also convey significant political preferences like voting records these textual signals are systematically recorded and readily accessible this study develops the text based ideal point tbip model a probabilistic topic model engineered to analyze unstructured political texts and quantify the underlying preferences of their authors while classical ideal point models examine how individuals vote on a shared set of legislation the tbip analyzes how different authors articulate a shared set of latent topics the model is grounded in the theory of political framing which posits that the specific lexical choices employed when discussing a topic can convey distinct political messages entman given a corpus of political texts the tbip estimates the latent topics under discussion the latent political positions of the authors and the variation in word choice per topic as a function of the author s political stance a defining characteristic of the tbip is its unsupervised nature it can be applied to any political text corpus regardless of whether the authors belong to established political parties furthermore it facilitates the analysis of non voting actors such as political candidates figure illustrates an application of the tbip to the speeches of the th u s senate

concurrently the application of alignment constraints facilitates the extraction of additional information from the source context thereby enhancing the training efficacy of non autoregressive nar models a notable observation is that nar models in text to speech tts tasks derive the most significant benefit from attention constraints as evidenced in table furthermore tts exhibits the lowest attention density ratio as illustrated in figure collectively these findings suggest that nar models characterized by limited target token dependency are particularly well suited to leverage alignment constraints related work existing literature has extensively analyzed and sought to understand nar models across various tasks this discussion categorizes these analyses into two primary dimensions knowledge distillation and source target alignment constraints knowledge distillation knowledge distillation has long been established as a technique for model compression hinton et al furlanello et al yang et al anil et al li et al and for transferring knowledge from teacher to student models tan et al liu et al a b subsequently this approach was adapted to nar models to improve accuracy gu et al oord et al guo et al a wang et al li et al b guo et al b ren et al several studies have investigated the underlying mechanisms of knowledge distillation phuong and lampert examined linear and deep linear classifiers concluding that data geometry optimization bias and strong monotonicity are critical determinants of distillation success similarly yuan et al posited that the efficacy of knowledge distillation stems from the regularization effect of soft targets which may be as significant as the inter class similarity information despite these insights few studies have specifically addressed why knowledge distillation benefits nar training recently zhou et al investigated the role of knowledge distillation in nar models for neural machine translation nmt finding that it reduces dataset complexity and aids the model in learning output variations additionally li et al b explored the causes of suboptimal performance in nar models by analyzing attention distributions and hidden states

a critical component of assessment design involves the precise definition of learning objectives and the establishment of corresponding metrics for their evaluation in the domain of computational thinking ct these objectives encompass both conceptual understanding—specifically regarding sequences iteration conditionals parallelism and data structures—and the proficiency to engage in core practices such as abstraction modularization reuse remixing and testing and debugging these practices represent competencies that learners must be able to demonstrate execute and articulate across diverse contexts while numerous existing assessments prioritize knowledge of programming and ct concepts they often neglect the evaluation of practical engagement although the integrated assessment of concepts and practices is theoretically optimal isolating the assessment of practices offers distinct advantages this approach mirrors the common practice of assessing concepts in isolation thereby preventing the conflation of theoretical knowledge with the ability to apply that knowledge within a computational solution despite this validated instruments for assessing ct practices remain scarce with a notable deficiency in resources tailored to early education levels this paper outlines the development of a validated ct practices assessment and its associated rubrics designed for students in grades through primary – in the hong kong context the instrument was constructed using a principled assessment design framework a defining characteristic of this assessment is its independence from specific programming representations by shifting the focus from particular programming structures to underlying ct practices the tool remains applicable across various computer science curricula and programming languages the development of this assessment was conducted as part of a broader evaluation of coolthink jc a four year pilot initiative in hong kong schools aimed at teaching programming fostering problem solving skills and cultivating interest in computing among students aged to findings from an initial implementation of the assessment highlight prevalent student challenges thereby contributing to the literature on ct comprehension and underscoring the necessity of emphasizing specific ct practices within curricula and teacher preparation programs current scholarship on k– ct assessment is expanding however many efforts remain centered on programming seeking evidence of learning primarily through the final artifacts of student coding relying solely on the presence or absence of specific programming constructs can yield inaccurate indicators of student knowledge and fails to capture the computational processes or ct practices employed to achieve the final product

summary of contributions in summary this work presents three primary contributions first we introduce a novel and practical task generating questions from product reviews in the absence of annotated instances a paradigm with significant potential for diverse applications second to address the challenge of data scarcity inherent to this task we propose a new framework featuring adaptive instance transfer and augmentation third we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of review based question generation across a wide range of product categories within e commerce datasets related work question generation qg has emerged as a prominent research area driven by its broad applicability in domains such as education wang et al goal oriented dialogue lee et al and question answering duan et al early neural qg models du et al zhou et al du and cardie demonstrated superior performance compared to rule based methods reliant on handcrafted features subsequent research has further enhanced model capabilities by incorporating factors such as question type dong et al answer position sun et al long passage modeling zhao et al b question difficulty gao et al and context relevance li et al additionally some studies have focused on identifying potential answer text spans to facilitate the learning process wang et al qg models are frequently integrated with their dual task reading comprehension or question answering for various objectives including improving auxiliary task performance duan et al yang et al golub et al fostering collaboration between qa and qg models tang et al and enabling unified learning frameworks xiao et al while qg has been applied to diverse datasets such as wikipedia du and cardie the majority of existing literature treats it as a dual task of reading comprehension yu et al cui et al in this standard formulation a question is generated from a text segment where a specific span is explicitly marked as the answer notable exceptions to this approach involve generating questions from sentences without designated answer spans du et al chali and baghaee

the subject under discussion represents a sophisticated programming environment characterized by advanced logical capabilities including recursion higher order functions complex data structures object oriented programming and lambda calculus despite its technical depth which has proven viable in commercial applications the platform utilizes a visual block based interface that minimizes syntactic complexity this design leverages visual metaphors to render powerful computational concepts accessible to novices empirical research corroborates this pedagogical approach indicating that students in block based curricula outperform their peers in comparable text based programs and demonstrate heightened interest in pursuing further studies in computing computer science cs is fundamentally a discipline comprising a body of knowledge and cognitive frameworks that enable the creation of hardware and algorithms to address complex challenges such as data sorting natural language processing and machine learning furthermore cs develops the languages and tools necessary for constructing applications across diverse domains including productivity healthcare entertainment scientific research and the arts given that programming is inherently creative and experiential the act of creation must constitute a central component of student learning adopting a project based learning model does not imply that the completion of a specific project is the sole objective if the goal were merely the production of a finished artifact prescriptive step by step instructions would suffice rather the primary aim is to cultivate the capacity for students to conceive and execute independent projects thereby fostering a sense of agency and problem solving competence this requires the acquisition of tools and techniques that facilitate improvisation consequently project based learning is defined as the mastery of essential reusable methodologies through the construction of functional artifacts rather than through isolated exercises this framework necessitates a rigorous selection of instructional content concepts that while intriguing lack reusability within the constraints of the curriculum are deemed unsuitable for instruction merely exposing students to a variety of advanced concepts without providing adequate time for assimilation and mastery is considered ineffective instead the curriculum emphasizes the iterative nature of development where student work is viewed as perpetually extensible and improvable projects are often structured in stages allowing learners to implement foundational elements with current knowledge and expand upon them as their expertise grows ultimately this approach conveys that work is never finished but flawed rather work that does not yet meet desired standards is simply incomplete there is no absolute state of perfection as all creations remain subject to revision and refinement

during the fall semester the committee convened multiple sessions to articulate programmatic aspirations define desired learning outcomes evaluate comparable undergraduate curricula at peer institutions and determine the feasibility of launching a new program throughout these deliberations the ad hoc committee naturally coalesced into two distinct factions one faction advocated for a data analytics program focused on training students to utilize established off the shelf tools for processing large scale disciplinary data the alternative faction supported a data science program designed not only to teach the application of existing tools but also to equip students with the competency to develop custom solutions when necessary following extensive debate the committee resolved to pursue the data science model a primary determinant in this decision was the recognition that many analytical tools are domain specific proficiency with a tool in one field does not readily translate to another consequently the committee concluded that the curriculum must prioritize general purpose methodologies to ensure skill transferability a critical consideration during the planning phase was the number of new courses required the introduction of new coursework necessitates either the reallocation of existing faculty resources or the recruitment of new personnel both of which entail significant financial and administrative costs given these constraints proposals requiring a high volume of new courses face a lower probability of approval at the institution compared to those that leverage existing coursework therefore there is a strategic imperative to minimize the creation of new courses by maximizing the integration of current offerings furthermore the development of a new university program demands substantial time and effort often resulting in proposals spanning hundreds of pages that must undergo a rigorous review process consequently the committee exercised caution regarding the investment of resources required to draft a comprehensive proposal particularly given the uncertainty of its ultimate approval

presence and consistency has enabled us to develop trust among the teachers who see us as credible and reliable supporters of their classroom activities resources since teachers have many demands on their time there is a need to help support and assist them this creates many opportunities for stars students to contribute they run computing clubs and develop cs ct course materials in return this helps them to develop leadership and professional skills additionally and perhaps most importantly the teacher leadership team is offered stipends to recognize their efforts and time commitments curriculum reform one approach that we took to help improve teacher selfefficacy is through professional development workshops we found that when developing these workshops for middle school teachers it is important to address how to adapt and integrate new tools into specific classroom lessons for this reason we offered examples templates and supporting delivery further when developing course materials we spent time discussing with the teachers to ensure that the materials were clearly in line with curriculum requirements this paper is a work in progress report which describes our experience in starting an rpp with a middle school this school is comprised primarily of students from groups that are underrepresented in stem and in particular in computer science the goal of this partnership is to help infuse cs ct into the school’s curriculum with the larger goal of broadening participation in computing our approach is based on an ecosystem model where various aspects of the community contribute to student success in particular we focus on how higher education institutions can contribute in this paper we described how the partnership began and our activities in the first year we found that there are barriers related to the difficulty of inserting new curriculum and we should focus on modifying existing curriculum we found that relationship building is key and used all opportunities to be present and to be part of the conversation we found that resources are important drivers and stipends for pd and teacher leaders provide a way to recognize the effort of the teachers beyond their normal duties our key lessons derived from our first year highlight on the importance of relationship development and leveraging teacher champions to build trust

fake news detection is almost universally approached as a classification task either as a binary classification more often or a multilabel task a binary classification problem has the goal to classify the instances of a given set into two categories for example in fake news detection as a binary classification task the goal is to differentiate fake news from real news however if a problem is concerned with more than two groups it is a multiclass task for example detecting a stance between a news headline and the whole text of the news article is a multiclass task since there are more than two labels involved such as agree disagree unrelated etc in general studies on fake news detection used different metrics to evaluate the performance of the presented methods creating some inconvenience compared to the results among different works for example we observe studies reporting precision recall accuracy f score and rocauc to evaluate the performance of various models trained on balanced datasets in contrast to the balanced datasets multiple studies have reported precision recall along with the f score for the fake class and rocauc to examine the overall system quality and evaluate the model performance in addition to this some studies also calculated microf scores for highly unbalanced datasets finally we noticed that most of the studies used the f score to measure the model’s performance since most of the datasets are unbalanced this work provides the first overview of various publicly available datasets for automatic fake news detection in asian and european languages most of which are poor resource languages providing comparative statistics on their sizes grouping them by the length and source of content news and surveying dataset annotation procedures we have also surveyed the approaches used in the studies on fake news detection in lowresource languages and grouped them into studies using traditional machine learning and neural network approaches we note that working on asian languages with more resources notably chinese demonstrates wider adoption of neural networks and achieves better results with those finally we provide a brief overview of the evaluation metrics used to report fake news detection performance it is important to note that due to a large variety of metrics available some studies choose to report different metrics than others which leads to difficulties in comparison among studies

word encoder the word encoder is designed to map a target word w into a low dimensional vector representation r this module comprises two primary components a word embedding layer and a character level encoder the word embedding layer is initialized using large scale pretrained embeddings such as glove pennington et al or fasttext bojanowski et al and remains fixed throughout the training process to incorporate morphological information which has been shown to enhance definition generation noraset et al ishiwatari et al a convolutional neural network cnn krizhevsky et al is employed to encode the character sequence of the word the final word representation r is obtained by concatenating the static word embedding with the learned character level encoding context encoder for context encoding a standard bidirectional long short term memory bi lstm network sundermeyer et al is utilized this network processes the sequence of word embeddings corresponding to the context c c c and generates a corresponding sequence of hidden states h h c semantic components predictor in the proposed esd framework it is necessary to model both the posterior distribution of semantic components q phi z mid w c d and the prior distribution p theta z mid w c semantic components posterior approximator directly modeling the true posterior q phi z mid w c d is generally intractable consequently an approximation method is adopted to simplify posterior inference zhang et al following the variational autoencoder vae paradigm bowman et al neural networks are employed to achieve a more effective approximation specifically the definition sequence d d t is first processed by a bidirectional lstm network to compute its representation h d h t

more recent studies have also explored how students engaged in plancomposition when explicitly taught planning strategies such as de raadt et al ’s work on teaching students a strategy guide for integrating plans muller et al talks about patternoriented instruction in which students are taught to attach labels to programming patterns and to look for common patterns across problems our work additionally looks at how students navigate between tasks and code in the context of having a multistep design recipe for approaching problems particularly in situations when they get stuck in their programming process perhaps due to not having plans or patterns to retrieve for novel problemtasks this work follows our existing line of work on understanding the programming processes of cs students taught with the htdp curriculum in an earlier exploratory study we analyzed video captures of students’ ide as they worked on a multitask problem and found that while students primarily work in terms of problemtasks they struggled to decompose and compose the code around these tasks students also used familiar code patterns verbatim without adjusting the patterns to the need of the tasks that study however lacked richer data on which observations can be drawn about how students were thinking around the problem why they struggled in their process and how they tried to get unstuck in a followup study we collected thinkaloud data as students solved the rainfall problem and found that students who connected specific parts of their solutions to specific tasks and maintained those connections throughout their process produced more correct code this is a key finding in this work as well additionally our work considers what happens around taskand codelevel thinking when students get a problem with entire tasks that they have not seen before how to design programs is an introductory computing curriculum that teaches students a multistep design recipe dr for designing programs based on the structure of the input data given a programming problem students are taught to work through a progression of steps identify and define the structure of the input data write examples of the input data as executable code write the name input types and output type the signature for a function that will solve the problem and a brief summary of the function’s goal the purpose statement

the task of semantic role labeling srl is to identify what semantic relations hold among a predicate and its associated participants and properties including “who” did “what” to “whom” etc for each sentence we extract predicateargument tuples via srl toolkits each tuple forms a subgraph where each tuple element e g arguments location and temporal is a node we add intertuple edges between nodes from different tuples if they have an inclusive relationship or potentially mention the same entity we employ the stateoftheart bertbased model in the allennlp toolkit to perform srl • dpbased semantic graph we employ the biaffine attention model for each sentence to obtain its dependency parse tree which is further revised by removing unimportant constituents e g punctuation and merging consecutive nodes that form a complete semantic unit afterwards we add intertree edges between similar nodes from different parse trees to construct a connected semantic graph the left side of figure shows an example of the dpbased semantic graph compared with srlbased graphs dpbased ones typically model more finegrained and sparse semantic relations as discussed in appendix a section gives a performance comparison on these two formalisms semanticenriched document representations we separately encode the document d and the semantic graph g via an rnnbased passage encoder and a novel attggnn graph encoder respectively then fuse them to obtain the semanticenriched document representations for question generation document encoding given the input document d we employ the bidirectional gated recurrent unit gru to encode its contexts we represent the encoder hidden states as x d where x i is the context embedding of w i as a concatenation of its bidirectional hidden states node initialization we define the srland dpbased semantic graphs in an unified way

the course is delivered utilizing a flipped classroom pedagogical model comprising two hour weekly laboratory sessions a series of structured assignments and a comprehensive semester long practical project the requisite technical infrastructure encompasses an industrial control systems ics testbed a capture the flag ctf environment e g ctfd or the kypo cyber range platform for student exercises and gitlab repositories for project management the curriculum leverages the extensive resources provided by the open source community including documentation learning materials and existing software projects to facilitate student comprehension of the relevant technologies the course structure is organized into three distinct modules fundamentals of ics development of an ics training game and presentation and submission of the final project module ics principles given the anticipated limited prior knowledge of ics among participants the initial session aims to establish motivation by analyzing historical cyber incidents such as stuxnet to illustrate the tangible real world impact of ics compromises this is followed by an exposition of key terminology including critical information infrastructure cii and an overview of pertinent legal frameworks such as national cybersecurity legislation assessment and laboratory activities week students are required to individually research a publicly documented ics cyberattack and present their findings in a minute session including q a during the subsequent class week the assigned reading consists of a primary research paper and associated literature cited in the related work section week students will engage with the ics testbed through hands on laboratories designed to familiarize them with the operational characteristics of its hardware and software components the session concludes with a group discussion on methodologies for replicating known attacks within the testbed environment week as an individual assignment students must compile a survey of existing ics security threat landscapes referencing resources such as the owasp internet of things project in the following session students will present their findings the cohort will then evaluate the severity of identified threats and assess their feasibility for demonstration on the kypoindustry testbed thereby delineating the platform s capabilities and limitations week the subsequent individual assignment requires the preparation of a one page written survey regarding ctf games within the ics domain

experimental setup throughout the training process word embeddings were held constant all neural architectures were trained for a maximum of epochs with the final model selected based on the epoch yielding the optimal validation performance to mitigate the effects of stochasticity inherent in neural network training and to ensure result stability each model was executed ten times using distinct random seeds the reported metrics represent the average performance across these trials results and analysis tables and present the experimental outcomes for the content type and speech label classification tasks the results indicate that a baseline word level bidirectional lstm bilstm classifier significantly outperforms the feature based support vector machine svm approach proposed by yarlott et al achieving improvements of and in macro and micro f scores respectively for content type classification the integration of a sentence level bilstm further enhanced performance by capturing contextual continuity yielding additional gains of in macro f and in micro f scores furthermore modeling the associations between sentence representations and the primary topic—utilizing either headline or document embeddings—resulted in incremental improvements to the average macro f score of and respectively empirical analysis suggests that the model leveraging document embeddings outperformed the headline based variant by this discrepancy likely reflects the prevalence of recency biased headline construction in news reporting finally attempts to further optimize performance by employing conditional random fields crf to capture interdependencies among content types were unsuccessful neither fine grained nor coarse grained label transition models surpassed the performance of the simple classifier this underperformance is attributed to the variability in news content organization structures e g inverted pyramid versus narrative formats suggesting that future research should focus on explicitly modeling these structural variations

subsequently to identify conversational turns that align with the target user s interests we propose a user aware attention mechanism applied at the turn level turn level modeling for each turn t within a conversation c we employ a convolutional neural network cnn encoder operating on pretrained word embeddings to derive a content representation denoted as z t this vector is then concatenated with the user embedding r uf u t of the turn s author u t resulting in a comprehensive turn level representation r t t this representation encapsulates both the semantic content of the utterance and the identity of the speaker conversation level modeling building upon these turn level representations we model turn interactions by leveraging two structural dimensions chronological order and reply topology chronological order to capture the sequential dependencies between a turn and its immediate temporal neighbors we utilize a bidirectional gated recurrent unit bigru the hidden states are updated as follows overrightarrow h c t text gru overrightarrow text gru overrightarrow h c t text gru r t t overleftarrow h c t text gru overleftarrow text gru overleftarrow h c t text gru r t t the forward and backward hidden states are subsequently concatenated to generate chronology encoded turn representations h c t text gru replying structure to explicitly encode the who replies to whom relationships inherent in conversation topology we apply a graph convolutional network gcn over the chronology encoded representations this approach is empirically superior to purely sequential models as reply relations typically exhibit a tree like structure where a single post may generate multiple replies specifically we construct a directed graph for the conversation by establishing edges from each turn to its corresponding replies within this graph turn interactions are modeled across three distinct directions predecessor to successor pre successor to predecessor suc and self interactions self

for instance a wellpredicted posterior can help assess the fairness of a recidivism prediction instrument or select the optimal number of labels in a diagnosis code prediction guo et al showed that a model with high classification accuracy does not guarantee good posterior estimation quality in order to correct the poorly calibrated posterior probability existing calibration methods generally rescale the posterior distribution predicted from the classifier after training such postprocessing calibration methods relearn an appropriate distribution from a heldout validation set and then apply it to an unseen test set causing a severe discrepancy in distributions across the data splits the fixed split of the data sets makes the postcalibration very limited and static with respect to the classifier’s performance we propose a simple but effective training technique called posterior calibrated poscal training that optimizes the task objective while calibrating the posterior distribution in training unlike the postprocessing calibration methods poscal directly penalizes the difference between the predicted and the true empirical posterior probabilities dynamically over the training steps poscal is not a simple substitute of the postprocessing calibration methods our experiment shows that poscal can not only reduce the calibration error but also increase the task performance on the classification benchmarks compared to the baseline mle maximum likelihood estimation training method poscal achieves performance improvements on glue and on xslue and at the same time posterior error reduction on glue and on xslue related work our work is primarily motivated by previous analyses of posterior calibration on modern neural networks guo et al pointed out that in some cases as the classification performance of neural networks improves its posterior output becomes poorly calibrated there are a few attempts to investigate the effect of posterior calibration on natural language processing nlp tasks nguyen and o’connor empirically tested how classifiers on nlp tasks e g sequence tagging are calibrated for instance compared to the naive bayes classifier logistic regression outputs wellcalibrated posteriors in sentiment classification task

beta text contradiction beta text unknown for each rule sentence s s s dots over multiple conversational turns two representative cases are illustrated in figure rules structured as bullet points figure a depicts a scenario where the rule text comprises a conjunction of four distinct bullet point conditions in the initial turn the model processes the scenario and initial question determining that the user s query is relevant to the rule text but insufficient to satisfy specific conditions consequently the entailment states for all rule sentences are classified as unknown prompting the model to generate an inquire decision upon receiving a positive response from the user regarding the first inquiry the model updates the state of rule sentence s from unknown to entailment however as the dialogue cannot yet be concluded a second follow up question is generated the subsequent negative response from the user leads the model to render a final decision of no in the third turn rules in plain text figure b presents a more complex case involving rules expressed in continuous prose in this context it is not feasible to formulate clarification questions based on entire sentences as demonstrated in figure a instead both the decision making module and the span extraction module are required to facilitate the interaction the span extraction module successfully identifies the relevant segments within sentence s upon determining that the user fails to satisfy the condition specified in s the model correctly concludes with the answer no it is noted that relying solely on multi head self attention mechanisms is insufficient for accurately determining whether the conditions enumerated in the rule text have been satisfied error analysis we conducted an analysis of prediction errors generated by emt on the sharc development set regarding decision making errors out of examples in the development set the model produced incorrect decisions in instances

in addition generating fewer duplications will also improve the chance to produce correct keyphrases that have not been predicted yet we conduct extensive experiments on four popular realworld benchmarks empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of our hierarchical decoding process besides both the soft and the hard exclusion mechanisms significantly reduce the number of duplicated keyphrases furthermore after employing the hard exclusion mechanism our model consistently outperforms all the sota sequential decoding baselines on the four benchmarks we summarize our main contributions as follows to our best knowledge we are the first to design a hierarchical decoding process for the keyphrase generation problem we propose two novel exclusion mechanisms to avoid generating duplicated keyphrases as well as improve the generation accuracy and our method consistently outperforms all the sota sequential decoding methods on multiple benchmarks under the new setting related work keyphrase extraction most of the traditional extractive methods focus on extracting present keyphrases from the input document and follow a twostep framework they first extract plenty of keyphrase candidates by handcrafted rules then they score and rank these candidates based on either unsupervised methods or supervised learning methods recently neuralbased sequence labeling methods are also explored in keyphrase extraction problem however these extractive methods cannot predict absent keyphrase which is also an essential part of a keyphrase set keyphrase generation to produce both present and absent keyphrases meng et al introduced a generative model copyrnn which is based on an attentional encoderdecoder framework incorporating with a copy mechanism a wide range of extensions of copyrnn are recently proposed all of them rely on beam search to overgenerate lots of keyphrases with large beam size and then select the top n e g five or ten ranked ones as the final prediction

finally it remains an open question whether a system optimized for f score will generate sentences capable of genuinely enhancing neural machine translation nmt performance related work a standard workflow for mining parallel corpora typically begins by aligning parallel documents to constrain the search space for subsequent sentence alignment early approaches relied on webpage structure resnik and smith shi et al subsequently uszkoreit et al proposed translating all documents into a single language and shortlisting candidate document pairs based on tf idf weighted n grams more recently guo et al introduced a neural method to compare document embeddings derived from sentence embeddings under the assumption that matched documents are parallel i e no cross alignment sentence alignment can be achieved by comparing sentence lengths in terms of words brown et al or characters gale and church this approach was later refined by incorporating lexical features varga et al alternatively after translating texts into a common language the bleu metric can be employed to identify parallel texts by anchoring the most reliable alignments first sennrich and volk most recently thompson and koehn proposed comparing bilingual sentence embeddings using dynamic programming with linear runtime complexity parallel research efforts have also focused on extracting parallel sentences without relying on prior document alignment munteanu and marcu acquired parallel phrases from comparable corpora using bilingual tries and seed dictionaries azpeitia et al computed the jaccard similarity of lexical translation overlap while leong et al utilized an autoencoder combined with a maximum entropy classifier bouamor and sajjad considered the cosine similarity between averaged multilingual word embeddings guo et al designed a dual encoder model to learn multilingual sentence embeddings directly incorporating negative examples wieting et al derived sentence embeddings from subword embeddings and trained a simplified model to distinguish between positive and negative examples finally artetxe and schwenk refined the work of guo et al achieving state of the art results by analyzing the margins of cosine similarities between pairs of nearest neighbors

abstract acknowledging the limitations inherent in existing methodologies this study investigates effective strategies for collecting responses to hate speech the proposed approach leverages large scale unsupervised language models such as gpt to generate silver data furthermore it evaluates optimal annotation strategies and neural architectures designed to filter data prior to expert validation and post editing introduction the proliferation of social media platforms over the past decade has exacerbated the prevalence of hate speech hs which spreads rapidly and extensively monitoring and mitigating its diffusion remains a significant challenge as cultural and linguistic nuances often obscure the distinction between hate speech and other forms of dangerous discourse schmidt and wiegand conventional mitigation strategies employed by social media platforms smps typically involve the suspension of user accounts or the deletion of offensive content however these reactive measures frequently attract accusations of censorship and over blocking alternatively shadowbanning has been implemented to balance freedom of expression this technique hides content or accounts from search results without deletion despite these measures it is imperative to move beyond reactive identify and delete protocols toward responsive intervention within online conversations bielefeldt et al jurgens et al in this context various non governmental organizations ngos have trained operators to intervene in hateful dialogues by generating counter narratives cns a counter narrative is defined as a non aggressive response grounded in factual arguments widely regarded as the most effective mechanism for countering hate messages benesch schieb and preuss to ensure efficacy and prevent the escalation of hostility cns must adhere to established guidelines such as those outlined in the get the trolls out initiative nevertheless manual intervention lacks scalability consequently data driven natural language generation nlg approaches are increasingly being explored to assist ngo operators in drafting counter narratives as a foundational step diverse strategies for cn collection have been proposed each presenting distinct advantages and limitations mathew et al qian et al chung et al this study aims to investigate methods for acquiring high quality counter narratives while minimizing the burden on domain experts specifically we compare data collection strategies based on two critical requirements for cn datasets i data quantity and ii data quality

an alternative interpretation of these results might be that after the first portion of the semester students who had been receiving automatic feedback realized that they were not meeting their performance goals and spent more effort during the second half of the semester however the overall results of exam do not support this interpretation the performance gap was both wider and more significant on the second exam than on the first see we conclude that receiving human feedback very likely improves students’ conceptual understanding but that this improvement is limited to certain asof yet undetermined areas we posit that students who receive human feedback are better able to grasp the logic of the algorithms taught but that this better grasp does not significantly improve their ability to perform higherlevel analysis of that logic most of our results suggest that human style feedback had little effect on students’ final projects when analysis is restricted to the middle two quartiles several small effects are revealed students who received human feedback appeared to do better on code style and worse on efficacy it is not difficult to imagine that giving students feedback on code style would result in them doing better when later graded on the same points of style however that it should result in them producing less successful projects is tougher to swallow efficacy was assessed as the degree to which final project groups met the goals they had set for themselves when proposing their final projects perhaps manually graded students’ more promising grades earlier in the semester led them to propose more ambitious projects but the difference in means is more likely due to an outlier effect from the two manually graded students who received far lower scores than any of the others all in all we conclude that our results do not indicate that feedback on coding style improves the results on subsequent collaborative projects as originally hypothesized there may be some extent to which students remember good style pointers but even this is not clear and may not justify the extensive labor of giving students style feedback in spite of the previous equivocal results our analyses of students’ overall performance revealed that students who received human feedback had a subsignificant performance advantage on the exams over those who received automatic feedback and that this advantage translated to higher overall course grades

the type restricted copying mechanism exhibits a tendency to over focus on specific nodes thereby neglecting other available candidates this behavior often results in the repetitive activation of certain copied tokens within short spans of the generated text leading to significant content redundancy to mitigate this issue we introduce a copying decay strategy designed to smoothly penalize the probabilities associated with frequently copied nodes we define a time based decay rate lambda m i for the i th tree node x i at the m th decoding step if a node is copied at time step m its decay rate is initialized to in the subsequent step m this rate is scaled by a coefficient gamma in as defined by lambda m i gamma lambda m i quad consequently the overall formulation for the type restricted decoder is expressed as p y m hat a m hat y m t x tau text softmax s m d m odot lambda m quad hierarchical reinforcement learning training the proposed framework presents two primary challenges the absence of ground truth labels for the operation selection stage and the misalignment between the evaluation metric and the objective function while it is feasible to train the framework using maximum likelihood estimation mle —either by constructing pseudo labels or by marginalizing all operations during the selection stage jia and liang gu et al —a discrepancy exists between the mle loss used for training and the non differentiable metrics employed for evaluation this loss evaluation mismatch often yields inconsistent results keneshloo et al ranzato et al to address these limitations we propose a hierarchical reinforcement learning hrl approach to jointly train the operation selection and word selection stages the objective of the hrl framework is to maximize the expected reward r hat y y between the predicted sequence hat y and the ground truth sequence y denoted as mathcal l r

force manifests in various forms ranging from the mechanical force required to elevate a mass to the electromagnetic force necessary to displace a charged particle within a magnetic field additionally force may be expressed as pressure acting upon a surface area such as a piston crown thereby inducing motion in the context of thermodynamics the performance of work necessitates the involvement of a defined system this interaction occurs in one of two directions either the system performs work upon its surroundings the external environment or the surroundings perform work upon the system to mathematically distinguish these scenarios a sign convention is established wherein work done by the system is designated as positive while work done on the system is designated as negative it is imperative to note that a system does not inherently possess work rather work is a transient phenomenon resulting from the interaction between the system and its surroundings exemplified by the displacement of a piston the capacity of a system to perform work is derived from the energy contained within the matter residing in or traversing the system consequently work constitutes a mode of energy transfer that exists solely during its transition across a system s boundary upon entering or leaving the system work induces a change in the energy state of the matter heat denoted by the symbol q shares a fundamental characteristic with work it is not an intrinsic energy form possessed by matter instead heat is defined strictly as energy crossing a system s boundary driven by a temperature differential between the system and its surroundings this technical definition diverges from colloquial usage which often incorrectly implies that matter contains an energy form termed heat there exist scenarios wherein a temperature difference persists between the system and surroundings without any resultant heat transfer such a scenario is termed an adiabatic process for instance a hot water pipe that is effectively insulated from its environment exhibits negligible heat flow and is consequently modeled as an adiabatic open system while the units for heat are joules identical to those for work the sign convention for heat is inverse to that of work heat flow into the system is considered positive whereas heat flow out of the system is negative the principle of conservation of energy or the first law of thermodynamics for a system of constant mass may be formulated as follows consider a system of constant mass possessing an initial energy state if this system receives energy in the form of heat and simultaneously performs work thereby expending energy the system will attain a final energy state

while the concept of orientation is developed to model dynamics within counseling interactions it is positioned as a complementary framework to existing characterizations of conversational behavior across diverse contexts previous scholarship has similarly investigated the relationship between utterances and their surrounding discourse webber for instance frameworks such as centering theory grosz et al focus on identifying referenced entities whereas the present approach seeks to model interlocutor choices at a more abstract level prior research has also examined how participants influence conversational trajectories by assuming or relinquishing control walker and whittaker or by shifting topics nguyen et al additionally althoff et al have analyzed the rate at which counselors progress through conversational stages although these actions are often interpreted as forward oriented the current study emphasizes the interplay between forward and backward oriented behaviors furthermore counselor objectives frequently transcend these categorical distinctions for example professional training emphasizes the necessity of empathetic reflection across all stages and topics the proposed orientation framework also complements prior work on dialogue acts which delineate the various functional roles of utterances within discourse mann and thompson core and allen ritter et al bracewell et al rosenthal and mckeown prabhakaran et al wang et al in counseling contexts such approaches have highlighted specific strategies including reflection and questioning houck gaume et al atkins et al can et al tanana et al pérez rosas et al park et al lee et al cao et al unlike these studies which typically model specific taxonomies of actions the present work focuses on how counselors balance underlying objectives the relationship between orientation and these strategies is further explored in section moreover while most existing approaches rely on manual annotations or predefined labeling schemes the methodology proposed herein is unsupervised measuring orientation this section outlines the methodology for measuring orientation the approach is first described at a high level followed by a detailed elaboration of its specific operationalization

mikolov et al a observed that continuous embedding spaces exhibit structural similarities across different languages a property that can be leveraged through a linear transformation mapping the source embedding space to the target embedding space this observation underpins the bilingual lexicon induction bli task which aims to align the embedding spaces of two languages to automatically generate a word translation lexicon as a fundamental problem in natural language processing bli facilitates a wide range of downstream applications including sentence translation rapp fung unsupervised machine translation lample et al and cross lingual information retrieval lavrenko et al recent studies lample et al alvarez melis jaakkola grave et al artetxe et al have demonstrated that unsupervised bli approaches can achieve performance comparable to supervised methods a critical component of these approaches is the matching procedure which determines the generation of the translation plan for instance alvarez melis and jaakkola utilized the gromov wasserstein distance to approximate cross lingual matching while grave et al framed the problem as a classic optimal transport task employing the sinkhorn algorithm cuturi to compute the translation plan in this work we adopt the established iterative framework but introduce a novel matching procedure conventional iterative algorithms necessitate the computation of an approximate one to one matching at each step however this strict constraint often results in redundant matchings to address this limitation we relax the one to one constraint by incorporating two kullback leibler kl divergence regularization terms into the original loss function thereby controlling the degree of relaxation this approach yields a more precise matching and significantly enhances performance furthermore we propose a bidirectional optimization framework that simultaneously optimizes the mapping from source to target and from target to source experimental results confirm the efficacy of our method demonstrating that it outperforms numerous state of the art approaches on the bli task background early research on the bli task relied on the availability of a parallel lexicon between the target languages

summary of findings no single method has been identified that definitively enhances both forward and counterfactual simulatability simultaneously within the text domain however the prototype and composite methods demonstrate superior average performance furthermore empirical evidence suggests a disconnect between user perceived explanation quality and the actual utility of those explanations for counterfactual simulation specifically while composite explanations receive high quality ratings from users they do not yield overt improvements in simulatability across either data domain background and related work defining interpretability the literature employs key terminology with varying degrees of consistency rudin distinguishes between interpretability and explainability positing that a model is interpretable only if its internal computations are directly understandable in contrast post hoc explanations are characterized as potentially misleading approximations of the true underlying computations gilpin et al similarly differentiate between these concepts albeit with distinct definitions in this study we do not maintain a strict distinction between interpretability and explainability instead we adopt the conceptual framework proposed by doshi velez and kim which evaluates interpretability based on downstream desiderata accordingly our terminology defines explanation methods as mechanisms that may enhance a model s interpretability specifically in the sense that an interpretable model is simulatable explanation methods various taxonomies have been proposed to categorize interpretability methods this study organizes these approaches into three primary categories feature importance estimation case based reasoning and latent space traversal feature importance estimation methods in this category provide insights into how models utilize specific features the most prominent approaches are gradient based techniques originally introduced for computer vision by simonyan et al and subsequently adapted for text data by li et al however research has demonstrated that these approaches can occasionally exhibit counterintuitive behaviors adebayo et al kim et al consequently numerous alternative methods have been developed to quantify feature importance across diverse data domains kim et al lundberg and lee sundararajan et al for the purposes of this study we evaluate two domain agnostic approaches lime and anchor ribeiro et al these methods employ simple surrogate models such as sparse linear models and rule lists to approximate the local in recent years the appearance of largescale conversational qa datasets like coqa and quac makes it possible to train datadriven sqg models and the coqa dataset was widely adopted by prior works since the test set of coqa is not released to the public its training set k passages with k questions was split into new training and validation set and its validation set k passages with k questions was used as the new test set different from traditional qa datasets where the answers are certain spans from given passages answers in coqa are freeform text with cor only of the answers overlap with the passage after ignoring punctuations and case mismatches responding evidence highlighted in the passage this brings a big trouble for qg as an example consider the yes no questions counting for among all questions given the answer “ yes ” and a corresponding evidence “ the group first met on july on the campus of the ohio state university ” there are many potential outputs e g “ did the group first met in july ” “ was the group first met in ohio state ” when considering the context formed by previous questions the potential outputs become even more the original question in coqa is “ was it founded the same year ” when there are too many potential outputs with significantly different semantic meanings training a converged qg model becomes extremely difficult for this reason gao et al directly discarded questions that cannot be answered by spans from passages however the remaining questions can become incoherent e g antecedent words for many pronouns become unclear to this end we build a new dataset from coqa by preserving all k passages and rewriting all questions and answers more specifically we first discarded questions that are unsuitable for sqg to do so three annotators were hired to vote for the preservation deletion of each question a question is preserved if and only if it can be answered by a certain span from the input passage as a result most deleted questions were yes no questions and unanswerable questions besides the kappa score between results given by different annotators was indicating that there was a strong interagreement between annotators

the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence ai currently exhibiting an exponential growth trajectory with an approximate doubling period of one year holds the potential to counteract the exponential increase in age related mortality which doubles roughly every seven years however a significant temporal lag exists in the deployment of medical ai technologies furthermore historical precedents of ai winters suggest that the current growth rate may decelerate potentially following a sigmoidal rather than purely exponential curve as noted by nick bostrom each day of delay in achieving superintelligent ai capable of reversing aging represents a substantial loss of human life given the inherent uncertainty regarding the pace of ai progress this discussion adopts a timeline agnostic framework to analyze the developmental stages of ai classification of future ai development stages this section delineates and refines the classification of prospective ai capabilities addressing the frequent conflation of these distinct levels in contemporary discourse narrow ai weak ai narrow ai refers to computer systems capable of surpassing human performance within specific well defined domains for instance distinct systems are required for tasks such as mri scan analysis and facial recognition despite potentially sharing underlying learning mechanisms the majority of existing ai systems fall into this category with their proliferation driven by advancements in machine learning and neural networks the primary distinction between narrow ai and conventional software lies in the former s capacity for learning autonomous vehicles serve as a prominent example of narrow ai however these systems lack full human cognitive capacity particularly regarding generalization and typically require ongoing human supervision artificial general intelligence agi agi denotes systems possessing human level intelligence across a broad spectrum of domains such systems would likely demonstrate natural language communication contextual understanding and the ability to perform the majority of intellectual tasks executable by humans while philosophical inquiries regarding machine consciousness fall outside the scope of this pragmatic definition the capacity for self improvement is an inherent consequence of achieving this level of development consequently as posited by nick bostrom the era of human level ai is expected to be transient an agi with self improving capabilities is projected to rapidly evolve into superintelligence

in natural language processing the removal of stop words is a critical prerequisite for semantic similarity tasks while stop words represent the most frequently occurring terms in a language no universal list exists that applies to all processing contexts consequently stop words are initially cataloged within a data structure e g a java array and subsequently filtered from the input texts denoted as s and t prior to the similarity analysis phase the processing pipeline proceeds through the following stages part of speech pos tagging and parsing the initial step involves labeling words using the penn treebank tag set to extract syntactic information these tagged tokens serve as input for generating a parse tree a top down parsing strategy is employed leveraging its specific advantages over bottom up approaches this process incorporates all relevant english grammatical roles to construct phrase structures which are then utilized to generate the final top down parse tree graph construction a multi stage undirected weighted graph is constructed with the number of stages corresponding to the depth of the parse tree this graph integrates the syntactic structure with statistical data derived from previous stages the construction adheres to the following criteria part of speech filtering stop words are excluded based on their pos tags when comparing two parse trees words appearing at the same level are only considered if they are not stop words node depth path analysis commences from the root node s and extends to leaf nodes representing specific words or concepts the depth of a word is a factor in similarity measurement only when the word appears at the same level in both parse trees and shares an identical pos tag string matching if a word with the pos tag nnp proper noun is identified in both parse trees s and t a specific weight is assigned to the connecting link if the nodes are identical weight calculation and similarity measurement upon completion of the graph construction weights are calculated between corresponding nodes of the two graphs the assignment of these weights is governed by specific conditions notably if two nodes at the same level possess different pos tags the wordnet taxonomy relationship is invoked to determine the appropriate weight
