course evaluation and recommendations for future offerings overview and challenges during the initial offering of this undergraduate level course students demonstrated strong collaboration on written assignments resulting in high performance during the early weeks however as the curriculum progressed to more complex topics it became evident that the mathematical foundation of the average computer science major was insufficient to fully master the lecture material and complete advanced assignments consequently the scope of the material and the overall workload proved overly ambitious for the target student demographic despite these challenges student feedback indicated a strong consensus that the course should be offered again citing the subject matter as both fascinating and highly relevant to contemporary computer science interests to enhance the efficacy of future iterations the following improvements to course logistics and delivery are recommended enforce prerequisite requirements although the course syllabus originally intended to list linear algebra and programming as prerequisites an administrative error resulted in the omission of these requirements this oversight contributed to an initial enrollment of over students followed by a significant attrition rate of approximately students after the first week recommendation strictly enforce linear algebra and programming as mandatory prerequisites establishing clear entry requirements will better align student expectations and allow instructors to focus on novel quantum computing content rather than remedial instruction alternative if prerequisites cannot be strictly enforced the curriculum should be adjusted to dedicate the first week to a comprehensive review of essential linear algebra concepts implement dedicated programming laboratories the current iteration relied heavily on student self directed learning supported by language documentation existing code samples quantum katas and asynchronous access to a q expert via online channels and office hours while students successfully organized peer study groups the lack of structured support limited the learning experience recommendation introduce a mandatory weekly programming laboratory session lasting to minutes scheduled in person access to an instructor with specific quantum computing expertise would significantly improve student outcomes and provide immediate technical support recruit specialized teaching assistants currently the instructional burden regarding programming and written assignments falls disproportionately on the primary instructors recommendation recruit teaching assistants tas possessing specific knowledge in quantum computing while the long term goal is to cultivate a pool of qualified alumni to serve in this capacity immediate recruitment of specialized tas is necessary to alleviate instructor workload and provide targeted student support calibrate curriculum to student proficiency recommendation future course content should be carefully adjusted to align with the actual proficiency levels of the enrolled student body ensuring that the pace and complexity of the material are appropriate for the cohort s background

each textual representation constitutes a real valued vector employed as a proxy for the confounder z within causal adjustment frameworks section open challenges estimates of causal effects are inherently contingent upon the garden of forking paths inherent in data analysis implying that alternative analytical choices could yield divergent conclusions gelman and loken in contexts involving textual confounders the initial analytical divergence concerns the selection of the representation method e g topic models or embeddings while the subsequent divergence involves preprocessing protocols and hyperparameter configurations it is well documented that such decisions significantly influence outcomes in predictive tasks for instance empirical studies indicate that preprocessing choices substantially alter topic model outputs denny and spirling schofield et al embeddings exhibit sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning levy et al and the composition of the training corpus antoniak and mimno and the performance of fine tuned language models is susceptible to random initialization phang et al consequently conducting sensitivity analyses regarding these modeling decisions is imperative to assess the robustness of causal effect estimates against variations in specification adjustment for confounding bias given a set of variables z that satisfy the backdoor criterion section the causal quantity of interest can be estimated via the backdoor adjustment formula p y y mid text do t t int p y y mid t t z z p z z dz quad in high dimensional domains such as natural language processing conditioning on the entirety of confounding variables is often impractical this review provides an overview of methodologies employed in existing applications to approximate such conditioning thereby facilitating unbiased estimates of treatment effects however it should be noted that this is not an exhaustive enumeration of available techniques readers are directed to more comprehensive guides for further detail morgan and winship athey et al

annotation tags the aac conversational data used for future experiments could benefit from aac context specific dialogue act tags although the damsl tags work well for comparison to results on existing datasets it would be more beneficial to use a refined tag set that is specific to the aac data that is being analyzed ideally the tags should be modified depending on the context in which different from a set that would be used to provide realtime suggestions on a device during a typical conversation once a set of context appropriate tags has been established the aac data should be annotated by a group of annotators who are familiar with interpreting aac output such as speech pathologists in order to establish more accurate standard tags and interannotator agreement in this the attention mechanism was originally introduced for sequence to sequence tasks where it has been reported to yield significant performance improvements this study investigates whether in such contexts attention can be effectively decoupled from its conventional interpretation as an alignment mechanism between input and output tokens to facilitate this analysis we employ three synthetic sequence to sequence tasks designed to evaluate the learning of elementary input to output mappings bigram flipping the objective is to reverse adjacent pairs of tokens within the input sequence i e transforming w w dots w n w n into w w dots w n w n sequence copying the model is required to reproduce the input sequence exactly i e w w dots w n rightarrow w w dots w n sequence reversal the goal is to invert the order of the entire input sequence i e w w dots w n rightarrow w n w n dots w these synthetic tasks were selected because the specific input tokens responsible for generating any given target token are deterministically known consequently the ground truth alignments serve as impermissible constraints within our experimental framework varying for each output token these tasks have previously been utilized in the literature to assess the capacity of recurrent neural networks rnns to learn long range reorderings and substitutions grefenstette et al for each of the three synthetic tasks we programmatically generated random training sequences each with a corresponding target sequence and a maximum length of tokens the input and output vocabularies were fixed at unique tokens notably for the bigram flipping task input sequence lengths were restricted to even numbers evaluation was conducted using two distinct sets of unseen random sequences drawn from the same distribution serving as the validation and test sets respectively in addition to synthetic benchmarks we evaluated the proposed approach on a real world machine translation task involving english to german translation for this purpose we utilized the multik dataset which consists of image descriptions elliott et al

in towards debiasing sentence representations paul pu liang irene mengze li emily zheng yao chong lim ruslan salakhutdinov louisphilippe morency machine learning department and language technologies institute carnegie mellon university as natural language processing methods are increasingly deployed in realworld scenarios such as healthcare legal systems and social science it becomes necessary to recognize the role they potentially play in shaping social biases and stereotypes previous work has revealed the presence of social biases in widely used word embeddings involving gender race religion and other social constructs while some methods were proposed to debias these wordlevel embeddings there is a need to perform debiasing at the sentencelevel given the recent shift towards new contextualized sentence representations such as elmo and bert in this paper we investigate the presence of social biases in sentencelevel representations and propose a new method s ent d ebias to reduce these biases we show that s ent d ebias is effective in removing biases and at the same time preserves performance on sentencelevel downstream tasks such as sentiment analysis linguistic acceptability and natural language understanding we hope that our work will inspire future research on characterizing and removing social biases from widely adopted sentence representations for fairer nlp introduction machine learning tools for learning from language are increasingly deployed in realworld scenarios such as healthcare legal systems and computational social science key to the success of these models are powerful embedding layers which learn continuous representations of input information such as words sentences and documents from large amounts of data although wordlevel embeddings are highly informative features useful for a variety of tasks in natural language processing nlp recent work has shown that wordlevel embeddings reflect and propagate social biases present in training corpora

rajani et al finetuned a transformerbased language model on freetext rationales provided by humans with an objective of generating openended explanations to improve performance on downstream tasks evaluating rationales work on evaluating rationales has often compared these to human judgments or elicited other human evaluations of explanations there has also been work on visual evaluations of saliency maps measuring agreement between extracted and human rationales or collecting subjective assessments of them assesses the plausibility of rationales but such approaches do not establish whether the model actually relied on these particular rationales to make a prediction we refer to rationales that correspond to the inputs most relied upon to come to a disposition as faithful most automatic evaluations of faithfulness measure the impact of perturbing or erasing words or tokens identified as important on model output we build upon these methods in section finally we note that a recent article urges the community to evaluate faithfulness on a continuous scale of acceptability rather than viewing this as a binary proposition datasets in eraser for all datasets in eraser we distribute both reference labels and rationales marked by humans as supporting these in a standardized format we delineate train validation and test splits for all corpora see appendix a for processing details we ensure that these splits comprise disjoint sets of source documents to avoid contamination we have made the decision to distribute the test sets publicly in part because we do not view the ‘correct’ metrics to use as settled we plan to acquire additional human annotations on heldout portions of some of the included corpora so as to offer hidden test set evaluation opportunities in the future evidence inference a dataset of fulltext articles describing randomized controlled trials rcts

model predictions and ground truth labels additionally regression outputs are converted into categorical values to derive binary classification accuracy ba and the f score for all metrics except mae discussion table presents the experimental results the key observations derived from these results are summarized below performance of magbert across all metrics on the cmumosi dataset magbert it is also used to recognize other finergrained elements of a conversation such as avoiding repetition asking questions refraining from giving unrelated responses and so on — all of which control aspects of the conversation such as fluency interestingness inquisitiveness or empathy commonsense knowledge is thus necessary to model the nature and flow of the dialogue and the emotional dynamics of the participants in natural language is often indicative of one’s emotion hence emotion recognition has been enjoying popularity in the field of nlp due to its widespread applications in opinion mining recommender systems healthcare and so on only in the past few years has emotion recognition in conversation erc gained attention from the nlp community due to the growing availability of public conversational data erc can be used to analyze conversations that take place on social media it can also aid in analyzing conversations in real time which can be instrumental in legal trials interviews ehealth services and more unlike vanilla emotion recognition of sentences utterances erc ideally requires context modeling of the individual utterances this context can be attributed to the preceding utterances and relies on the temporal sequence of utterances compared to the recently published works on erc both lexiconbased and modern deep learningbased vanilla emotion recognition approaches fail to work well on erc datasets as this work ignores the conversation specific factors such as the presence of contextual cues the temporality in speakers’ turns or speakerspecific information in this paper we introduce c osmic a commonsenseguided framework for emotion identification in conversations

this document presents a synthesis of pedagogical innovations and empirical findings across three distinct educational contexts introductory computer science intermediate programming curricula and advanced database systems in the realm of introductory computer science student engagement was observed to be highly adaptable a significant portion of the cohort adopted a flexible interpretation of holiday themes for their projects utilizing personal milestones such as birthdays celebrating non traditional observances like national food days or creating entirely original holidays technical analysis of student artifacts revealed a high degree of retention and application of prior learning specifically over of students utilized the say block a concept introduced in module and reinforced in preceding activities furthermore at least incorporated size manipulation blocks from lesson while others effectively deployed go to and move blocks from the initial module these findings underscore the successful integration of required coding blocks into student generated projects regarding intermediate programming education this study introduces scratch encore a curriculum designed for students aged to the program synthesizes the use modify create pedagogical framework culturally relevant pedagogy and the tipp see learning strategy this integration aims to address equity issues while simultaneously establishing foundational computer science concepts preliminary pilot data indicate strong instructor interest and demonstrate that the curriculum fosters desirable and productive student engagement finally an experience report details a pedagogical shift in an upper year course on the internals of relational database systems the course transitioned from a traditional model of lectures and homework to a partially flipped model replacing standard assignments with an extensive repository of worked examples and more frequent comprehensive examinations grounded in cognitive load theory this approach seeks to mitigate the cognitive demands associated with complex problem solving tasks such as query evaluation and optimization student surveys indicate that exposure to numerous worked examples significantly enhanced learner confidence regarding course objectives to evaluate the efficacy of this transition a bloom s taxonomy weighted index was developed using bloom s dichotomous key to track exam difficulty over time while final examination grades remained relatively stable the increasing difficulty of the assessments suggests improved learning outcomes despite the absence of a statistically significant increase in performance on transfer questions principal component analysis of the final examinations revealed that a substantial proportion of questions is required to account for the majority of variance in student scores although comparative studies on flipped versus traditional classrooms have yielded mixed results generally this specific implementation—distinct in its reliance on worked examples rather than pre class video lectures—demonstrates a viable pathway for enhancing student confidence and managing cognitive load in complex technical domains

third faculty members have acknowledged significant challenges in adequately preparing students for industry demands these difficulties are attributed to expanding class sizes a lack of practical industry experience among instructors and the complexities of designing realistic projects without overburdening either students or faculty wilson et al posit that aligning student and instructor expectations is essential for enhancing learning outcomes and performance consequently curriculum designers must possess a clear understanding of student motivations and programmatic expectations research into student motivations across disciplines including geography and computer science indicates a strong preference for curricula that emphasize career guidance and vocational training in the field of computer science students often value career relevant instruction but may fail to recognize the professional applicability of certain theoretical courses concurrently industry practitioners report a persistent gap between the competencies of university graduates and the expectations of the workforce encompassing both technical and non technical domains specific deficiencies identified among recent computer science graduates include an undervaluation of software testing difficulties with configuration management systems and other professional tools a lack of relevant project experience and insufficient exposure to maintaining or extending large poorly documented legacy codebases furthermore numerous studies highlight a deficit in the social and soft skills necessary for professional success an analysis of course syllabi revealed a notable absence of soft skills in curricular documentation graduates have been found to struggle with effective communication with colleagues and clients as well as with seeking assistance from peers or senior engineers when required additionally students often hold fundamental misconceptions regarding software engineering practices in industry particularly concerning the importance of team collaboration although internship experiences have been shown to mitigate these misunderstandings to some degree craig et al synthesized prior research and conducted further student interviews concluding that computer science students are ill prepared for customer centered projects with evolving scopes long term development cycles collaboration within large teams on complex systems and the application of professional tools and formal methodologies notably a recent study indicated that approximately half of computer science students felt their academic programs did not adequately prepare them for their professional careers

related work and methodological distinction prior research in this domain has predominantly relied on conventional recurrent neural network rnn architectures limiting its direct applicability to advanced transformer models to address optimization challenges in deeper encoders bapna et al introduced a transparent network architecture for neural machine translation nmt enabling each decoder layer to access a unique weighted combination of all encoder layer outputs rather than relying solely on the final layer subsequent studies by wang et al a demonstrated that strategic application of layer normalization facilitates the training of deep encoders while also proposing shortcut connections to enhance gradient flow zhang et al a further contributed depth scaled initialization techniques to preserve norms and introduced a merged attention sublayer to mitigate computational overhead in deep models alternative approaches have explored staged model growth for instance wu et al proposed a two stage training process where shallow encoders and decoders are trained and subsequently frozen before stacking additional layers concurrently xu et al investigated the impact of the computational ordering of residual connections and layer normalization proposing a parameter initialization method with lipschitz constraints to ensure convergence in deep transformers while other works have examined deep representations to strengthen feature propagation and reuse shen et al dou et al wang et al b these approaches typically restrict decoder attention to the final output of the encoder stack in contrast the proposed method distinguishes itself by establishing multigranular dependencies between the encoder and decoder across different spatial scales at earlier stages of processing conclusion and future work this paper presents a multiscale collaborative framework designed to facilitate the training of extremely deep nmt models departing from the conventional reliance on the topmost encoder representation our approach enables the decoder to attend to multigranular source information across varying spatial scales empirical results demonstrate that this methodology significantly enhances the trainability of very deep architectures yielding substantial improvements in translation quality attributable to increased model depth experiments conducted across diverse language pairs confirm that the proposed multiscale collaborative msc framework achieves prominent performance gains over both strong baselines and existing deep models

experimental findings empirical results indicate that integrating the outputs of a fine tuned bert model as auxiliary features within the grammatical error correction gec framework method c yields the most significant performance gains across the majority of the corpora evaluated furthermore the combination of the bertfuse mask and bertfuse graph edit distance ged methodologies demonstrates a synergistic effect further enhancing gec performance the proposed optimal configuration achieves state of the art results on both the bea and conll benchmarks related work existing literature establishes that masked language models mlms can enhance gec performance when deployed as reranking mechanisms chollampat et al kaneko et al or as filtering tools asano et al kiyono et al while encoder decoder encdec based gec models can be integrated with these pipeline approaches certain methodologies such as the sequence labeling models proposed by asano et al are incompatible with our architecture due to structural differences consequently while our approach leverages existing encdec gec knowledge it cannot directly incorporate these specific sequence labeling techniques to the best of our knowledge no prior research has effectively integrated mlm derived information to facilitate the training of encdec gec models although mlms are typically adapted for downstream tasks via fine tuning liu zhang et al zhu et al demonstrated superior efficacy in providing the final layer outputs of an mlm as contextual embeddings to an encdec model additionally weng et al addressed the domain mismatch between contextual knowledge from pre trained models and target bilingual machine translation tasks similarly this study posits that bridging the distributional gap between grammatically correct raw corpora and gec specific corpora is critical for advancing gec system performance methodologies for integrating pre trained mlms into gec models this section delineates the strategies employed to incorporate a pre trained mlm into the gec architecture three distinct approaches were evaluated initializing the gec model with bert weights utilizing bert outputs as supplementary features for the gec model and employing outputs from a bert model fine tuned on gec corpora as additional features bert initialization bertinit in this approach a gec encdec model is initialized using the pre trained weights of bert this methodology is grounded in the framework established by lample and conneau

instance transfer and augmentation firstly a pretrained generation model based on userposed answerquestion pairs is utilized as an initial question generator a ranker is designed to work together with the generator to improve the training instance set by distilling it via removing unsuitable answerquestion pairs to avoid “negative transfer” and augmenting it by adding suitable reviewquestion pairs for selecting suitable reviews for question generation the ranker considers two factors the major aspects in a review and the review’s suitability for question generation the two factors are captured via a reconstruction objective and a reinforcement objective with reward given by the generator thus the ranker and the generator are iteratively enhanced and the adaptively transferred answerquestion pairs and the augmented reviewquestion pairs gradually relieve the data lacking problem in accordance with the second characteristic of our task it is plausible to regard a review sentence or clause as the answer to the corresponding question originated from it such treatment brings in the second challenge how can we guarantee that the generated question concentrates on the critical aspect mentioned by the review sentence for example a question like “how was the experience for gaming ” is not a favourable generation for “i have battery life for more than two days for normal use i e not powerconsuming gaming ” to solve this problem we incorporate aspectbased feature discovering in the ranker and then we integrate the aspect features and an aspect pointer network in the generator the incorporation of such aspectrelated features and structures helps the generator to focus more on critical product aspects other than the less important parts which is complied with the real userposed questions

the main contributions of this paper are as follows • a novel mt task is proposed which can only use the groundtruth bilingual dictionary and monolingual corpora while is independent on parallel sentences • at is proposed as a solution to the task at uses the bilingual dictionary to place anchors that can encourage monolingual spaces of both languages to become closer so that translation becomes easier • the detailed evaluation on various language pairs shows that at especially biview at performs significantly better than various methods including wordbyword translation unsupervised mt and crosslingual embedding transformation on distant language pairs that unsupervised mt struggled to be effective at and biview at perform remarkably better related work the bilingual dictionaries used in previous works are mainly for bilingual lexicon induction bli which independently learns the embedding in each language using monolingual corpora and then learns a transformation from one embedding space to another by minimizing squared euclidean distances between all word pairs in the dictionary later efforts for bli include optimizing the transformation further through new training objectives constraints or normalizations besides the bilingual dictionary is also used for supervised nmt which requires largescale parallel sentences to our knowledge we are the first to use the bilingual dictionary for mt without using any parallel sentences our work is closely related to unsupervised nmt unmt which does not use parallel sentences neither the difference is that unmt may use the artificial dictionary generated by unsupervised bli for initialization or abandon the artificial dictionary by using joint bpe so that multiple bpe units can be shared by both languages we use the groundtruth dictionary instead and apply it throughout a novel training process

the data collection method can be applied in a neverending learning scenario becoming a moving target for nlu rather than a static benchmark that will quickly saturate introduction progress in ai has been driven by among other things the development of challenging largescale benchmarks like imagenet in computer vision and snli squad and others in natural language processing nlp recently for natural language understanding nlu in particular the focus has shifted to combined benchmarks like senteval and glue which track model performance on multiple tasks and provide a unified platform for analysis with the rapid pace of advancement in ai however nlu benchmarks struggle to keep up with model improvement whereas it took around years to achieve “nearhuman performance” on mnist and approximately years to surpass humans on imagenet the glue benchmark did not last as long as we would have hoped after the advent of bert and rapidly had to be extended into superglue this raises an important question can we collect a large benchmark dataset that can last longer the speed with which benchmarks become obsolete raises another important question are current nlu models genuinely as good as their high performance on benchmarks suggests a growing body of evidence shows that stateoftheart models learn to exploit spurious statistical patterns in datasets instead of learning meaning in the flexible and generalizable way that humans do given this human annotators—be they seasoned nlp researchers or nonexperts— might easily be able to construct examples that expose model brittleness we propose an iterative adversarial humanandmodelin theloop solution for nlu dataset collection that addresses both benchmark longevity and robustness issues

zeng et al su et al further extend their approach by separating the domainshared and domainspecific knowledge within the embedding in addition zeng et al and shen et al propose a maximum weighted likelihood estimation method where the weight is obtained by wordlevel domain aware masking to encourage the model to pay more attention to the domainspecific words the aforementioned methods however have a notable limitation they enforce one single encoder to learn shared embedding across all domains which often lacks adaptivity to each individual domain to better capture domainshared knowledge beyond shared embedding from a single encoder we propose a novel multidomain nmt model using individual modules for each domain on which we apply wordlevel adaptive and layerwise domain mixing our proposed model is motivated by the observation that although every sentence of the training data has a domain label the words in the sentence are not necessarily only related to that domain for instance the word “article” appears in the domains of laws and business therefore we expect the knowledge for translating the word “article” to be shared between these two domains our proposed model assigns a contextdependent domain proportion to every word in the sentence the domain proportions of the words can be naturally integrated into the transformer model for capturing domainshared specific knowledge as a word actually has multiple domain proportions at different layers of our model see more details in section the multihead dotproduct attention mechanism is applied at the wordlevel specifically we carefully design multihead dotproduct attention modules for different domains and eventually mix these modules by taking weighted averages of their parameters by their layerwise domain proportions compared with existing models ours has the following two advantages • our proposed model is more powerful in capturing the domainspecific knowledge as we design multiple dotproduct attention modules for different domains in contrast existing models rely on one single shared encoder and then one single unified translation model is applied which often cannot adapt to each individual domain very well • our proposed model is more adaptive in the process of domain knowledge sharing for common words across domains their domain proportions tend to be uniform and therefore can significantly encourage knowledge sharing

recently trade off relations have garnered significant attention within the field of biomimetics adriaens as technological definitions of trade offs can be directly applied to identify relevant biological literature vincent these relations characterize a problem space through mutual exclusivity constraints between competing demands consequently trade offs occupy a central position in evolutionary theory agrawal et al and constitute the primary subject of investigation in a substantial portion of biological research garland the functional demands involved in such trade offs are typically abstract and domain independent concepts such as safety and efficiency despite their importance a gap persists in the rapid comprehension of central textual information particularly regarding the biological mechanisms employed to manipulate these trade offs information extraction ie and specifically relation extraction re offers a means to enhance access to such critical data for downstream applications santos et al zeng et al jiang et al miwa and bansal luan et al a however existing re systems and datasets exhibit significant limitations current approaches are either overly narrow focusing on a limited set of semantic relations e g used for or synonymy or excessively broad extracting an unbounded number of generic relations from large heterogeneous corpora—a methodology known as open information extraction oie etzioni et al banko et al niklaus et al narrow ie approaches applied to scientific texts augenstein et al gábor et al luan et al a typically capture only a fraction of the information contained within a paper often restricted to the abstract given that scientific literature encompasses a diverse array of unique relation types it is impractical to develop distinct narrow ie classifiers for each groth et al conversely oie systems which are primarily optimized for web and newswire domains have demonstrated poor performance when applied to scientific texts

although every computational problem is unique these types of problems often share significant similarities with other problems computer scientists have identified many canonical problems that represent these common problem templates although each of these canonical problems may have many algorithmic solutions computer scientists have also identified canonical algorithms for solving these problems in this section we will introduce canonical problems and survey canonical algorithms for each problem data structure problems are not only useful for implementing data structures but also as fundamental algorithm design patterns for organizing data to enable efficient solutions to almost every other computing problem searching is the problem of retrieving a target element from a collection of elements searching in a linear data structure such as an array list can be done using either sequential search or binary search recursively narrows down the possible locations for the target in the sorted list initially the algorithm has no information—the target can be anywhere in the entire range of the sorted list by comparing the target to the middle element of the range we can ruleout half of the elements in the range this process can be repeated until we have found the expected location of the target in the sorted list the runtime of binary search is in o log n with respect to n the number of elements in the sorted list so long as indexing is a constanttime operation see although the sequential search algorithm works with any collection type such as lists sets dictionaries and priority queues the binary search algorithm relies on a sorted list with access to elements by index consequently binary search is efficient on array lists that provide constanttime access to the element at any index and inefficient on linked lists which do not enable constanttime access to elements by index binary search relies on the structure of the sorted list to repeatedly ruleout half of the remaining elements binary search trees represent the concept of binary search in a tree data structure by arranging elements in the tree in sorted order from left to right ideally the root node represents the middle element in the sorted tree and each child roughly divides each subtree in half but in the worst case a binary search tree can look exactly like a linked list where each node contains either zero children or one child

this includes prior work on elementary cs education curricula and computing education outreach initiatives designed to broaden participation in the field and work on elementary cs learning trajectories while traditionally a subject for high school and beyond there is growing demand for bringing cs into k classrooms early work by papert and colleagues found that programming was accessible to younger students and could serve as a powerful pedagogical strategy in the last decade bringing cs to k has grown in popularity and has been facilitated by programming tools designed for young students an increasingly popular approach for creating engaging and accessible programming environments is the graphical blockbased programming interface visual blockbased languages use a programmingcommand aspuzzle piece metaphor to visually render syntax rules and allow users to use a dragand drop approach to construct programs blockbased programming tools provide numerous scaffolds that make programming easier including limiting syntax errors providing visual cues on how commands can be used and providing an easy way to browse available commands blockbased programming interfaces have been used to create an array of programming environments and tools including museum exhibits libraries for controlling robots and tools for creating mobile applications much of the curricular and programming environment design effort for younger students has drawn inspiration from the constructionist design and learning approach constructionism emphasizes focusing on the powerful ideas of a discipline and foregrounds learningby doing giving students opportunities to construct personally meaningful public artifacts constructionist learning experiences are often designed so as to give the learner agency in the activity enabling selfdirected learning and supporting exploration in the process of constructing public and shareable artifacts additionally constructionist designs provide students opportunities to present their work to peers and share their ideas and experiences through teacherled classroom discussions providing a public forum for students to share their ideas experiences and programs encourages students to become more invested in their own learning and promotes an inclusive learning environment in which all students can see themselves as members of a computing community this focus on studentdriven learning not specific technical content has coincided with work in informal spaces and placed an emphasis on selfdirected learning and online collaboration and the practices of computing

to achieve the stated objective three distinct test sets designated as alpha alpha and alpha were constructed a brief overview of these sets is provided herein with detailed specifications reserved for section the first test set alpha mirrors the distribution of the training data regarding both the lexical composition of the hypotheses and the domains of the premises the second set alpha serves as an adversarial benchmark while it maintains a distribution similar to the training data the hypothesis labels have been modified by expert annotators through minimal textual alterations for instance an entailment statement such as album x was released in the st century is converted into a contradiction album x was released before the st century by altering a single word this design aims to challenge models that rely on superficial textual artifacts in constructing alpha entailment examples were rewritten as contradictions and vice versa while neutral examples remained unchanged the third test set alpha is a cross domain evaluation set it utilizes premises from domains absent from the training split yet these domains generally require similar reasoning processes to determine entailment this set is designed to identify models that have overfit to domain specific artifacts while this work introduces these three specific test sets it is anticipated that future research will identify additional dimensions of overfitting and develop corresponding evaluation benchmarks the info tabs dataset this section details the construction methodology of the info tabs dataset the general workflow adapts established crowdsourcing approaches for natural language inference nli tasks such as those utilizing amazon mechanical turk e g bowman et al data sources the dataset comprises unique infoboxes extracted from wikipedia articles across various categories listed in appendix d several filtering criteria were applied during data collection tables containing fewer than three rows were excluded entries containing non english text e g latin plant nomenclature or highly technical information requiring specialized expertise e g astronomical data regarding exoplanets were omitted non textual elements such as images were removed large tables were segmented into smaller units at subheadings to simplify the data structure

let s t denote the set of labeled spans associated with a parse tree t formally this set is defined as s t i t j t l t t s t quad text where i t j t quad here s t represents the cardinality of the set corresponding to the total number of spans in the tree figure illustrates an example constituency tree alongside its corresponding labeled span representation adhering to established conventions in syntactic parsing gaddy et al shen et al the n ary tree structure is converted into a binary form in this process a dummy label emptyset is assigned to spans that do not constitute valid constituents in the original tree but are introduced as a consequence of binarization furthermore labels within unary chains corresponding to nested labeled spans are collapsed into unique atomic labels e g svp as depicted in figure while the proposed method adopts a span based perspective similar to that of stern et al a it diverges significantly from their framework regarding the overall formulation of the parsing problem as well as the specific representation and modeling of spans as detailed in the following sections parsing as pointing in contrast to prior approaches this work formulates parsing as a sequence of pointing decisions for each index i within the input sequence the parsing model identifies a target index p i to define the tree span i p i subject to the constraint i neq p i analogous to pointer networks vinyals et al a each pointing mechanism is modeled as a multinomial distribution over the indices of the input tokens or encoder states however unlike the original pointer network architecture where a decoder state points to an encoder state the proposed approach establishes a direct mapping wherein every encoder state h i points to another encoder state h p i throughout this paper the notation x to y denotes that x points to y the pointing operation is referenced either as a function of the encoder states e g h i to h p i or simply by the corresponding indices e g i to p i

as a result even stateoftheart systems trained with ample data still produce summaries with a substantial number of factual errors in this work we aim to optimize the factual correctness of existing neural summarization systems with a focus on summarizing radiology reports this task has several key properties that make it ideal for studying factual correctness in summarization models first the clinical facts or observations present in radiology reports have less ambiguity compared to opendomain text which allows objective comparison of facts second radiology reports involve a relatively limited space of facts which makes automatic measurement of factual correctness in the generated text approachable lastly as factual correctness is a crucial metric in this domain improving factual correctness will directly lead to an ability to use the system to this end we design a framework where an external information extraction system is used to extract information in the generated summary and produce a factual accuracy score by comparing it against the human reference summary we further develop a training strategy where we combine a factual correctness objective a textual overlap objective and a language model objective and jointly optimize them via reinforcement learning rl on two datasets of radiology reports collected from different hospitals we show that our training strategy substantially improves the factual correctness of the summaries generated by a competitive neural summarization system moreover we observe for the first time that even in the absence of a factual correctness objective optimizing a textual overlapbased metric substantially improves the factual correctness of the resulting system compared to maximum likelihood training we further show via human evaluation and analysis that our training strategy leads to summaries with higher overall quality and correctness and which are closer to the humanwritten ones our main contributions are i we propose a general framework and a training strategy for improving the factual correctness of summarization models by optimizing a multipart objective via rl ii we apply the proposed strategy to radiology reports and empirically show that it improves the factual correctness of the generated summaries and iii we demonstrate via radiologist evaluation that our system is able to generate summaries with clinical validity close to humanwritten ones

a list is defined as an ordered sequence of elements that supports insertion retrieval and deletion at arbitrary positions as an indexed data structure it facilitates direct element access via a specific positional identifier a common application involves representing chronological task sequences such as a to do list a set constitutes an unordered collection of unique elements permitting addition retrieval and removal operations while sets generally offer a more limited functional scope compared to lists this constraint enables more efficient internal representations they are frequently employed to manage collections of distinct items such as a registry of intended travel destinations a map also referred to as a dictionary maintains unordered associations between unique keys and their corresponding values each key within the structure must be distinct mapping to a specific value maps are often utilized in conjunction with other data structures for instance a travel itinerary might employ a map to associate each destination with a specific list of planned activities a priority queue manages a collection of elements each assigned a priority value beyond standard insertion this structure prioritizes the retrieval and removal of the element possessing the highest priority this mechanism is essential in scenarios requiring triage such as managing patient intake in an emergency department based on the urgency of care a graph models binary relations among a set of entities represented as vertices relationships between these entities are denoted by directed or undirected edges connecting pairs of vertices typical operations include the addition and removal of vertices and edges as well as the retrieval of adjacent edges for a specific vertex a primary use case is the representation of social networks where undirected edges signify mutual relationships such as friendships between individuals

normative evaluation from adversarial evaluation adversarial attacks typically target a specific trained model by perturbing correctly classified examples in ways that according to the normative definition of the task should not alter the classifier s decision for instance in question answering systems adversarial evaluation may involve inserting an irrelevant sentence into a context passage to induce a change in the system s response jia and liang in contrast the objective of the normative evaluation paradigm is not to exploit specific model weaknesses to induce failure but rather to define desirable task performance unambiguously regarding test only benchmarks a critical principle must be reiterated models should not be fine tuned on the evaluation benchmark itself despite rigorous design efforts benchmarks may inevitably contain unintended statistical regularities fine tuning on such data risks allowing models to learn these spurious correlations liu et al a while pretrained models require instruction on how to perform transfer tasks this training should be conducted using a separate dataset such as those aggregated in glue consequently a generalization leaderboard should prioritize models capable of performing tasks with minimal instruction akin to human few shot learning capabilities yogatama et al concerning efficiency the paid paradigm remains agnostic to pretraining resources and specific model properties including parameter count inference speed and computational training costs these implementation level factors marr are orthogonal to generalization concerns which are formulated at the level of input–output correspondence however if efficiency is a primary concern these properties can be directly optimized by adapting pretraining agnostic benchmarks to incorporate such metrics schwartz et al finally a breakdown by task and phenomenon is recommended

illustrative example and problem statement consider the sentence wuhu a woman fell into the qingge river and the public rallied to rescue her in this instance qingge river constitutes a typographical error the correct designation for the waterway in wuhu china is the qingyu river while human readers can readily identify this discrepancy through geographical knowledge detecting and correcting such context dependent errors remains a significant challenge for current general artificial intelligence systems related work spelling error correction has been the subject of extensive research due to its critical role in diverse applications including information retrieval gao et al optical character recognition ocr afli et al and automated essay scoring burstein chodorow chinese spelling correction csc represents a particularly complex subset of this domain its difficulty is exacerbated by the intrinsic interdependence between spelling correction and chinese word segmentation a relationship that has garnered substantial scholarly attention yu et al yu li tseng et al wang et al early methodologies in csc typically adhered to a multi stage pipeline comprising error detection candidate generation and final candidate selection approaches varied between unsupervised techniques leveraging language models and rule based systems yu li tseng et al and sequential labeling frameworks utilizing conditional random fields crf or hidden markov models hmm tseng et al zhang et al more recently deep learning architectures have been applied to spelling error correction guo et al wang et al notably hong et al employed a sequence to sequence seqseq model with bert as the encoder to transform input sentences containing errors into corrected outputs bert devlin et al is a language representation model based on the transformer encoder architecture it is initially pre trained on a massive corpus using self supervised objectives specifically masked language modeling and next sentence prediction subsequently the model is fine tuned on a smaller set of labeled data to adapt to specific downstream tasks

for a similar purpose tang et al employed memory network with multihop attention and external memory fan et al proposed a multigrained attention network with both finegrained and coarsegrained attentions the pretrained language model bert has made successes in many classification tasks including absa for example xu et al used an additional corpus to posttrain bert and proved its effectiveness in both aspect extraction and absa sun et al converted absa to a sentencepair classification task by constructing auxiliary sentences some other efforts try to directly include the syntactic information in absa since aspects are generally assumed to lie at the heart of this task establishing the syntactic connections between each target aspect and the other words are crucial qiu et al manually defined some syntactic rules to identify the relations between aspects and potential opinion words liu et al obtained partial alignment links with these syntactic rules and proposed a partially supervised word alignment model to extract opinion targets afterward neural network models were explored for this task lakkaraju et al used a recursive neural network rnn to hierarchically encode word representations and to jointly extract aspects and sentiments in another work wang et al combined the recursive neural network with conditional random fields crf moreover dong et al proposed an adaptive recursive neural network adarnn to adaptively propagate the sentiments of words to the target aspect via semantic composition over a dependency tree nguyen et al further combined the dependency and constituent trees of a sentence with a phrase recursive neural network phrasernn in a simpler approach he et al used the relative distance in a dependency tree for attention weight decay they also showed that selectively focusing on a small subset of context words can lead to satisfactory results recently graph neural networks combined with dependency trees have shown appealing effectiveness in absa

domain adaptive pre training via masked language modeling in the context of cross domain sentiment analysis where labeled data in the target domain is unavailable we leverage abundant unlabeled target domain data to perform domain adaptive pre training of the bert model using the masked language modeling mlm objective specifically of the tokens within the target domain corpus are randomly replaced with the token the final hidden representations corresponding to these masked positions are projected through a softmax layer over the vocabulary to predict the original token ids thereby maximizing the likelihood of the correct tokens this pre training strategy effectively mitigates domain shift by aligning the model s knowledge with the target domain for instance in a sentence such as this movie is the model is compelled to learn fine grained representations of domain specific opinion terms e g touching or disturbing relevant to movie reviews a potential challenge arises in the domain distinguish ddt task where sentence pairs may contain mixed domains including sentences from non target domains in the mlm objective could introduce noise and domain bias to address this masking is applied exclusively to tokens within target domain sentences when the ddt label indicates a mixdomain scenario the total loss function for this pre training phase is defined as the sum of the target domain mlm loss and the domain distinguish task loss the entire adaptation process requires approximately five hours of computation on a single nvidia p gpu adversarial training following the domain adaptive pre training which imbues the bert model with target domain knowledge and awareness we employ adversarial training to suppress domain specific features and extract domain invariant representations this approach utilizes a sentiment classifier and a domain discriminator both operating on the hidden state h derived from the special classification embedding token sentiment classifier the sentiment classifier consists of a fully connected layer followed by a softmax activation function to output class probabilities y s text softmax w s h b s quad the classifier is trained using labeled data from the source domain the optimization objective is the cross entropy loss mathcal l sen sum j k hat y s j log y s j quad where hat y s in represents the ground truth label in the source domain and k denotes the number of sentiment polarities domain discriminator the domain discriminator is designed to predict the domain origin of input samples i e source or target in the adversarial framework the parameters of the bert encoder are optimized to maximize the loss of the domain discriminator this minimizes the discriminator s ability to distinguish between domains thereby forcing the model to learn features that are invariant to domain shifts while preserving sentiment information

by contrast sequencelevel training with rl provides an effective means of solving this challenge by treating text generation as a sequential decisionmaking problem by directly optimizing an evaluation score cumulative rewards stateoftheart results have been obtained in many textgeneration tasks however one problem in such a framework is that rewards in rl training are particularly sparse since a scalar reward is typically only available after an entire sequence has been generated furthermore the recurrent models focus more on local fluency and may cause inconsistent semantic meanings for long text generation for rlbased text generation most existing works rely on a modelfree framework which has been criticized for its high variance and poor sample efficiency on the other hand while modelbased rl methods do not suffer from these issues they are usually difficult to train in complex environments further a learned policy is usually restricted by the capacity of an environment model recent developments on modelbased rl combine the advantages of these two approaches and have achieved improved performance by learning a modelfree policy assisted by an environment model in addition modelbased rl has been employed recently to solve problems with extremely sparse rewards with curiositydriven methods in this paper we propose a modelbased imitationlearning method to overcome the aforementioned issues in textgeneration tasks our main idea is to employ an explicit guider network to model the generation environment in the feature space of sentence tokens used to emit intermediate rewards by matching the predicted features from the guider network and features from generated sentences the guider network is trained to encode global structural information of training sentences and thus is useful to guide nexttoken prediction in the generative process within the proposed framework to assist the guider network we also develop a new type of selfattention mechanism to provide highlevel planningahead information and maintain consistent semantic meaning

recent research suggests that it is wise to interpret such findings with caution as noted in both mccoy et al and niven and kao found that bert picked up idiosyncratic patterns in the data for their tasks and not “meaning” beyond such diagnostic research on why large pretrained lms boost such tasks so much we think there is a more fundamental question to be asked here are we climbing the right hill topdown and bottomup theorybuilding there are two different perspectives from which one can look at the progress of a field under a bottomup perspective the efforts of a scientific community are driven by identifying specific research challenges a scientific result counts as a success if it solves such a specific challenge at least partially as long as such successes are frequent and satisfying there is a general atmosphere of sustained progress by contrast under a topdown perspective the focus is on the remote end goal of offering a complete unified theory for the entire field this view invites anxiety about the fact that we have not yet fully explained all phenomena and raises the question of whether all of our bottomup progress leads us in the right direction there is no doubt that nlp is currently in the process of rapid hillclimbing every year states of the art across many nlp tasks are being improved significantly — often through the use of better pretrained lms — and tasks that seemed impossible not long ago are already old news thus everything is going great when we take the bottomup view but from a topdown perspective the question is whether the hill we are climbing so rapidly is the right hill how do we know that incremental progress on today’s tasks will take us to our end goal whether that is “general linguistic intelligence” or a system that passes the turing test or a system that captures the meaning of english arapaho thai or hausa to a linguist’s satisfaction it is instructive to look at the past to appreciate this question computational linguistics has gone through many fashion cycles over the course of its history grammarand knowledgebased methods gave way to statistical methods and today most research incorporates neural methods

comparative analysis and methodology this study evaluates the proposed biencoder model bem against established baselines including wordnet sense s and the most frequent sense mfs as well as a frozen bertbase classifier and recent state of the art approaches in word sense disambiguation wsd notably we contrast our methodology with glossbert huang et al which employs a cross encoder architecture to jointly encode context sentences and glosses in contrast the bem utilizes a more computationally efficient biencoder framework independently encoding contexts and glosses model architecture and optimization the pretrained baseline is constructed by applying a single linear layer followed by a softmax function to the output of the final layer of a frozen bertbase model similarly both encoders within the bem are initialized using bertbase weights representations are derived from the final layer outputs of each encoder the model is optimized using a cross entropy loss function applied to the dot product of these representations comprehensive details regarding hyperparameters and optimization procedures are provided in the supplementary materials note bertbase was selected for initialization due to its superior baseline performance on wsd tasks compared to robertabase see section for further discussion evaluation a comprehensive series of experiments was conducted to assess the efficacy of the bem the evaluation proceeds in two stages first a comparative analysis of the bem against various baselines and prior work on the english all words wsd task section second a granular performance assessment across most frequent sense mfs less frequent sense lfs and zero shot examples section overall results table presents the aggregate f scores for the english all words wsd task raganato et al b the frozen bertbase model serves as a robust baseline surpassing all prior systems that do not incorporate pretraining specifically gas ext hcan and ewise furthermore systems utilizing bert representations without fine tuning glu and svc demonstrate marginal improvements over the pretrained baseline while glossbert achieves enhanced performance through its fine tuned cross encoder approach the proposed bem attains the highest f score on the aggregated all evaluation set the bem outperforms all baselines and existing literature by a margin of at least f points this performance advantage is consistent across all evaluation sets within the wsd framework and holds true for every part of speech category evaluated

and future work in this work we propose and explore the direction of converting mrc datasets into interactive partially observable environments we believe informationseeking behavior is desirable for neural mrc systems when knowledge sources are partially observable and or too large to encode in their entirety where knowledge is by design easily accessible to humans through interaction our idea for reformulating existing mrc datasets as partially observable and interactive environments is straightforward and general it is complementary to existing mrc dataset and models meaning almost all mrc datasets can be used to study interactive informationseeking behavior through similar modifications we hypothesize that such behavior can in turn help in solving realworld mrc problems involving search as a concrete example in real world environments such as the internet different pieces of knowledge are interconnected by hyperlinks we could equip the agent with an action to “click” a hyperlink which returns another webpage as new observations thus allowing it to navigate through a large number of web information to answer difficult questions imrc is difficult and cannot yet be solved however it clearly matches a human’s informationseeking behavior compared to most static and fullyobservable laboratory mrc benchmarks it lies at the intersection of nlp and rl which is arguably less studied in existing literature for our baseline we adopted offtheshelf topperforming mrc and rl methods and applied a memory mechanism which serves as an inductive bias despite being prior research has extensively demonstrated the utility of linguistic analysis in social media for predicting a wide array of societal and psychological phenomena for instance eichstaedt et al established that twitter language serves as a predictor for community level psychological correlates and cardiovascular disease rates similarly demszky et al investigated political polarization while voigt et al analyzed the relationship between police officers linguistic politeness and race a substantial body of literature has further linked social media language to mental health and substance use including depression de choudhury et al eichstaedt et al benton et al park et al and alcohol consumption kiciman et al additional studies have examined how demographic attributes such as gender influence online communication patterns bamman et al wang jurgens voigt et al the present study extends this line of inquiry by investigating the relationship between individual language use and forecasting proficiency to the best of our knowledge this work represents the first computational exploration of this specific domain our research is closely aligned with prior efforts to predict diverse phenomena from textual data notable examples include tan et al who analyzed the impact of wording on message propagation gillick and bamman who examined the correlation between political rhetoric and audience applause and pérez rosas and mihalcea who identified linguistic markers distinguishing truthful from deceptive statements furthermore ganjigunte ashok et al demonstrated that linguistic cues are strong indicators of book sales success while tsur and rappoport developed an unsupervised model to assess the helpfulness of book reviews based on text analysis regarding forecasting specifically several studies have utilized data from the good judgment project mellers et al b among these schwartz et al focused on the linguistic dimensions of the dataset their primary objective was to propose objective metrics as alternatives to subjective ratings for evaluating recommendation quality in their approach justifications provided by one group served as guidance for another these justifications were subsequently evaluated based on their efficacy in persuading recipients to update their predictions thereby generating measurable objective benefits

abstract this study evaluates the efficacy of deebert through experiments conducted on bert and roberta architectures across six glue benchmark datasets the results demonstrate that deebert achieves inference acceleration of approximately while maintaining negligible degradation in model performance on downstream tasks furthermore detailed analysis uncovers distinct patterns within the transformer layers of these models highlighting significant redundancy in both bert and roberta related work bert and roberta represent large scale pretrained language models grounded in the transformer architecture vaswani et al despite their substantial capabilities extensive research has sought to investigate and leverage their inherent overparameterization notable contributions include analyses of redundancy in attention heads by michel et al and voita et al in the domain of compression qbert shen et al employs quantization techniques while layerdrop fan et al utilizes group regularization to facilitate structured pruning during inference regarding knowledge distillation tinybert jiao et al and distilbert sanh et al distill bert into more compact transformer based models whereas tang et al explore distillation into non transformer architectures while this work draws inspiration from the methodologies of cambazoglu et al teerapittayanon et al and huang et al it distinguishes itself by prioritizing the enhancement of model efficiency with minimal compromise to predictive quality early exit mechanisms for bert inference deebert modifies the fine tuning and inference protocols of bert models without altering the pretraining phase the architecture incorporates an off ramp at each transformer layer enabling inference samples to terminate processing early at any given off ramp rather than traversing the full depth of the network the final off ramp corresponds to the original classification layer of the bert model fine tuning procedure for deebert the methodology begins with a pretrained bert model comprising n transformer layers to which n off ramps are appended during fine tuning for a specific downstream task the loss function for the i th off ramp is defined as l i d theta frac d sum x y in d h y f i x theta quad where d denotes the fine tuning training set theta represents the aggregate model parameters x y signifies the feature label pair for a given sample h is the cross entropy loss function and f i represents the output generated by the i th off ramp the network undergoes fine tuning in two distinct stages

ordering adjectives according to integration cost is functionally equivalent to arranging them such that those capable of modifying a broader typical set of nouns are positioned further from the head noun this configuration ensures that each adjective incrementally reduces the entropy of the subsequent noun candidates thereby mitigating the information processing costs associated with entropy reduction hale dye et al information gain we propose a novel efficiency based predictor for adjective ordering information gain this approach conceptualizes the noun phrase—comprising prenominal adjectives followed by the head noun—as a decision tree designed to identify a specific referent within this framework each lexical item partitions the space of potential referents each partition corresponds to a specific information gain quantifying the degree to which the set of candidate referents is reduced consistent with the logic governing integration cost we posit that words yielding lower information gain should be positioned earlier in the sequence this arrangement facilitates a gradual narrowing of the referential set with each successive word as standardly implemented in decision tree algorithms information gain denotes the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning a set based on a specific feature quinlan in the present context the distribution of nouns n is partitioned by a given adjective a resulting in two subsets n a nouns modified by a and its complement n a c nouns not modified by a the information gain of a is defined as the difference between the initial entropy h and the weighted sum of the entropies of the resulting partitions ig a h left frac n a n h frac n a c n h right quad consequently information gain incorporates both positive and negative evidence for instance specifying an adjective such as big partitions the probability distribution of nouns into n text big the subset of n that accepts big as a dependent and n text big c the subset that does not it is crucial to note that in general h is not equivalent to the conditional entropy h

data analysis plays a pivotal role for organizations of all sizes particularly through the extraction and evaluation of public opinions available on the internet the systematic analysis of such sentiments significantly influences customer relationship management specifically companies leverage feedback regarding product deficiencies to drive improvements furthermore the application of sentiment analysis extends beyond commercial product reviews to diverse domains including politics and sports methodologies for sentiment classification are generally categorized into three distinct groups statistical knowledge based and hybrid approaches statistical approaches employ machine learning algorithms such as support vector machines svm or naïve bayes to classify text these methods may utilize either supervised learning which relies on labeled datasets or unsupervised learning which operates on raw data statistical techniques are frequently applied to identify sentiment holders and their targets in contrast knowledge based approaches classify text by mapping it to affect categories based on the presence of unambiguous affective terms e g sad happy afraid or bored these methods utilize lexicons to quantify the frequency of positive and negative words within a text for instance good is categorized as positive while bad is categorized as negative such lexicons may comprise single words or multi word phrases a primary advantage of knowledge based approaches is their independence from trained data however they are often limited by a lack of scalability hybrid approaches seek to mitigate these limitations by integrating statistical and knowledge based methods to enhance overall performance and accuracy regarding specific implementations pak and paroubek developed a dataset comprising documents with known positive and negative polarities their classification method calculates the cosine similarity between a target document and reference documents of known polarity subsequently evaluating the results using the naïve bayes algorithm various lexicon based approaches have been adapted for sentiment classification across different languages with a predominant reliance on adjectives to determine text polarity the development of sentiment lexicons typically follows one of three methodologies manual corpus based or dictionary based compilation manual construction while precise is time intensive and is often combined with other techniques to optimize performance corpus based methods utilize existing lists of sentiment words their polarities and syntactic patterns to identify additional sentiment bearing terms for example hatzivassiloglou and mckeown developed a graph based technique that identifies adjective polarity through conjunctions and employs clustering algorithms to segregate words into positive and negative sets achieving an accuracy rate of finally dictionary based approaches offer a distinct advantage by not requiring precompiled lists of sentiment words
