revised text in generic formal style the prevailing methodology for dialogue state tracking dst in speech processing relies on a two stage pipeline initial speech to text transcription via automatic speech recognition asr followed by state tracking on the resulting text henderson et al henderson mrkšić et al however in the specific context of this study exclusive reliance on asr is impractical due to the significantly elevated word error rates wer observed in our data compared to standard benchmarks for instance while wer in our dataset follows a normal distribution with a mean of the most challenging existing dst datasets rarely exceed a wer of jagfeld vu and standard asr benchmarks typically report even lower error rates park et al consequently standard asr paradigms are ill suited for real world scenarios characterized by such high levels of acoustic noise from an alternative perspective existing literature in audio recognition primarily focuses on discrete tasks such as single word command detection or keyword spotting de andrade et al acoustic event classification e g environmental or urban sounds salamon et al piczak xu et al music pattern recognition or document level classification of entire audio sequences liu et al a comprehensive overview of this domain is provided by mcmahan and rao although certain methodologies in this field share technical similarities with our approach—specifically the application of convolutional neural networks to audio data sainath parada salamon bello —they differ fundamentally in scope our task involves the processing of questions as linguistic units of significantly greater complexity than the simple commands or events typically addressed in audio recognition research furthermore while research into multimodal and multiview deep learning ngiam et al li et al offers valuable insights into the integration of multiple data modalities most existing frameworks do not directly address the specific constraints of our problem key discrepancies include i the underrepresentation of the audio text modality combination ii a lack of requirement for online real time processing capabilities and iii a predominant focus on document level classification rather than sequence labeling zadeh et al conclusions this study proposes a novel framework for speech sequence labeling grounded in the learning of a multimodal representation derived from the temporal alignment of audio signals and their automatic transcriptions this approach enables the real time identification of questions with high accuracy even when trained on a limited annotated dataset empirical results demonstrate that the proposed multimodal representation yields superior accuracy and exhibits greater robustness to acoustic noise compared to unimodal baselines

the proposed framework is designed to be broadly applicable addressing a diverse spectrum of errors frequently encountered by novice programmers this includes complex defects arising from multiple simultaneous conceptual mistakes that span across several lines of code furthermore the system is engineered for scalability ensuring efficient performance in large scale educational environments and non traditional learning settings the core premise of this work is that effective debugging heuristics can be derived from a comprehensive corpus of real world examples wherein novices autonomously resolve their own errors these novice generated bug fix pairs encapsulate static dynamic and contextual data regarding the prevalence of specific error patterns and their corresponding resolutions leveraging this insight we present a novel methodology for localizing defects in unannotated beginner written programs in the absence of test suites by training on a corpus of novice programs the system generates outputs that align with human debugging actions and judgments our system pablo program analytics based localization oracle operates through the following workflow input processing it accepts a dataset of program pairs each consisting of a crashing version and its corrected counterpart feature extraction for each crashing program pablo computes a bag of abstracted terms boat representation in this representation every abstract syntax tree ast node is abstracted into a vector comprising syntactic dynamic and contextual features model training a classifier is trained on these feature vectors inference when presented with a new crashing program written by a novice pablo computes boat vectors for each ast node classifies them to determine the probability of each node being the source of the error and returns a ranked list of the top k program locations we hypothesize that the integration of a rich data corpus with domain specific features enables the automatic classification of common defect categories and root causes thereby assisting novices in identifying the true sources of their bugs the primary contributions of this paper are as follows feature identification we identify a set of features that facilitate precise classification for dynamic imperative languages methodological innovation we introduce and evaluate a suite of static dynamic contextual and slice based features that enable scalable data driven defect localization specifically for python user centric validation through a user study we demonstrate that pablo is perceived as helpful by subjects and is sufficiently general to provide useful debugging hints in scenarios where multiple valid fixes exist—a capability lacking in baseline python interpreters large scale evaluation we conduct a systematic evaluation utilizing a dataset comprising over programs derived from four years of interactions on the pythontutor platform

many existing curricula on quantum computing emphasize the intricacies of underlying quantum physics and hardware architecture while these subjects are intellectually significant they are not prerequisites for mastering quantum computing much as integrated circuit design is not a prerequisite for studying classical algorithms furthermore traditional pedagogical approaches in this field have predominantly focused on theoretical aspects often neglecting practical applications and the programming of quantum systems this report details the experience of developing and delivering cseq introduction to quantum computing and quantum programming in q an undergraduate course at the university of washington the primary objective was to establish a practical and accessible curriculum suitable for a broad academic audience we outline the guiding principles informing our course design describe the key instructional artifacts developed and discuss the lessons derived from this implementation it is our hope that this experience will encourage other educators to adopt a similar software driven approach to quantum computing instruction moreover we posit that facilitating broader engagement in quantum programming is essential to mitigating the projected shortage of skilled professionals in this domain historically quantum computing education has been characterized by a theoretical orientation courses are frequently situated within physics departments prioritizing the theoretical physics of quantum systems and hardware devices or they focus heavily on the mathematical foundations of information theory in these contexts student assessment typically relies on written assignments examinations and final projects comprising oral presentations and written reports on specific algorithms while such courses generally target graduate students undergraduate offerings within computer science departments remain rare although quantum programming has recently been integrated into some introductory courses the majority still fail to provide training in the practical aspects of the field in contrast computer science departments traditionally teach classical computing by introducing a programming language and tasking students with solving foundational problems this progression continues through the study of classical data structures and algorithms implemented within the chosen language before advancing to specialized electives this report advocates for a similar pedagogical trajectory in quantum computing education

existing research indicates that introductory computer science courses centered on programming may be ill suited for students lacking confidence in or interest in coding furthermore prior exposure to programming is not a reliable predictor of success as self reported experience often fails to capture the quantity quality and retention of that experience this study extends prior work by evaluating student performance in a subsequent rigorous ten week c course cs while controlling for specific prior exposure to computer science concepts and the extent of coding utilized to learn and apply them traditionally students at the host institution engage with cs concepts—including algorithms data representation control flow conditional and iterative functions and lists—within the context of python programming during their introductory course cs in this conventional model students begin writing python programs as early as the second week of a ten week term instruction emphasizes basic python syntax while problem solving skills are cultivated through the creation of design documents and testing plans prior to code implementation to investigate whether increased coding exposure in cs confers an advantage in cs we developed an alternative pedagogical framework termed story programming this approach teaches computational concepts outside the context of coding utilizing narrative structures derived from once upon an algorithm for instance pathfinding algorithms are introduced through the narrative of hansel and gretel with students physically enacting these algorithms using pebbles during laboratory sessions the curriculum requires students to read relevant chapters prior to class and complete weekly online assessments topics are generally addressed sequentially with occasional reordering to group related concepts the integration of this narrative based methodology shifts the pedagogical emphasis enabling greater breadth and depth in concept coverage compared to the traditional model specifically the story programming approach provides a more rigorous treatment of data structures and decidability in contrast the traditional approach offers only a cursory examination of data structures via lists and merely alludes to the concept of unsolvable problems

introduction existing graph neural networks gnns predominantly focus on developing diverse aggregation mechanisms to synthesize features from the local neighborhoods of nodes thereby extracting richer and more comprehensive graph representations notable examples include graph convolutional networks gcns proposed by kipf et al which utilize mean pooling to aggregate neighborhood information graphsage hamilton et al which concatenates node features and employs mean max or lstm operators to facilitate inductive learning of node embeddings and mrgnn xu et al which integrates multi resolution features to simultaneously exploit node level subgraph level and global level information furthermore message passing neural networks gilmer et al extend these frameworks by incorporating edge information during the aggregation process however reliance on such aggregation schemes often precipitates the oversmoothing phenomenon li et al wherein aggregated node representations become increasingly indistinguishable as network depth increases this outcome is fundamentally at odds with the objective of extracting distinct and discriminative information to address this limitation this paper proposes a novel distillation operation departing from traditional aggregation strategies our approach leverages a distillation mechanism specifically designed to isolate and extract distinguishable features between semantically similar legal articles problem formulation this section establishes the necessary notations and terminology before formally defining the legal judgment prediction ljp task law cases a legal case comprises a factual description and a set of judgment outcomes see figure the factual description is represented as a text document denoted by f the judgment outcomes may encompass applicable legal articles criminal charges sentencing terms and other relevant determinations let t denote the number of distinct judgment categories the i th judgment result is modeled as a categorical variable y i taking values from the set y i consequently a legal case is formally represented as a tuple f y dots y t law articles legal cases are adjudicated based on a jurisdiction s statutory law also referred to as written law formally the statutory law is defined as a set of legal articles mathcal l triangleq l dots l m where m represents the total number of articles analogous to the representation of case facts each legal article l i is treated as a text document legal judgment prediction in this study the ljp task is defined as the prediction of three specific types of judgment outcomes applicable legal articles criminal charges and terms of penalty

subsequent gender focused initiatives have failed to significantly increase the representation of black women in computing primarily due to an insufficient recognition of how intersectionality—the complex interplay of social constructs such as gender race ethnicity and class—shapes the lived experiences of women of color within the computing ecosystem neglecting these intersectional realities results in erroneous generalizations and consequently ineffective strategies for enhancing both the recruitment and retention of black women in the field to address this challenge this study employs intersectionality as a theoretical framework to examine the overlapping social constructs of race and gender that influence the daily experiences of fourteen black women navigating the computing landscape the primary objectives of this research are twofold to critique the prevailing narrative in computer science cs regarding the attributes of cs material and the associated predictions of success or failure and to propose specific transformations in formal cs education to better support black women through the analysis of fourteen in depth interviews this paper addresses the following research questions how do black women enter the field of computing what are the intersectional experiences of black women at the undergraduate and graduate levels within cs education what implications do these experiences hold for broadening participation in computing this study makes three significant contributions to the field of cs education first it introduces intersectional computing a critical analytical framework designed to explore the lived experiences of black women a population that has historically been overlooked and understudied in computing second these intersectional narratives serve as counter narratives to the dominant discourse surrounding entry into and progression through formal cs education finally the analysis of these experiences offers transformative insights aimed at increasing the likelihood of success for black women in formal cs education while simultaneously dismantling myths regarding their capabilities in the field motivated by the imperative to recruit and retain black women in computing this section begins with a concise overview of intersectionality and concludes by introducing intersectional computing as a novel approach to broadening participation in the discipline

limitations of current methodologies and experimental analysis a significant limitation of prevailing methodologies is their reliance on small scale datasets for evaluation consequently the generalizability of these approaches to massive datasets and real world scenarios remains uncertain to address this the present study utilizes the jecqa dataset zhong et al a for experimental validation as the largest corpus derived from bar examination materials jecqa ensures a rigorous assessment of model performance the dataset comprises multiple choice and multiple answer questions supported by relevant legal articles it categorizes inquiries into knowledge driven questions kdquestions and case analysis questions caquestions providing baseline human performance metrics for comparison we implemented several representative question answering architectures including bidaf seo et al bert devlin et al comatching wang et al and haf zhu et al the results detailed in table indicate that while these models demonstrate efficacy in open domain question answering their performance on legal queries is suboptimal a substantial performance gap persists between current automated systems and human experts in the domain of legal question answering lqa to advance the state of lqa several critical challenges must be addressed legal multi hop reasoning as noted by zhong et al a existing models are capable of single step inference but struggle with multi hop reasoning given the inherent complexity of legal cases which often require synthesizing information across multiple documents or clauses single step reasoning is insufficient legal concept understanding empirical evidence suggests that current models perform better on case analysis than on knowledge retrieval this disparity highlights the ongoing difficulty in modeling legal knowledge effectively since legal knowledge serves as the foundational basis for lqa developing robust mechanisms for its representation is essential conclusion this paper has outlined the current development status of various legalai tasks and identified avenues for future research beyond the applications discussed the legalai domain encompasses numerous other tasks such as legal text summarization and information extraction from contracts regardless of the specific application a hybrid approach leveraging embedding based methods for performance optimization and symbol based methods for enhanced interpretability is recommended furthermore three fundamental challenges remain unresolved knowledge modeling legal reasoning and interpretability these elements constitute the bedrock upon which reliable legalai systems must be built while existing research has begun to address these issues significant progress is still required before legalai can fully and reliably serve the legal domain

exam folder once the exam starts they can click an internal link to launch part a consisting of conceptual questions in blackboard’s native ‘test’ they can also click an external link to launch the vocareum platform for attempting and submitting part b on practical programming students can switch between these two parts at their convenience or even return to the exam folder to access additional parts of the grading can be automated e g based on test cases but all submissions are manually checked before the final grade is confirmed we also still run a plagiarism detection tool in vocareum to mitigate the risk of any potential flaws in the invigilation of the exam or seb policies

furthermore this capability facilitates the recognition of finer grained conversational elements including the avoidance of redundancy the formulation of inquiries and the maintenance of topical relevance these mechanisms collectively govern critical dialogue attributes such as fluency engagement inquisitiveness and empathy consequently commonsense knowledge is indispensable for accurately modeling the structural flow of dialogue and the emotional dynamics among participants given that natural language frequently serves as an indicator of emotional states emotion recognition has garnered significant attention within the field of natural language processing nlp driven by its broad applicability in opinion mining recommender systems and healthcare however emotion recognition in conversation erc has only recently emerged as a focal point for the nlp community largely due to the increasing availability of public conversational datasets erc offers substantial utility in analyzing social media interactions and enabling real time dialogue assessment which is instrumental in domains such as legal proceedings interviews and e health services distinct from standard sentence level emotion recognition erc necessitates the contextual modeling of individual utterances relying heavily on preceding dialogue turns and the temporal sequence of speaker interactions recent studies indicate that both lexicon based and conventional deep learning approaches when applied without conversational context perform suboptimally on erc datasets this limitation stems from their failure to account for conversation specific factors including contextual cues the temporality of speaker turns and speaker specific information to address these challenges this paper introduces cosmic a commonsense guided framework designed for robust emotion identification within conversational contexts

paraphrase augmented response generation figure illustrates the architecture of the proposed paraphrase based data augmentation framework which comprises three primary components a paraphrase generation model a quality filtering module and a response generation model the operational details of each module are described below paraphrase generation model the paraphrase generation model employs a sequence to sequence seqseq architecture featuring a context encoder and two distinct decoders one for action decoding and another for paraphrase decoding the context encoder processes the concatenation of the preceding system response r t and the current user utterance u t encoding them into hidden states these states subsequently drive the decoding of the previous system action a t defined as a token sequence as introduced by zhang et al finally the paraphrase decoder generates the paraphrased utterance u p t by leveraging hidden states from both the encoder and the action decoder the generation process is formalized as follows h a t text seqseq r t u t quad u p t text seqseq r t u t mid h a t quad where h a t represents the hidden states of the action decoder to facilitate the transfer of specific entities a copy mechanism gu et al is integrated to replicate words from the input utterances into both the system action and the generated paraphrase furthermore an attention mechanism connects the two decoders this linkage allows the action decoding process to inform the paraphrase decoding thereby enhancing the model s awareness of the dialog context paraphrase filtering module generated paraphrases are subsequently evaluated by a filtering module to determine their suitability as additional training instances the primary objective is to retain paraphrases that preserve the semantic intent and dialog function of the original utterance consequently the filter excludes any paraphrase that fails to include all semantic slots present in the original input additionally consistent with the criteria established during the initial paraphrase data construction the module discards instances that exhibit semantic divergence or excessive surface form similarity to the original utterance specifically if the calculated diversity score falls below a defined threshold e g in experimental settings the paraphrase pair is rejected due to insufficient linguistic variation for original utterances that yield no valid paraphrases following this initial filtering the diversity threshold is iteratively relaxed until at least one acceptable paraphrase is identified for each instance

this work has generally employed a supervised machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained to distinguish between speech samples produced by patients with dementia and those from controls using a set of deliberately engineered or computationally identified features however on account of the limited training data available overfitting is a concern this is particularly problematic in dat where the nature of linguistic anomalies varies between patients and with ad progression in the current study we take a different approach focusing our attention on the perplexity of a speech sample as estimated by neural lms trained on transcripts of the speech of participants completing a cognitive task to date the most successful approach to using lm perplexity as a sole distinguishing feature between narratives by dementia patients and controls was proposed by fritsch et al and replicated by klumpp et al the approach consists of training two recurrent neural lms one on transcripts from patients with dementia and the other on transcripts from controls the difference between the perplexities estimated with these two lms results in very high classification accuracy auc reported by both studies the explanation for this performance offered by fritsch et al relies on observations that patients with dat describe the picture in an unforeseen way and their speech frequently diverts from the content of the picture contains repetitions incomplete utterances and refers to objects in the picture using words like “thing” or “something” this explanation however conflicts with the findings by klumpp et al that demonstrate similarly high classification accuracy auc with a single hidden layer nonrecurrent neural network and bagofwords input features suggesting that while word sequences play a role it may not be as large as previously believed by fritsch et al klumpp et al ’s explanation contrasts “local” with “global language properties” of the picture descriptions being captured by recurrent neural lms vs the nonrecurrent bagofwords neural network classifier respectively both of these explanations are based on informal qualitative observations of the data and are not entirely satisfying because both fail to explain the fact that it is precisely the difference between the control and dementia lms that is able to discriminate between patients and controls

this paper presents the design and evaluation of an autonomous systems curriculum and associated community centered on a th scale open source autonomous racecar platform the program is structured to facilitate project based training in the core domains of perception planning control and ethical embodied intelligence following three iterations of the course the study validates four primary hypotheses scalability and accessibility the th scale platform successfully executes full scale autonomous vehicle av code and algorithms establishing itself as a cost effective accessible and safe environment for algorithmic development and testing ethical integration utilizing the platform and its simulator student teams successfully encoded moral constraints into their implementations thereby creating a robust foundation for discourse regarding av ethics and risk mitigation community sustainability a thriving community surrounding the th scale racecar ecosystem has emerged sustained by two annual autonomous racing competitions career outcomes participants have secured employment with leading industry entities including tesla nvidia honda aurora nuro rivian zoox built robotics and the drone racing league while project based software engineering courses aim to ground learning in professional workflows replicating every industry process within an academic setting is often constrained by logistical limitations previous research has largely relied on retroactive analysis of student reactions to various processes in contrast this study employs a proactive methodology that synthesizes findings from a field study of professional agile extreme programming xp teams with an established team process taxonomy this approach enables the strategic selection of specific team processes to enhance a mature software engineering curriculum guided by field data three xp processes were selected based on their professional significance and their ability to comprehensively cover the taxonomy s primary categories a comparative case study between the augmented course design and the original curriculum yielded the following results the integration of these new processes did not impede learning opportunities for xp practices already present in the course even without additional resources student teams derived benefits from the new processes comparable to those experienced by professional teams with students explicitly recognizing their utility and value consequently this approach empowers instructors to make informed decisions regarding the incorporation of xp processes thereby enhancing student learning outcomes and better preparing them for professional careers through exposure to a more comprehensive set of methodologies it is recommended that course designers operating under resource constraints adopt this methodology to evaluate and refine their own project based curricula

supplementary data sources in addition to the primary dataset the experimental framework incorporates a corpus of articles cited by users within the tweet collection these articles originate from media outlets with established bias profiles as documented by the media bias fact check website user stance detection to analyze the stance of influencers regarding specific topics this study employs a two stage methodology first determining the stances of individual twitter users and second projecting these stances onto the influencers cited by said users this approach relies on the premise that users who include links to articles in their tweets are likely to endorse the content s message similarly users who retweet content verbatim without additional commentary are presumed to align with the original tweet s stance user labeling for each topic is achieved through a hybrid approach combining projection clustering and supervised classification initially an unsupervised method darwish et al is utilized to identify clusters of core vocal users in this phase users are mapped into a lower dimensional space based on similarity metrics and subsequently clustered following this unsupervised step a supervised classifier is trained using the two largest identified clusters to label a broader user population for this classification task fasttext a deep neural network text classifier demonstrated to be effective across various text classification domains joulin et al is employed

the bio labeling scheme defined as the set y text b text i text o xu et al serves as the output space for each token in the source sentence in this framework the labels b i and o denote the beginning of an aspect the interior of an aspect and non aspect tokens respectively the proposed architecture begins by representing source sentence tokens using glove embeddings pennington et al these embeddings are subsequently encoded via a bidirectional long short term memory bilstm network liu et al to generate initial hidden state vectors denoted as h text lstm i to address long distance dependencies a self attention mechanism vaswani et al is applied yielding attention weighted hidden states h text att i the final feature representation for the i th token hat h i is constructed by concatenating these two vectors hat h i h text lstm i oplus h text att i conditioned on hat h i the model estimates the emission probabilities for the bio labels a fully connected dense layer maps the feature vector to the labeling space expressed as p i text bio f text den hat h i to determine the optimal label sequence across the entire sentence a linear chain conditional random field crf wang et al is employed over the emission probabilities tokens identified with b or i labels are subsequently extracted as aspects the aspect extractor is trained by maximizing the log likelihood of the sequence labeling task luo et al sum i n e log p y mid f text den hat h i hat w hat b quad where n e represents the number of tokens in the training set hat w denotes the trainable transition matrix parameters within the crf and hat b represents the bias term recycling mechanism while the extractor can be trained on benchmark datasets provided by semeval tasks pontiki et al training the positioner component independently is impractical due to the scarcity of negative examples characterized by boundary mis specification

numerous studies have proposed neural architectures for semantic parsing e g jia and liang zhong et al wang et al hwang et al with hwang et al notably employing a bert based model in these works as in the present study models are trained under full supervision regarding the mapping from natural language to logical forms without incorporating feedback derived from the execution of those forms specifically the environmental effects of the denoted actions conversely significant progress has been made in weakly supervised semantic parsing artzi and zettlemoyer liang et al guu et al where logical forms are treated as latent variables and supervision is provided solely through execution outcomes furthermore recent approaches have demonstrated efficacy by bypassing discrete logical forms entirely riedel et al neelakantan et al we anticipate that the dataset introduced herein which offers supervision at the logical form level while leveraging an underlying grammar and environment capable of generating virtually infinite weakly supervised signals or execution rewards will facilitate the investigation of such models the minecraft environment particularly through the malmo project johnson et al has served as a foundational testbed for various machine learning research efforts it is frequently utilized for reinforcement learning rl shu et al udagawa et al alaniz oh et al tessler et al where agents are trained to complete tasks by issuing low level actions and receiving success based rewards additionally large scale datasets for rl and imitation learning have been curated guss et al a b while some of these studies e g oh et al incorporate simplified templated language for compositional task specification the task space and linguistic scope in those works remain more constrained than those presented here this distinction arises because training an rl agent to execute the scripted primitives defined by our grammar is inherently nontrivial necessitating a more complex framework than the low level action spaces typically employed in prior rl literature

significant barrier to progress particularly within the maxtemps context was the inability to explicitly define the relationships between tasks at the code level for instance students attempting the reshape first approach were unable to determine how to maintain state across sublists the critical missing element was the conceptualization of an appropriate data structure—specifically a list of lists—to manage these sublists without articulating this structure students could not define the expected output of a reshaping function select the necessary code constructs for implementation or determine the appropriate input data for subsequent processing functions interviews and a review of course syllabi suggest that students had not previously encountered problems involving list of lists or the restructuring of flat lists into nested forms consequently they lacked retrievable patterns for data reshaping similar difficulties arose in the process until and collect first approaches where students struggled to track specific data points such as the current maximum value or the current sublist these strategies also necessitate recursion over a modified list suffix yet students lacked a pre existing schema for modifying list suffixes to facilitate recursion additionally some participants failed to adapt retrieved patterns to novel contexts in the rainfall task students encountered difficulties handling the sentinel value interview data revealed that they had not previously solved problems requiring premature termination of list computations at a specific element rather than at the list s end the empty list this impasse stemmed from an inability to recognize the functional similarity between the sentinel and the empty list as base cases ultimately a recurring issue among those who became stuck was the failure to identify the inherent limitations of the patterns they had retrieved

it is observed across all three experimental datasets that text overlapping methods consistently yielded the lowest mean scores the integration of distributional measures resulted in relative mean improvements of and over text overlapping baselines respectively the most substantial gains were recorded in the fnwm dataset followed by the smt dataset given that texts in both the fnwm and smt datasets are systematically longer than those in the onwn and headline datasets these findings suggest that the efficacy of distributional measures is more pronounced in longer textual contexts furthermore the sts qgrams text similarity function demonstrated its robustness as a strong text overlapping baseline for semantic textual similarity sts tasks in the context of the corests shared task at sem our initial systems achieved rankings of th nd and rd these systems utilized a novel parameterized resemblance coefficient derived from tversky s ratio model combined with soft cardinality had the three proposed text similarity functions—which incorporate q gram overlap and distributional measures derived from the ukwack corpus—been employed they would have secured th th and th places respectively moreover a simple average of these three functions would have attained th place a key conclusion from this analysis is that plain text overlapping methods are consistently enhanced through the incremental application of the proposed distributional measures an effect that is particularly evident in longer texts ultimately the proposed text similarity functions have proven to be highly competitive despite their methodological simplicity and reliance on limited resources

the study comprised a control group consisting of males and females and a treatment group comprising males and females the notable reduction in participant numbers within the treatment group is attributed to the increased workload associated with the intervention relative to the control condition data collection was conducted using four distinct instruments the first instrument gathered fundamental demographic information including age gender race and socioeconomic status ses the second instrument was the revised purdue spatial visualization test psvt r which consists of items presenting two dimensional representations of three dimensional objects each item displays an example object illustrating a before and after state following a specific rotation followed by a target object in its initial state and five potential outcomes resulting from the application of the same rotation the third instrument was a validated revision of parker et al s second computer science exam designated herein as the scsr this assessment comprises nine questions covering topics standard to introductory computer science curricula designed to be language agnostic the fourth instrument was a modified version of dorn and tew s computing attitude survey casv augmented with gender specific items derived from wiebe s survey the final instrument consisted of items utilizing a point likert scale to assess student attitudes toward computation instructional delivery followed the modular framework established by cooper et al comprising eight modules administered over the semester however diverging from cooper et al s face to face approach this study utilized an online delivery model to ensure consistency across all sections a hybrid pedagogical strategy was employed integrating online video lectures with structured worksheets each module included a primary instructional video created by the researchers at least one example video a link to online practice resources and a printable worksheet to maintain student engagement all instructional videos were limited to a duration of eight minutes the online practice component developed by sorby provided additional examples and practice questions the worksheets totaling pages featured a combination of multiple choice and manual drawing exercises participants were allotted a period of one to two weeks to complete and submit each worksheet

of the students who completed the learning style assessment were classified as active learners as neutral learners and as reflective learners the study was conducted over a nine week semester during with student preferences additionally this approach mitigates the validity threat of lower performing students being disproportionately influenced by higher performing partners thereby facilitating a more accurate interpretation of the effects of paired work qualitative feedback from participants further supported a preference for working with peers of comparable skill levels the data were this paper is organized as follows section ii provides a brief literature review on multimodal sentiment analysis section iii briefly discusses the baseline methods experimental results and discussion are given in section iv finally section v concludes the paper in ekman et al carried out extensive studies on facial expressions their research showed that universal facial expressions are able to provide sufficient clues to detect emotions recent studies on speechbased emotion analysis have focused on identifying relevant acoustic features such as fundamental frequency pitch intensity of utterance bandwidth and duration as to fusing audio and visual modalities for emotion recognition two of the early works were done by de silva et al and chen et al both works showed that a bimodal system yielded a higher accuracy than any unimodal system while there are many research papers on audiovisual fusion for emotion recognition only a few research works have been devoted to multimodal emotion or sentiment analysis using text clues along with visual and audio modalities wollmer et al fused information from audio visual and text modalities to extract emotion and sentiment metallinou et al fused audio and text modalities for emotion recognition both approaches relied on featurelevel fusion in this paper we study the behavior of the method proposed in in the aspects rarely addressed by other authors such as speaker independence generalizability of the models and performance of individual modalities we employ convolutional neural networks cnn for textual feature extraction following we obtain ngram features from each utterance using three distinct convolution filters of sizes and respectively each having featuremaps outputs are then subjected to maxpooling followed by rectified linear unit relu activation these activations are concatenated and fed to a dimensional dense layer which is regarded as the textual utterance representation this network is trained at utterance level with the emotion labels b audio and visual feature extraction identical to we use dcnn and opensmile for visual and acoustic feature extraction respectively c fusion in order to fuse the information extracted from different modalities we concatenated the feature vectors representative of the given modalities and sent the combined vector to a classifier for the classification this scheme of fusion is called featurelevel fusion

the following principles and examples illustrate the ethical and methodological framework of the ripple platform consent upon initial platform access users are presented with a consent form requesting permission to utilize their data to enhance the academic developers understanding of the learning process the system permits users to modify their consent status at any time while platform access is granted to all users irrespective of their consent decision data utilized for research purposes is strictly limited to those who have provided and have not subsequently withdrawn their consent transparency to ensure transparency the platform provides researchers with a standardized consent form this document is subject to mandatory updates to reflect any modifications regarding the purpose scope or specific details of planned research initiatives nonmaleficence the platform s terms of service explicitly caution researchers against conducting studies that result in interventions potentially detrimental to student performance or learning experiences or those that constitute an inefficient use of student time comparative context and methodological approach other educational technologies such as peerwise and assistments have successfully supported extensive research peerwise has facilitated over publications primarily examining the impact of gamification and the development of high quality learning resources by students similarly assistments has enabled publications focusing on adaptive learning and the personalization of feedback the success of these platforms is attributed to distinct operational models peerwise grants instructors access to data from their own courses rather than retaining exclusive rights for developers while the assistments ecosystem supports cost effective experimental design through randomized control trials rcts drawing inspiration from these precedents ripple aims to empower instructors to conduct rigorous large scale educational research the platform facilitates collaboration between instructors and system developers to execute rcts although the application of rcts in education has been the subject of significant debate regarding their opportunities and challenges they remain the gold standard for establishing causality in many educational research domains to address the ethical complexities associated with rcts in educational settings ripple also supports quasi experimental designs wherein students self select their engagement with an intervention it is acknowledged that quasi experiments are susceptible to threats to internal validity specifically self selection may be influenced by particular student traits or needs potentially resulting in a lack of baseline comparability between control and experimental groups

evaluation metrics for token scoring and rationale faithfulness for models employing continuous or soft token scoring evaluation metrics are predicated on token rankings specifically rewarding the assignment of higher scores to ground truth tokens the primary metric utilized is the area under the precision recall curve auprc derived by varying the decision threshold across token scores while the rationales provided for most tasks are generally sufficient to support judgments they are not necessarily exhaustive however for specific datasets comprehensive rationales have been explicitly curated for a subset of the test instances consequently on these datasets recall serves as a direct measure of comprehensiveness on other datasets this metric functions only as a noisy proxy table delineates the corpora containing such comprehensive rationales within their test sets measuring faithfulness as previously noted a model may generate rationales that appear plausible to human evaluators yet were not instrumental in generating the model s output in many applications it is essential that rationales genuinely explain model predictions that is the extracted rationales for a given instance must have meaningfully influenced the prediction for that same instance we term such explanations faithful rationales determining the optimal method for quantifying rationale faithfulness remains an open research question in this initial iteration of the eraser framework we propose straightforward metrics grounded in prior literature zaidan et al yu et al specifically following the approach of yu et al we define metrics to assess two distinct properties comprehensiveness whether all features necessary for a prediction were selected and sufficiency whether the extracted rationales contain sufficient signal to derive the correct disposition to quantify comprehensiveness we employ the construction of contrast examples zaidan et al for a given input instance x i we generate a contrast example tilde x i by removing the predicted rationales r i from the original input in a classification context let m x i j denote the original probability assigned by model m to the predicted class j we then evaluate the model s predicted probability for class j when presented with the modified input tilde x i i e with the supporting rationales removed intuitively a faithful model should exhibit reduced confidence in its original prediction once the supporting rationales are excised from the input

elevated correlation coefficients may engender unwarranted confidence in the reliability of evaluation metrics upon the exclusion of outlier systems the disparity in correlation between bleu and alternative metrics e g chrf yisi and esim becomes more pronounced in extreme cases outliers may artificially inflate correlation values masking a lack of genuine association between the metric and human judgments for the remaining systems consequently future evaluations should routinely assess correlations following the removal of outlier data points furthermore a single correlation coefficient is insufficient to characterize the underlying error patterns as identical values may correspond to distinct data distributions therefore visualizing metric scores against human scores—via scatter plots for individual language pairs or aggregated representations—is essential for deriving meaningful insights into metric reliability given that metrics are primarily employed to compare system performance this study also examines the interpretative value of score differentials regarding human judgments while a bleu difference of points is frequently cited in published literature as indicative of improvement our analysis demonstrates that such a magnitude corresponds to genuine quality enhancements as perceived by humans in only approximately of cases although this analysis treats direct assessment as the gold standard despite its inherent limitations the findings suggest that the prevailing convention of asserting empirical improvements based on marginal bleu differences lacks substantive validity in conclusion this work reinforces the argument for retiring bleu as the de facto standard in favor of more robust alternatives such as chrf yisi or esim which offer superior sensitivity to empirical improvements nevertheless human evaluation must remain the definitive benchmark for the continued advancement of machine translation and the establishment of statistically significant improvements over prior work automatic metrics serve as inadequate substitutes for direct human assessment

the tokenization procedure for bert is decomposed into two sequential stages initial word level tokenization followed by the potential decomposition of individual words into multiple subword units specifically a word w is segmented into a sequence of subwords w dots w k such that their concatenation reconstructs the original word i e text cat w dots w k w subsequently each layer of the model generates corresponding vector representations w c dots w k c to derive a unified word representation w c from these subword vectors four pooling mechanisms are evaluated w c f w c dots w k c quad text where f in text min text max text mean text last in this formulation text min cdot and text max cdot denote element wise minimum and maximum pooling respectively text mean cdot represents the arithmetic average and text last cdot selects the final subword vector w k c context combination strategies two distinct approaches are proposed for defining contexts c dots c n and aggregating the associated representations w c dots w c n decontextualized approach for a target word w a single context is defined as c w the word is fed into the pretrained model in isolation and the resulting output vector serves as its representation if the word is split into multiple subwords the aforementioned subword pooling mechanism is applied aggregated approach recognizing that the decontextualized in the united states the prevailing oversight regarding student technology access stems from two concurrent narratives the characterization of current college students as digital natives or members of a distinct net generation although empirical research has refuted these assertions the popular perception persists while it is widely assumed that nearly all current students possess connected devices this belief is primarily supported by survey based evidence for instance a pew research center study indicated that of undergraduates owned a laptop and owned a desktop computer similarly the ecar annual report cited a laptop ownership rate of however prior investigations into technology access rely heavily on self reported surveys which are susceptible to various biases including respondent perception errors self selection bias and the prerequisite of device ownership to access the survey itself consequently these methods lack the objectivity systematic rigor and continuity required for comprehensive analysis to address these limitations this study leverages operational usage data derived from campus wireless networks and computer laboratories unlike qualitative surveys where non response often results in missing data the absence of usage in an operational dataset constitutes a valid data point this approach enables the systematic coverage of every student s on campus activity we introduce a novel methodology utilizing unsupervised machine learning to analyze and visualize student usage patterns of personal laptops and computer laboratories furthermore we compare these observed behaviors against self reported data from qualitative surveys finally we propose a predictive model that incorporates student computing usage as a determinant of academic success the study is guided by the following research questions rq what are the usage patterns of a personal laptops and b computer laboratories as observed through operational datasets rq do the usage patterns of personal laptops and computer laboratories serve as factors influencing the academic success of computer science cs students the study encompasses the undergraduate cs student population over a six quarter period from september to june the cohort consists of degree seeking undergraduate adults including cs students data were collected from multiple sources to address the research objectives specifically to address rq a session logs were extracted from the university s authenticated wireless networks used for internet access the visitor network was excluded from the analysis as it does not identify individual users and accounts for a negligible portion of total traffic less than

with these in mind our choice of tasks and datasets for gluecos are based on the following principles • we choose a variety of tasks ranging from simpler ones on which the research community has already achieved high accuracies to relatively more complex on which very few attempts have been made • we desire to evaluate models on languagepairs from different language families and on a varied number of tasks to enable detailed analysis and comparison this led us to choose englishhindi and englishspanish as we found researched upon datasets for almost all tasks in our benchmark for these language pairs • english and spanish are written in the roman script while englishhindi datasets can contain hindi words written either in the original devanagari script or in the roman script thus adding script variance as an additional parameter to analyse upon • we include multiple datasets from each language pair where available so that results can be compared across datasets for the same task due to the lack of standardized datasets we choose to create our own traintestvalidation splits for some tasks also we use an offtheshelf transliterator and language detector where necessary details of which can be found in appendix a table shows all the datasets that we use with their statistics while table shows the codeswitching statistics of the data in terms of standardized metrics for codeswitching briefly the codemixing metrics include • codemixing index cmi the fraction of language dependent tokens not belonging to the matrix language in the utterance • average switchpoints sp avg the average number of intrasentential language switchpoints in the corpus • multilingual index mindex a wordcountbased measure quantifying the inequality of distribution of language tags in a corpus of at least two languages • probability of switching iindex the proportion of the number of switchpoints in the corpus relative to the number of languagedependent tokens • burstiness the quantification of whether switching occurs in bursts randomly similar to a poisson process or has a more periodic character • language entropy le the bits of information needed to describe the distribution of language tags • span entropy se the bits of information needed to describe the distribution of language spans

each candidate answer is assigned a binary label of either true or false the commonsense explanations cose dataset rajani et al consists of multiple choice questions and answers derived from talmor et al augmented with supporting rationales these rationales are provided in two forms highlighted extracted text snippets and free text open ended reasoning descriptions given the current focus on extractive rationales the eraser framework incorporates only the highlighted snippets in alignment with talmor et al the training and validation sets have been repartitioned to establish a canonical test split the esnli dataset camburu et al extends the snli corpus bowman et al by including rationales marked within the premise and or hypothesis alongside natural language explanations which are not utilized in this study annotation guidelines for esnli require that for entailment pairs at least one word in the premise be highlighted for contradiction pairs at least one word in both the premise and the hypothesis must be highlighted and for neutral pairs highlighting is restricted to the hypothesis regarding human agreement table reports inter annotator consistency across multiple annotators and documents for the extracted rationales all datasets demonstrate high cohen s kappa kappa values cohen indicating substantial or better agreement within the eraser framework models are evaluated on two dimensions predictive performance and the quality of the extracted rationales predictive performance is assessed using established metrics specific to each task the following section details the proposed metrics for evaluating the quality of extracted rationales it should be noted that these metrics are not claimed to be optimal rather the release of eraser is intended to stimulate further research into the most effective methods for measuring the quality of model explanations in natural language processing

a primary objective of both course iterations was to mitigate the potential for ableist perspectives as previously identified by shinohara bennett and wobbrock prior to students engaging directly with visually impaired co designers the following analysis synthesizes findings derived from direct observations contemporaneous instructor documentation and a preliminary review of student reflections and written submissions it is posited that this examination of the pedagogical experience may offer valuable insights for educators instructing similar courses particularly those considering the implementation of participatory design methodologies instructional delivery across the modules employed a diverse array of pedagogical methods modules three through six which focused on specific disabilities were extended in duration and in certain instances incorporated content from relevant medical literature the instructional strategy for these modules involved a structured progression the initial lecture addressed the etiology of the specific disability while subsequent lectures two and three where applicable emphasized assistive technologies case studies and multimedia examples such as demonstrations of technology usage student feedback gathered through written reflections on the canvas learning management system and during in class discussions indicated a generally positive reception of the multimedia components however students noted that the slide based lecture materials were less engaging to address this in future semesters the instructional team plans to develop original video content to supplement existing public resources this curated video library is intended for dissemination to the broader research community via publicly accessible video platforms the participatory design process in phase ii emerged as a significant success of the course student engagement remained high throughout the design activities with reflections and discussions consistently identifying this component as one of the most compelling aspects of the curriculum this process was facilitated by the south carolina national federation of the blind which assisted in recruiting visually impaired co designers located in proximity to the university campus in total three co designers participated meeting with their assigned student teams comprising two graduate teams and one undergraduate team three to four times each these collaborative teams successfully conceptualized three autonomous vehicle assistive technology prototypes resulting in the production of low fidelity models utilizing materials such as paper and cardboard

experimental configuration and model architecture in our experiments the evaluation thresholds for bleu and diversity scores are set to and respectively for response generation we employ two state of the art sequence to sequence seqseq architectures tscp lei et al for single domain tasks and damd zhang et al for multi domain scenarios the operational workflow of our framework is detailed below with reference to the tscp model see fig the damd architecture follows an analogous process differing primarily by the inclusion of an additional action span decoder situated between the belief span decoder and the response decoder the model input consists of the concatenation of the current user utterance u t the preceding belief span b t and the prior system response r t here u t represents either the original user utterance u t or a paraphrased variant u p t generated by the paraphrase module the architecture utilizes a two stage decoding network wherein the belief span and system response are generated sequentially via a copy mechanism to facilitate end to end optimization an attention connection is established between the paraphrase decoder and the belief span decoder this linkage enables gradients from the response generation model to backpropagate to the paraphrase generation model thereby allowing the response generator to guide the production of higher quality paraphrases and vice versa this process is mathematically formulated as h b t text seqseq r t u t b t mid h u p t quad r t text seqseq r t u t mid h b t quad where h b t and h u p t denote the hidden states of the belief span decoder and the paraphrase decoder respectively training and evaluation the model undergoes joint optimization via supervised learning specifically the cross entropy loss is computed across four distinct decoders using the following data sources system action labels paraphrase data as collected in the preceding section dialog state labels and reference responses these this study demonstrates that the proportion of gold standard trees within the english k best list is significantly higher than that observed for german and czech furthermore the trees in the english k best list exhibit greater structural diversity thereby facilitating the reranker s ability to distinguish between high and low quality candidates the remainder of this paper is organized as follows section reviews related work on reranking for neural dependency parsing section provides a detailed description of the various reranking models employed in section we first replicate existing reranking results for english and evaluate our proposed reranker on english data subsequently we assess the performance of these models on two morphologically rich languages presenting both the evaluation results and our analysis the paper concludes in section related work reranking is a widely adopted technique designed to enhance the parsing performance of base parsers the process typically involves generating the top k candidate trees via a base parser followed by a reranking phase that incorporates additional features unavailable to the initial parser this approach provides a more global and comprehensive perspective on the candidate trees contrasting with the local and often incomplete features utilized by the base parser discriminative rerankers have proven highly effective in constituency parsing collins and koo charniak and johnson however traditional feature rich rerankers suffer from a significant drawback the reliance on a large number of potentially sparse features increases the risk of overfitting and diminishes system efficiency neural rerankers address these limitations by learning dense low dimensional feature representations that offer superior generalization capabilities thereby mitigating the risk of overfitting the first neural reranker was introduced by socher et al for constituency parsing this method utilizes a recursive neural network to process parse tree nodes in a bottom up manner learning dense feature representations for the entire tree this approach was subsequently adapted for dependency parsing by le and zuidema

returns observed at higher data volumes this approach is particularly advantageous for low resource named entity recognition ner tasks such as those involving only labeled samples per language table additionally our investigation into performance trade offs demonstrates that parameter compression does not inherently guarantee reduced inference latency nor does latency reduction necessarily imply parameter efficiency the interplay between model performance parameter compression inference latency and f scores is detailed in figure and appendix table in the context of multilingual word embeddings while random initialization yields acceptable results a more parameter efficient and effective strategy involves initializing embeddings via singular value decomposition svd applied to fine tuned mbert embeddings this method achieves the highest performance on downstream tasks table finally the proposed distillation techniques and strategies are model architecture and language agnostic although this study focuses on ner and classification tasks the methodology is readily extensible to arbitrary tasks and languages

as indicated in the accompanying table recurrent neural network language model rnnlm vectors consistently demonstrate superior performance compared to latent semantic analysis lsa vectors of equivalent dimensionality furthermore an increase in dimensionality appears to correlate with enhanced information retention and improved model efficacy consequently dimensional rnnlm vectors were selected to construct the final directional similarity model the second model designed to measure relational similarity is predicated on lexical patterns it is well established that the contexts in which two words co occur provide significant cues for identifying semantic relations for instance the frequent occurrence of the text fragment x such as y strongly suggests a classinclusion taxonomic relation wherein y is a subtype of x this approach has proven effective notably the utd system leveraged lexical pattern mining from large corpora to outperform other participants in the semeval relational similarity task to maximize the identification of co occurrences for each word pair a comprehensive document set comprising the gigaword corpus wikipedia and los angeles times articles was utilized totaling over gigabytes of text for every word pair w w appearing within the same sentence the intervening words were extracted as the context or raw pattern for example in the phrase x such as y the sequence such as constitutes the extracted context for the pair x y to mitigate noise contexts exceeding nine words were excluded resulting in a total collection of patterns each raw pattern was treated as a feature with its value defined as the logarithm of its occurrence count a probabilistic classifier was subsequently developed to determine the association between specific contexts and semantic relations in this framework word pairs belonging to a target relation served as positive examples while pairs from all other relations served as negative examples a total of classifiers were trained each utilizing examples the degree of relational similarity for any given word pair is then derived from the output of the corresponding classifier although this methodology aligns with standard supervised learning paradigms the high dimensionality of the feature space presents a significant challenge with approximately one million features and only training examples the model is highly susceptible to overfitting without rigorous regularization this vulnerability likely accounts for the suboptimal performance observed when applying support vector machines svms to pattern based features

the course assessment framework comprises online quizzes group guided assessments ggas a final group project and a final examination while online quizzes and ggas utilize a multiple choice format to address similar problem sets the final examination is exclusively non multiple choice all assessments are conducted under open book conditions however while students may utilize any resources including digital materials for assignments and quizzes the final examination restricts permitted resources to hard copy formats only take home assignments are allocated a two week completion window online quizzes are administered within a minimum hour window on the day following the corresponding gga and may be completed remotely each quiz consists of five multiple choice questions with a minute time limit the instructional modules do not follow a strictly sequential progression feedback on assignments is provided prior to the ggas and the gga and online quiz for module i overlap temporally with module i the gga is a minute in class collaborative activity designed to solve a series of problems it consists of ten multiple choice questions structured into individual and group phases initially students must answer all ten questions individually and submit their responses online subsequently students form groups to discuss the problems and record collective answers the recommended and maximum group size is five students though this limit may be adjusted to accommodate absences feedback mechanisms differ between the two phases no immediate feedback is provided during the individual component whereas the group stage employs an immediate feedback assessment technique ifat in this stage the entire group receives a uniform score based on their collective performance groups are provided with an ifat form referred to as a scratchy containing four or five answer options a–d or a–e for each question the group scratches the box corresponding to their selected answer if a star symbol is revealed the answer is correct and full marks are awarded if the answer is incorrect the group may attempt again however the score for that question decreases with each additional attempt no credit is awarded if all possible answer options for a question are scratched without revealing the star

model architecture and latent representation in the context of variational autoencoders the treatment of discrete latent variables differs between architectures specifically within a vector quantized variational autoencoder vqvae the embedding for the latent variable hat z ml is directly retrieved as e m hat z ml conversely in a categorical vae each discrete latent variable is mapped to a continuous space via a dedicated trainable embedding layer regarding the integration of these embeddings into the downstream model local model when the number of mixture components m exceeds one the m individual embeddings are concatenated to construct a single real valued vector representation for the l th latent variable global model the m embeddings are utilized directly without concatenation the resulting sequence of real valued vectors of length t or m serves as the source input for a standard single layer transformer encoder decoder architecture vaswani et al the decoder generates the output sequence x utilizing causal masking in the context of hard expectation maximization em the encoder does not yield a sequence of discrete vectors instead it produces a sequence of softmax probability distributions following the procedure established for the categorical vae these distributions are projected through an embedding layer and subsequently fed into the transformer encoder decoder evaluation of latent representations following the methodology of gururangan et al the efficacy of the learned latent representations is assessed by employing them as features within a text classification framework the primary objective is to determine whether latent representations derived from unlabeled text can enhance the performance of classifiers trained on limited labeled data the comparative evaluation of discrete latent variable models proceeds in two stages pretraining an encoder decoder model is pretrained on in domain unlabeled text by optimizing the evidence lower bound elbo training is terminated early based on validation perplexity downstream fine tuning the encoder is frozen to extract discrete latent representations for the classification task a minimal set of task specific parameters is then trained atop these representations using varying quantities of labeled data consistent with the introduction this evaluation considers two strategies re embedding the latents from scratch versus utilizing the embeddings learned during the pretraining phase tasks and datasets the classification experiments utilize the ag news dbpedia and yelp review full datasets zhang et al these datasets correspond to the prediction of news categories wikipedia ontology labels and yelp star ratings respectively detailed dataset statistics are provided in table for all datasets examples are randomly sampled to constitute the development set to evaluate the efficiency of the latent representations in low resource scenarios the classifier is trained using varying numbers of labeled instances and the full training set size varies by dataset model performance is measured using accuracy

when queried regarding their methodological preference twelve of the fifteen participants indicated a preference for the two computer c model over the one computer c model subsequent inquiries regarding challenges revealed distinct issues associated with each approach in the c setting participants reported that the driver role was frequently monopolized by one partner a phenomenon corroborated by observational data students expressed a strong preference for the driver role and dissatisfaction with the latency involved in waiting for their turn furthermore communication efficacy was perceived as diminished due to interpersonal conflict and a lack of active listening participants also noted reduced opportunities for hands on engagement and cited physical constraints as the presence of individual computers per student resulted in insufficient seating space additionally the navigator role was often characterized by a lack of attentiveness to the driver s actions conversely challenges in the c setting were primarily technical participants frequently encountered latency issues within the netsblox environment which hindered the real time synchronization of programs the inability to observe a partner s actions instantaneously often resulted in parallel rather than coordinated work regarding positive aspects participants in the c model noted enhanced learning opportunities derived from observing a partner s workflow and identifying errors in real time in contrast the c model was praised for enabling the decomposition of tasks into subtasks facilitating parallel work and affording greater independence and control while the c setup was noted to be less physically cramped due to the single computer arrangement the c model was perceived to offer superior hands on experience some participants also reported higher learning gains in the c environment following observations of both methodologies across three schools it is evident that while students identified specific benefits and challenges inherent to each approach overall satisfaction was higher with the c model based on these findings several key takeaways are proposed for practitioners considering the implementation of pair programming independence and control students reported increased efficiency in the c model attributing this to the greater autonomy and control the saturated network may be seen intuitively as a “stable” version of its original rnn in which the in originally referred to as the asymptotic rnn ternal activations act discretely a growing body of work—including this paper—finds that the saturated theory predicts differences in practical learnable capacity for various rnn architectures we compare the expressive power of rational and nonrational rnns distinguishing between state expressiveness what kind and amount of information the rnn states can capture and language expressiveness what languages can be recognized when the state is passed to a classifier to do this we build on the theory of saturated rnns state expressiveness we introduce a unified hierarchy figure of the functions expressible by the states of rational and nonrational rnn encoders the hierarchy is defined by two formal properties space complexity which is a measure of network memory and rational recurrence whether the internal structure of the rnn can be described by wfas the hierarchy reveals concrete differences between lstms and qrnns and further separates both from a class containing convolutional neural networks elman rnns and gated recurrent units we provide the first formal proof that lstms can encode functions that rational recurrences cannot on the other hand we show that the saturated elman rnn and gru are rational recurrences with constant space complexity whereas the qrnn has unbounded space complexity we also show that an unrestricted wfa has rich expressive power beyond any saturated rnn we consider—including the lstm this difference potentially opens the door to more expressive rnns incorporating the computational efficiency of rational recurrences language expressiveness when applied to classification tasks like language recognition rnns are typically combined with a “decoder” additional layer s that map their hidden states to a prediction thus despite differences in state expressiveness rational rnns might be able to achieve comparable empirical performance to nonrational rnns on nlp tasks in this work we consider the setup in which the decoders only view the final hidden state of the rnn we demonstrate that space complexity measures the number of different configurations an rnn can reach as a function of input length
